The concrete tulips were an engineering answer to the fact that the Hudson bottom couldn't take a normal pier; Heatherwick designed a park that floats on a forest of stems. The amphitheater below hosts free music, theater, and dance all summer, and the view from here is one of the better sunset views in Manhattan.

stops.title: Little Island, Southwest Overlook
stops.caption: Stand at the Southwest Overlook, the highest point on the island, the amphitheater below you and the Hudson to your south.
stops.transcriptText:
Titanic survivors landed on a pier that used to be here.

The Southwest Overlook is the highest point on Little Island, the 2.4-acre park that floats above the Hudson River at 13th Street, in the Meatpacking District. From here, you can see most of the park in one glance. Below you, the amphitheater curves down toward the water. To your south, the Hudson stretches toward the Statue of Liberty, visible in the distance. To your east, the Manhattan skyline — Hudson Yards just to the north, the Empire State Building beyond it. And directly beneath your feet, the engineering trick that holds the whole park up: more than a hundred concrete piles rising out of the river, each one carved into a shallow bowl. Concrete tulips. They support everything.

The park stands on the site of what was once Pier 54 — one of the great transatlantic piers of the early twentieth century. The Lusitania departed from here on its final voyage in 1915. Three years earlier, in 1912, the Cunard ship Carpathia had docked here carrying the survivors of the Titanic — the seven hundred and ten passengers and crew rescued from the lifeboats. The pier was destroyed by Hurricane Sandy in 2012, by which point it was already long abandoned.

What was built in its place is one of the most expensive privately funded public parks in American history. Little Island opened in 2021. It was designed by Heatherwick Studio — the same firm responsible for the Vessel in Hudson Yards. It cost about two hundred and sixty million dollars, almost entirely paid for by Barry Diller and Diane von Furstenberg.

The concrete tulips were an engineering response to a real constraint. The Hudson River bottom couldn't take a normal pier. So Heatherwick designed something that floats, more or less, on a forest of stems. The tulip tops act as planters, water catchments, and structural supports all at once.

[beat]

The amphitheater below you hosts free performances all summer — music, theater, dance. The view from where you're standing is one of the better sunset views in Manhattan.

For now, stay a while. The Hudson is in front of you. The city is behind you. Most visitors walk straight through the island in fifteen minutes. The view rewards a longer look.

tours.title: Manhattan Bridge, from DUMBO
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes on the workhorse bridge of New York — and the corner in DUMBO where everyone takes the picture.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Washington and Water Streets in DUMBO, look east. The view in front of you — the Manhattan Bridge framing the Empire State Building, with the cobblestones of Washington Street running toward it — has shown up in more films, fashion shoots, and Instagram photos than almost any other view in New York. Every day a few hundred people stop on this spot to take the picture.

The bridge itself is the Manhattan Bridge — a suspension bridge with a main span of just under fifteen hundred feet, designed by the engineer Leon Moisseiff and opened in 1909, twenty-six years after the Brooklyn Bridge just to the south. Where the Brooklyn Bridge is the romantic one, the older sibling on every postcard, the Manhattan Bridge is the workhorse: four subway lines run across its lower decks, seven lanes of cars cross its middle, and a pedestrian walkway and a separate bike lane sit above. It is what actually keeps Lower Manhattan connected to Brooklyn day in and day out.

On the Manhattan side, the bridge's grand entrance — a Beaux-Arts colonnade and triumphal arch on the Bowery — was designed by Carrère and Hastings, the firm behind the New York Public Library. It is some of the most impressive civic architecture in the city, and almost nobody notices. But what most people see is what you're looking at right now: the bridge framed in a perfect Brooklyn view, with Manhattan visible through its arches. Take the photo. Then walk under the bridge and out to the East River — the view from the waterfront is even better.

stops.title: Manhattan Bridge, from DUMBO
stops.caption: Stand at Washington and Water Streets in DUMBO, looking east at the bridge framing the Empire State Building.
stops.transcriptText:
You're standing on the most photographed corner in Brooklyn.

The corner you're on is Washington Street and Water Street, in DUMBO. Look east. The view in front of you — the Manhattan Bridge framing the Empire State Building, with the cobblestones of Washington Street leading toward it — has appeared in more films, fashion shoots, and Instagram photos than almost any other view in New York. Every day, several hundred people stop on this exact spot to take the picture.

The bridge itself is the Manhattan Bridge. It opened in 1909 — twenty-six years after the Brooklyn Bridge, just to the south. It's a suspension bridge, with a main span of just under fifteen hundred feet, designed by the engineer Leon Moisseiff. Like the Brooklyn Bridge, it connects Lower Manhattan to Brooklyn — but it's larger, faster, and carries far more traffic. Four subway lines run across its lower decks. Seven lanes of cars cross its middle. Above all of that, a pedestrian walkway and a separate bike lane.

The Manhattan Bridge is one of the great forgotten landmarks of New York. It opened to less fanfare than its older neighbor, and it has never quite escaped the Brooklyn Bridge's shadow. The Brooklyn Bridge is the romantic one — older, more storied, the symbol on every postcard. The Manhattan Bridge is the workhorse. It carries the trains. It carries the trucks. It is what actually keeps Lower Manhattan connected to Brooklyn day in and day out.

On the Manhattan side, the bridge's grand entrance was designed by the firm Carrère and Hastings — the architects of the New York Public Library. The colonnade and triumphal arch at the foot of the bridge, on the Bowery, are some of the most impressive Beaux-Arts civic architecture in the city. Almost nobody notices them.

[beat]

But what most people see is what you're looking at right now — the bridge framed in a perfect Brooklyn view, with Manhattan visible through its arches. The composition is so good it's hard to take a bad photo here. Go ahead, take one.

When you're done, walk under the bridge and out to the East River. The view from the waterfront is even better.

tours.title: Museum of the City of New York
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes on the Colonial Revival museum at 103rd Street — the only major NYC museum whose single subject is the city itself.
tours.longDescription:
On Fifth Avenue at 103rd Street — the northern end of Museum Mile, just across from Central Park's Conservatory Garden — stands a red-brick, white-marble Colonial Revival facade with a symmetrical entrance and tall arched windows. This is the Museum of the City of New York, founded in 1923 to do something simple but unusual: document the city itself. Not the art of New York, not the natural history of New York, but the actual city — its streets, its people, its political history, its architecture, its photographs, its everyday objects. It's the only major museum in New York whose single subject is the city itself.

The current building opened in 1932, designed by Joseph H. Freedlander in a Colonial-American style with no real historical accuracy in this neighborhood — a deliberate signal of "old New York" to anyone walking up. Behind the facade, the collection now numbers around one and a half million objects. The strongest section is photography — over a million images, including Berenice Abbott's photographs of 1930s New York and Jacob Riis's photographs of the Lower East Side that helped expose the conditions of nineteenth-century tenement life. There are also costumes, maps, paintings, theatrical ephemera, political artifacts — anything that documents what New York has been.

Among the big New York museums, this one gets less attention. It's smaller; it doesn't have the marquee paintings. But if you've ever wondered what New York looked like a hundred years ago, what the air felt like in the tenements, who lived where, what they wore — this is the museum that knows. Go in if you have time. Or keep walking up Fifth Avenue: the Conservatory Garden, just across the street, is one of the quietest spots in Central Park.

stops.title: Museum of the City of New York
stops.caption: Stand on Fifth Avenue at 103rd Street, looking at the red-brick Colonial Revival entrance.
stops.transcriptText:
Most museums in this city are about somewhere else. This one is about here.

Red brick and white marble, a Colonial Revival facade — the Museum of the City of New York stands on Fifth Avenue at 103rd Street, at the northern end of what's called Museum Mile. The entrance is symmetrical, with tall arched windows. Across the street, behind you, is the entrance to Central Park's Conservatory Garden.

The museum was founded in 1923 to do something simple but unusual: document the city itself. Not the art of New York, not the natural history of New York, but the actual city — its streets, its people, its political history, its architecture, its photographs, its everyday objects. It is the only major museum in New York that takes the city as its single subject.

The current building opened in 1932, designed by the architect Joseph H. Freedlander to look colonial-American — not a style with much historical accuracy in this neighborhood, but a deliberate choice. The museum's founders wanted a building that signaled "old New York" — that telegraphed history at first glance, before you even walked in.

The collection now numbers around one and a half million objects. The strongest section is photography — over a million images, including the Berenice Abbott photographs of 1930s New York and the Jacob Riis photographs of the Lower East Side that helped expose the conditions of nineteenth-century tenement life. There are also costumes, maps, paintings, theatrical ephemera, political artifacts. Anything that documents what New York has been.

[beat]

Among the big New York museums — the Met, MoMA, the Whitney — this one gets less attention. It's smaller. It doesn't have the marquee paintings. But if you've ever wondered what New York looked like a hundred years ago, what the air felt like in the tenements, who lived where, what they wore, this is the museum that knows.

Go in if you have time. Or just keep walking up Fifth Avenue — Conservatory Garden, just across the street, is one of the quietest spots in Central Park.

tours.title: New York Public Library, Fifth Avenue
tours.shortDescription: Just under two minutes at Patience and Fortitude — the 1911 Beaux-Arts library where anyone can walk in and read a Gutenberg.
tours.longDescription:
At Fifth Avenue and 42nd Street, two marble lions sit at the foot of the steps: Patience and Fortitude. They were carved in 1911 by Edward Clark Potter and originally named Leo Astor and Leo Lenox, after the two wealthy donors whose private libraries became the foundation of the collection. In the depths of the Depression, Mayor Fiorello La Guardia renamed them — the virtues, he said, that New Yorkers would need to survive the decade. The names stuck.

The building they guard is the main branch of the New York Public Library, a solid white-marble Beaux-Arts landmark with six Corinthian columns and a carved frieze above the steps, essentially unchanged since 1911. Bryant Park sits directly behind it. The site was previously occupied by the Croton Reservoir — a massive granite structure that had supplied New York's drinking water for half a century — and the library, the result of an 1895 agreement among three private libraries to merge their holdings into a single free public reference library, took fourteen years to build.

Inside is one of the largest research libraries in the world — over fifty-five million items, including a Gutenberg Bible, the original copy of Jefferson's draft of the Declaration of Independence, and the manuscript of George Washington's farewell address. The Rose Reading Room — three hundred feet long, its ceiling painted with murals of clouds — is one of the great rooms in any American building. And it's all free. Anyone can walk in. That was the founders' idea, and it still is.

stops.title: New York Public Library, Fifth Avenue
stops.caption: Stand at the foot of the steps on Fifth Avenue at 42nd Street, between Patience and Fortitude.
stops.transcriptText:
Look at the lions guarding the steps. Their names are Patience and Fortitude.

The building they guard is the main branch of the New York Public Library, on Fifth Avenue at 42nd Street. Above the steps, six Corinthian columns support a carved frieze. The building is solid white marble, designed in the Beaux-Arts style, and has stood here essentially unchanged since 1911. Bryant Park is directly behind it.

The lions were sculpted in 1911 by Edward Clark Potter. They were originally called Leo Astor and Leo Lenox, after the two wealthy donors whose private libraries became the foundation of the collection. In the 1930s, during the depths of the Depression, Mayor Fiorello La Guardia renamed them Patience and Fortitude — the virtues, he said, that New Yorkers would need to survive that decade. The names stuck.

The library itself was the result of an unusual idea: in 1895, three private libraries agreed to merge their holdings and open a single, free, public reference library in the heart of Manhattan. The site they chose, this corner, was previously occupied by the Croton Reservoir — a massive granite structure that had supplied New York's drinking water for half a century. They tore it down and built this in its place. It took fourteen years.

[beat]

Inside is one of the largest research libraries in the world — over fifty-five million items, including a Gutenberg Bible, the original copy of Jefferson's draft of the Declaration of Independence, and the manuscript of George Washington's farewell address. The Rose Reading Room — three hundred feet long, ceiling painted with murals of clouds — is one of the great rooms in any American building.

It's all free. Anyone can walk in. That was the founders' idea, and it still is.

tours.title: The Oculus
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes on Calatrava's white-ribbed Oculus at the World Trade Center — a $4 billion train station shaped like a dove.
tours.longDescription:
On the World Trade Center plaza stands a white skeleton of steel ribs that looks, depending on the angle, like a dove, a whale, or a ribcage. The ribs curve upward to a three-hundred-and-fifty-foot skylight running the length of the building. This is the Oculus — at heart, a train station: inside is the Westfield mall, and beneath that, the PATH transit station that connects Manhattan to New Jersey. The PATH station beneath the original Twin Towers was destroyed on September 11, and the brief for its replacement was ambitious — not just to rebuild infrastructure, but to create a symbol, a phoenix rising on the site. The Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava — known for sculptural transportation hubs that lean into the boundary between engineering and art — got the commission.

Calatrava's original concept was a dove of peace being released from a child's hands. The white ribs flaring out around you are its wings. The execution didn't go smoothly. Construction stretched on for years, opening in 2016 — seven years behind schedule — and went four billion dollars over its initial estimate. Critics savaged it; the New York Times called it a monument to public extravagance. The wings, which were designed to mechanically open and let light pour in on September 11 each year, did so for only a few years before the mechanism was largely deactivated.

And yet — when you walk inside and the light pours down through the skylight onto the white marble floor, it's hard not to be moved. The building works as a sculpture, even if it failed as a transit project. Fifty million people pass through it every year and almost all of them stop to look up. Whatever Calatrava's intentions, he built something people respond to. The interior is the experience — stand under the ribs and look up.

stops.title: The Oculus
stops.caption: Stand on the World Trade Center plaza facing the white ribs, then step inside and look up.
stops.transcriptText:
The structure in front of you cost four billion dollars. It is, at heart, a train station.

World Trade Center plaza. In front of you is the Oculus — a white skeleton of steel ribs that looks like a dove, a whale, or a ribcage, depending on your angle. The ribs curve upward to a 350-foot-long skylight that runs the length of the building. Inside is the Westfield World Trade Center mall, and beneath that, the PATH transit station that connects Manhattan to New Jersey.

The building was designed by the Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava, who's known for sculptural transportation hubs — bridges, train stations, airports — that lean into the boundary between engineering and art. The Oculus was commissioned after September 11 to replace the PATH station that had been destroyed beneath the original Twin Towers. The brief was ambitious: not just to rebuild infrastructure, but to create a symbol — a phoenix, of sorts, rising on the site.

Calatrava's original concept was a dove of peace being released from a child's hands. The white ribs that flare out around you are its wings.

The execution didn't go smoothly. The construction stretched on for years, opening in 2016 — seven years behind schedule — and went four billion dollars over its initial estimate. Critics savaged it. The New York Times called it a monument to public extravagance. The wings, which were designed to mechanically open to let in light on September 11 each year, did so for only a few years before the mechanism was largely deactivated.

[beat]

And yet — when you walk inside, when the light pours down through the skylight onto the white marble floor, it's hard not to be moved. The building works as a sculpture, even if it failed as a transit project. Fifty million people pass through it every year, and almost all of them stop to look up. Whatever Calatrava's intentions, he built something people respond to.

If you have a minute, walk inside. The interior is the experience — stand under the ribs and look up at the skylight.

tours.title: St. Patrick's Cathedral
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes at the Gothic cathedral that began on the city's northern frontier — built by Irish-American pennies into a claim to belong.
tours.longDescription:
On Fifth Avenue between 50th and 51st Streets, two white-marble spires rise three hundred and thirty feet above the sidewalk. This is St. Patrick's Cathedral — the seat of the Archdiocese of New York and the largest Gothic Revival Catholic cathedral in the United States. It was designed by James Renwick Jr., the architect of the Smithsonian Castle in Washington and Grace Church downtown, in a Gothic style modeled on the great cathedrals of France and Germany. Construction began in 1858 and continued through the Civil War, finally opening in 1879.

What made it remarkable wasn't just the architecture. When ground was broken, this stretch of Fifth Avenue was the northern frontier of the city — open fields, a few farmhouses. Locating the cathedral here was deliberate: a public claim that Irish-American Catholics, then the city's largest immigrant group and one of its most despised, belonged at the center of New York's future. Archbishop John Hughes raised the construction funds largely from the city's Catholic poor — pennies and nickels from servants, laborers, and washerwomen, contributed over two decades. The result was a building whose presence was, and is, a political statement as much as a religious one.

St. Patrick's remains a working cathedral. Mass is celebrated multiple times a day. State funerals have been held here for Babe Ruth, Robert F. Kennedy, and Joe DiMaggio. The bronze doors weigh more than nine thousand pounds each. The Rose Window above the main entrance is twenty-six feet across. And the side chapels, including the Lady Chapel behind the main altar, are open to anyone who walks in — a quiet pocket of stone and stained glass that, on a weekday morning, feels like a different city entirely.

stops.title: St. Patrick's Cathedral
stops.caption: Stand on Fifth Avenue between 50th and 51st Streets, looking up at the spires.
stops.transcriptText:
When this cathedral was built, it sat on the northern frontier of the city. Now it sits in the middle of midtown Manhattan.

Fifth Avenue between 50th and 51st Streets. In front of you is St. Patrick's Cathedral — the seat of the Archdiocese of New York and the largest Gothic Revival Catholic cathedral in the United States. The two white-marble spires rise 330 feet above the sidewalk. The building is the work of James Renwick Jr., the architect who also designed the Smithsonian Castle in Washington and Grace Church downtown in the Village. Construction began in 1858 and continued through the Civil War, finally opening in 1879.

The design is Gothic Revival, modeled on the great cathedrals of France and Germany — pointed arches, flying buttresses, a Rose Window above the main entrance that's twenty-six feet across. What made it remarkable wasn't just the architecture, though. When ground was broken, this stretch of Fifth Avenue was open fields and a few farmhouses. Locating the cathedral here was deliberate: a public claim that Irish-American Catholics, then the city's largest immigrant group and one of its most despised, belonged at the center of New York's future.

[beat]

Archbishop John Hughes raised the construction funds largely from the city's Catholic poor — pennies and nickels from servants, laborers, and washerwomen, contributed over two decades. The result was a building whose presence was, and is, a political statement as much as a religious one.

St. Patrick's remains a working cathedral. Mass is celebrated multiple times a day. State funerals have been held here for Babe Ruth, Robert F. Kennedy, and Joe DiMaggio. The bronze doors weigh more than nine thousand pounds each.

If you have a few minutes, the side chapels are open to anyone who walks in — including the Lady Chapel behind the main altar. On a weekday morning, it's a quiet pocket of stone and stained glass that feels like a different city entirely.

tours.title: Vessel, Hudson Yards
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes at Heatherwick's $200 million copper honeycomb — a sculpture, climbing frame, and selfie spot all at once.
tours.longDescription:
In the Public Square at the heart of Hudson Yards stands a 150-foot copper-colored honeycomb of interlocking staircases — 2,500 steps and 80 landings spiraling around an open central void. This is the Vessel, designed by the British architect-designer Thomas Heatherwick and opened in 2019 as the centerpiece of Hudson Yards, the largest private real estate development in American history. The structure weighs six hundred tons and cost roughly two hundred million dollars to build.

Heatherwick's idea was a kind of urban climbing frame — a public artwork designed to be walked, not just looked at. Visitors would climb the staircases, take in the Hudson from different heights, and descend by different paths. He called it "public architecture," a category he essentially invented. Critics have struggled to categorize it more precisely: sculpture? climbing frame? selfie backdrop? For Heatherwick's defenders, that ambiguity is the point — a piece of public art that resists being one thing. For his critics, it's a two-hundred-million-dollar selfie spot dressed up as architecture.

Around the Vessel is Hudson Yards itself — a twenty-eight-acre, twenty-five-billion-dollar development built over the West Side Rail Yards, on what was once one of the last large undeveloped tracts in Manhattan. The skyscrapers ringing the plaza are the work of some of the most prominent architects of this generation: Bjarke Ingels, Kohn Pedersen Fox, and Diller Scofidio + Renfro, whose white cultural venue The Shed sits just to the south on what look like enormous wheels. Walk the plaza, look up at the Vessel from different angles, and — if it's open — climb the lower levels for a view of the river.

stops.title: Vessel, Hudson Yards Plaza
stops.caption: Stand in the Public Square at Hudson Yards facing the copper-colored honeycomb.
stops.transcriptText:
The structure in front of you has two thousand five hundred steps, eighty landings, and looks like a giant copper-colored honeycomb.

The Vessel — Thomas Heatherwick's 150-foot sculpture — stands in the Public Square at the heart of Hudson Yards. The exterior is a network of interlocking staircases that rise and twist around an open central void. The whole thing weighs six hundred tons. It opened in 2019 as the centerpiece of Hudson Yards, the largest private real estate development in American history.

The idea was a kind of urban climbing frame — a public artwork designed to be walked, not just looked at. Visitors would climb the staircases, look out at the Hudson from different heights and angles, and come down by different paths. Heatherwick designed it as the focal point that would make Hudson Yards feel like a neighborhood, not a corporate campus.

What you're looking at cost about two hundred million dollars to build. Heatherwick called it a piece of "public architecture" — a category he essentially made up. Critics have struggled to categorize it more precisely. Sculpture? Climbing frame? Selfie backdrop? It is all of those, and none of them quite. For Heatherwick's defenders, that ambiguity is the point — a piece of public art that resists being one thing. For his critics, it's a two-hundred-million-dollar selfie spot dressed up as architecture.

[beat]

Around the Vessel is Hudson Yards itself — a twenty-eight-acre development built over the West Side Rail Yards, on what was once one of the last large undeveloped tracts in Manhattan. About twenty-five billion dollars went into it. The skyscrapers around you are the work of some of the most prominent architects of this generation — Bjarke Ingels, Kohn Pedersen Fox, Diller Scofidio + Renfro, whose white cultural venue, the Shed, sits just south of here on what look like enormous wheels.

For now, walk the plaza. Look up at the Vessel from different angles. If it's open, climb the lower levels for a view of the river and the city.

tours.title: Wall Street
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes at the corner where George Washington was inaugurated and, three years later, the stock market was born across the street.
tours.longDescription:
At the intersection of Wall, Broad, and Nassau Streets, two founding moments of American life face each other across a single block. To the north is Federal Hall, a Greek Revival temple with massive columns and a bronze statue of George Washington on its steps — standing roughly where, on April 30, 1789, Washington placed his hand on a Bible and was sworn in as the first president of the United States. The original Federal Hall, torn down in 1812, was also where the first Congress met, where the Bill of Rights was passed, and where the Supreme Court first convened. For about a year, this corner was the capital of the United States.

To the south, across the intersection, is the New York Stock Exchange — six Corinthian columns, a carved pediment, and, on most days, an enormous American flag draped across the facade. In 1792, twenty-four brokers signed an agreement under a buttonwood tree just down the street to trade with each other on fixed terms. That agreement became the NYSE. The street itself takes its name from a wooden wall the Dutch built here in 1653 to defend against an expected British attack — the British took the city anyway, removed the wall, and the road kept the name.

For most of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, the civic founding and the financial founding sat across the street from each other, quietly. Then, slowly, the financial side took over. The capital moved to Washington. The traders grew rich. By the twentieth century, "Wall Street" had stopped meaning a place and started meaning an idea — a synonym for American capitalism, for power, sometimes for excess. But the corner is still both things at once. The country was made on these stones. The market was made across the way.

stops.title: Wall Street, the Corner of Wall, Broad, and Nassau
stops.caption: Stand at the intersection of Wall, Broad, and Nassau Streets, between Federal Hall and the New York Stock Exchange.
stops.transcriptText:
The first American president was inaugurated here. And then this became Wall Street.

You should be standing at the intersection of Wall, Broad, and Nassau Streets. Look north. The Greek Revival building with the massive columns and the bronze statue on its steps is Federal Hall. The statue is George Washington. He's standing roughly where, on April 30, 1789, he placed his hand on a Bible and was sworn in as the first president of the United States.

Look south, across the intersection. The building draped in the enormous American flag — the one with the six Corinthian columns and the carved pediment — is the New York Stock Exchange.

The street running east-west between them is called Wall because in 1653, the Dutch built an actual wooden wall here, to defend against an expected British attack. The British took the city anyway, removed the wall, and the road kept the name.

Two American institutions were born on this corner.

The first was the country itself. The original Federal Hall — torn down in 1812 — was where George Washington was inaugurated, where the first Congress met, where the Bill of Rights was passed, where the Supreme Court first convened. For about a year, this was the capital of the United States.

The second was the stock market. In 1792, twenty-four brokers signed an agreement under a buttonwood tree just down the street to trade with each other on fixed terms. That agreement became the New York Stock Exchange.

[beat]

For most of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, these two institutions were neighbors — the civic founding and the financial founding sat across the street from each other, quietly. Then, slowly, the financial side took over. The capital moved to Washington. The traders grew rich. By the twentieth century, "Wall Street" had stopped meaning a place and started meaning an idea — a synonym for American capitalism, for power, sometimes for excess.

But the corner is still both things at once. The country was made on these stones. The market was made across the way. It's worth knowing they happened on the same block.

tours.title: Washington Square Park
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes under the marble arch — a centennial monument built atop a potter's field with twenty thousand graves still beneath it.
tours.longDescription:
At the southern end of Fifth Avenue, a seventy-seven-foot white marble arch frames the entrance to Washington Square Park. It was designed by Stanford White and modeled on the Arch of Titus in Rome. Two statues of George Washington flank its piers — Washington in civilian dress on the east, Washington as commander in chief on the west. The first version of the arch wasn't this one: in 1889, for the centennial of Washington's inauguration on Wall Street, the city built a temporary wooden arch at this spot, also designed by White. It was so popular that within three years donors raised the money for a permanent marble version. The wooden original stood for a few months. The marble one has stood for over a hundred and thirty years.

What you're standing on used to be a cemetery. Before Washington Square was a park, it was the city's potter's field — an unmarked burial ground for the poor, the indigent, and the victims of yellow fever epidemics. Around twenty thousand people are still buried beneath the square. When the field closed in 1825, the city converted the land to a parade ground, then a public park. In the late nineteenth century, the surrounding blocks became some of the most fashionable addresses in New York — Henry James grew up here, Edith Wharton lived nearby. By the twentieth century the neighborhood had turned bohemian: Beat poets, folk singers, Bob Dylan, Joan Baez all spent time in this park.

It's still that mix, in different proportions. Beyond the arch, the open square holds fountains, chess tables, dog runs, street musicians, and the surrounding NYU campus — where students study, where chess hustlers play for cash, where someone is almost always performing music near the fountain. New York used to be a city full of squares like this. Most of them got built over. This one survived.

stops.title: Washington Square Park
stops.caption: Stand at the north entrance to the park, just under the marble arch at the foot of Fifth Avenue.
stops.transcriptText:
The arch in front of you was originally built out of wood.

The marble one has stood for over a hundred and thirty years.

What you're standing on used to be a cemetery. Before Washington Square was a park, it was the city's potter's field — an unmarked burial ground for the poor, the indigent, and the victims of yellow fever epidemics. In the late nineteenth century, the surrounding blocks became some of the most fashionable addresses in New York. Henry James grew up here. Edith Wharton lived nearby. It's still that mix, in different proportions. The park is where NYU students study, where chess hustlers play for cash, where someone is almost always performing music near the fountain. New York used to be a city full of squares like this. Most of them got built over. This one survived.

For now, find a bench. The arch will be there when you're ready.

tours.title: Cooper Hewitt, Smithsonian Design Museum
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes at Andrew Carnegie's "modest" 64-room mansion — now the only U.S. museum dedicated entirely to design.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Fifth Avenue and 91st Street stands a five-story red-brick and limestone mansion behind a wrought-iron fence and a small lawn. Andrew Carnegie commissioned it in 1899; it opened in 1902 as the home of the Scottish-born steel magnate who had just sold Carnegie Steel for four hundred and eighty million dollars and was, briefly, the richest man in the world. The architects — Babb, Cook and Welch — worked in a restrained Georgian Revival style. Carnegie called it "the most modest, plainest, and roomiest home in New York." It has sixty-four rooms.

Restraint was the point. Carnegie was famously uncomfortable with Gilded Age ostentation. Modest, by his standard, meant sixty-four rooms, the first private residence in New York to have a passenger elevator, and a state-of-the-art steel structural frame — but no obvious gold leaf, no marble palace, no homage to a French château. By the standards of Fifth Avenue mansions of the era, the building was almost austere. Carnegie spent the last sixteen years of his life giving away most of his fortune — funding thousands of libraries around the world, building Carnegie Hall, founding Carnegie Mellon University, supporting peace organizations. He died in 1919. His wife continued to live in the house until 1946, after which the Carnegies donated the building to the Smithsonian.

The Cooper Hewitt is the only museum in the country dedicated entirely to design — not art, not craft, but design as a discipline. Furniture, graphic design, typography, product design, architecture. Every visitor is given an interactive pen that lets you "save" any object you see on display, building a personal catalogue you can review online later. What you go inside for is two things: the objects, and the rooms. Walking through the Carnegie mansion gives you a sense of how the richest American of his era actually chose to live — wealthy but not extravagant, deliberate but not flashy. Of all the Gilded Age mansions in New York, this is the one most worth walking through.

tours.title: El Museo del Barrio
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes at the northern end of Museum Mile — the only New York museum dedicated to Latino art, founded in 1969 in a classroom.
tours.longDescription:
At the northernmost edge of Museum Mile, on the corner of Fifth Avenue and 104th Street, stands El Museo del Barrio. The building — the Heckscher, a 1921 brick structure at 1230 Fifth Avenue — was originally a children's hospital, later served as an orphanage, and now houses both the museum and several public school programs. El Museo is the only museum in New York dedicated to the art and culture of Latin America, the Caribbean, and the Latino diaspora.

The museum was founded in 1969 by Raphael Montañez Ortíz — a New York-born artist, educator, and activist of Puerto Rican descent. Montañez Ortíz was teaching in East Harlem at a time when there was almost no representation of Puerto Rican or Latino artists in any major New York museum. Together with parents and community members, he started El Museo del Barrio as an educational program — initially housed in a public school classroom, then in a series of borrowed spaces around East Harlem. The name translates roughly to "the museum of the neighborhood" — barrio being both the Spanish word for neighborhood and the colloquial name for the surrounding part of East Harlem, sometimes called Spanish Harlem. The collection grew through donations from artists, families, and community members — pre-Columbian artifacts from the Caribbean, twentieth-century Puerto Rican folk art, contemporary work by Latino artists across the Americas — and the museum settled in this building in the late 1970s.

What sets El Museo apart from the other museums on Fifth Avenue is its origin. The Met, the Guggenheim, the Frick, the Neue Galerie — all began as the private collections of wealthy donors. El Museo began as a teaching project in a public school classroom. Its founding question was not "what art should we collect?" but "what art is being ignored?" The collection rewards visiting precisely because it covers ground other museums in New York don't: the pre-Columbian objects are unusually strong, the twentieth-century work from Puerto Rico is rarely shown elsewhere, and the contemporary work is some of the best contemporary Latino art being shown in the United States.

stops.title: El Museo del Barrio
stops.caption: Stand on Fifth Avenue at 104th Street, in front of the Heckscher Building.

Until 1969, no museum in New York City was dedicated to Latino art.

tours.title: The Frick Collection
tours.shortDescription: Two and a quarter minutes outside Henry Clay Frick's Fifth Avenue mansion — the Gilded Age home of one of America's most hated industrialists, holding one of its greatest art collections.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Fifth Avenue and 70th Street stands a limestone Beaux-Arts mansion built between 1913 and 1914 for Henry Clay Frick — the Pittsburgh steel baron, Andrew Carnegie's partner at Carnegie Steel, and one of the most hated industrialists of the late nineteenth century. The architects were Carrère and Hastings, the same firm that designed the New York Public Library. Frick lived here for the last five years of his life. After his death, his will turned the building into a public museum.

The paradox of Frick is that while he was crushing labor strikes — most famously the Homestead Strike of 1892, where he hired three hundred armed Pinkerton agents to break a Pittsburgh steel plant strike, leaving ten men dead — he was simultaneously assembling one of the most refined art collections in the world. Vermeer, Rembrandt, Bellini, Whistler, Velázquez, Holbein. The collection inside includes some of the most important paintings in any American museum, displayed in the rooms where Frick chose to hang them — the dining room, the library, the West Gallery — exactly as he arranged them.

The Frick reopened in 2025 after a multi-year renovation by the architect Annabelle Selldorf — the first major expansion in its history, adding new galleries above the mansion while preserving the period rooms below. What you go inside for is the experience of seeing a private collection in a private home. The Met is institutional; the Frick is intimate. You're not in a museum — you're in someone's living room, looking at the paintings they chose to live with. They happen to be some of the best paintings in the country.

stops.title: The Frick Collection
stops.caption: Stand on Fifth Avenue at 70th Street, in front of the limestone mansion behind the iron fence.
stops.transcriptText:
The Gilded Age mansion in front of you holds one of the great art collections in America. The man who built it was one of the most hated industrialists of his era.

Henry Clay Frick — a Pittsburgh steel baron, one of the richest Americans of the late nineteenth century — commissioned the mansion at One East 70th Street in 1913. The architects were Carrère and Hastings, the same firm that designed the New York Public Library. The building was completed in 1914 in the late Beaux-Arts style: limestone facade, classical proportions, a private garden behind. Frick lived here for the last five years of his life. After his death, his will turned the building into a public museum.

Frick had earned his fortune in coke and steel, partnered with Andrew Carnegie at Carnegie Steel, where Frick handled the labor side of the business. The signature moment of his career was the Homestead Strike of 1892. When workers at a Pittsburgh steel plant struck for better conditions, Frick hired three hundred armed Pinkerton agents to break it. The Pinkertons arrived by barge and were met by armed workers. A battle followed. Ten men died. The strike was broken. Frick became one of the most hated men in America.

While he was crushing strikes, he was assembling one of the most refined art collections in the world. Vermeer, Rembrandt, Bellini, Whistler, Velázquez, Holbein. The collection inside includes some of the most important paintings in any American museum, displayed in the rooms where Frick chose to hang them — the dining room, the library, the West Gallery — exactly as he arranged them.

[beat]

The Frick reopened in 2025 after a multi-year renovation by the architect Annabelle Selldorf — the first major expansion in its history. The expansion added new galleries above the mansion while preserving the period rooms below.

What you go inside for is the experience of seeing a private collection in a private home. The Met is institutional; the Frick is intimate. You're not in a museum — you're in someone's living room, looking at the paintings they chose to live with. They happen to be some of the best paintings in the country.

tours.title: Neue Galerie
tours.shortDescription: Two and a quarter minutes at the Fifth Avenue mansion holding Klimt's Woman in Gold — the painting at the center of one of the great Holocaust-restitution stories of the twentieth century.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Fifth Avenue and 86th Street stands the Neue Galerie — a private museum dedicated to German and Austrian art from the early twentieth century. The building itself was completed in 1914 by Carrère and Hastings, the same firm that designed the New York Public Library a few blocks south. It was originally the residence of William Starr Miller, an heir to a mining fortune. The Vanderbilts later owned it. In 2001, the cosmetics heir Ronald Lauder turned it into a museum.

The collection's centerpiece is Gustav Klimt's Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer the First — a 1907 painting now widely known as the Woman in Gold. The portrait was commissioned by the Viennese banker Ferdinand Bloch-Bauer of his wife Adele. When the Nazis annexed Austria in 1938, the Bloch-Bauer family fled; their art collection, including the Klimt, was seized. After the war, the painting was held by the Austrian state, which displayed it at the Belvedere museum in Vienna and claimed it as a national treasure. In 1998, Adele's niece Maria Altmann, then in her eighties, began a legal effort to recover the family's paintings. The case went to the United States Supreme Court. In 2006, an arbitration panel in Vienna ruled in her favor. The painting was returned to the family — and almost immediately sold to Ronald Lauder for one hundred and thirty-five million dollars, at the time the highest price ever paid for a painting. It has hung in this building ever since.

The rest of the collection is smaller but exquisite. Egon Schiele, Oskar Kokoschka, Klimt's other portraits, the German Expressionists. The museum is also famous for Café Sabarsky on the ground floor, a Viennese coffeehouse that serves the most authentic Sachertorte you can find outside Vienna. The building you're looking at houses one painting whose price tag still defines how the world thinks about art valuation — and one of the great Holocaust-restitution stories of the twentieth century. They are the same painting.

stops.title: Neue Galerie
stops.caption: Stand on Fifth Avenue at 86th Street, in front of the mansion on the corner.

The Klimt inside this museum was stolen by the Nazis.

tours.title: Museum of Arts and Design
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes at Columbus Circle — the contemporary box that replaced the controversial "Lollipop Building" in 2008, and the design museum it now houses.
tours.longDescription:
Columbus Circle is the open traffic circle at the southwest corner of Central Park, with a statue of Christopher Columbus rising on a column at its center. The ten-story tower with the white-paneled facade at the southeast corner of the circle is the Museum of Arts and Design — usually called MAD. The building you see today is not the original.

The original was designed in 1964 by Edward Durell Stone for the Huntington Hartford Gallery of Modern Art. Stone's design was strange and divisive: a white marble facade pierced by rows of round porthole-like openings, with a colonnaded arcade at the base. Critics called it the "Lollipop Building" — some affectionately, most not. The architecture writer Ada Louise Huxtable called it a "die-cut Venetian palazzo on lollipops." It became an architectural in-joke — depending on who you asked, either the worst or the most interesting building of postwar New York. In 2008, the Museum of Arts and Design bought the building and gutted it. The architect Brad Cloepfil redesigned the facade entirely, removing the porthole openings, the colonnade, and most of what had made the original distinctive. Preservationists protested. A long lawsuit failed. The original building, for better or worse, is gone.

What MAD does inside is mostly craft and design — ceramics, textiles, jewelry, glass, furniture, contemporary artisan work. The museum is small enough to see in an hour and large enough to feel like a real experience. The restaurant on the top floor, Robert, has one of the best Central Park views in any museum in the city. The question the building raises, standing here, is how much of New York's architecture worth preserving has been quietly destroyed in the last fifty years. The Lollipop Building was not, by any official measure, a masterpiece. But it was distinctive. And it was lost without much of a fight.

stops.title: Museum of Arts and Design
stops.caption: Stand on Columbus Circle, facing the white-paneled tower at the southeast corner.
stops.transcriptText:
Until 2008, this building looked completely different.

Columbus Circle is in front of you, with the statue of Christopher Columbus rising on a column at the center of the traffic circle. The ten-story tower of white-paneled facade at the southeast corner is the Museum of Arts and Design — usually called MAD.

The original building was designed in 1964 by Edward Durell Stone, for the Huntington Hartford Gallery of Modern Art. Stone's design was strange and divisive: a white marble facade pierced by rows of round porthole-like openings, with a colonnaded arcade at the base. Critics called it the "Lollipop Building" — some affectionately, most not. The architecture writer Ada Louise Huxtable called it a "die-cut Venetian palazzo on lollipops." It became an architectural in-joke — depending on who you asked, either the worst or the most interesting building of postwar New York.

In 2008, the Museum of Arts and Design bought the building and gutted it. The architect Brad Cloepfil redesigned the facade entirely, removing the porthole openings, the colonnade, and most of what had made the original building distinctive. The result is what you're looking at: a contemporary box, more conventional, less controversial. Preservationists protested. A long lawsuit failed. The original building, for better or worse, is gone.

[beat]

What MAD does inside is mostly craft and design — ceramics, textiles, jewelry, glass, furniture, contemporary artisan work. The museum is small enough to see in an hour and large enough to feel like a real experience. The restaurant on the top floor, Robert, has one of the best Central Park views in any museum in the city.

What's worth knowing, standing here, is the question the building raises: how much of New York's architecture worth preserving has been quietly destroyed in the last fifty years? The Lollipop Building was not, by any official measure, a masterpiece. But it was distinctive. And it was lost without much of a fight.

tours.title: New Museum
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes on the Bowery — one museum now made of two buildings by two living Pritzker Prize-winning architects, standing connected with neither one deferring to the other.
tours.longDescription:
Since March 2026, the New Museum on the Bowery has been two buildings standing side by side. On the left, at 235 Bowery, the aluminum-mesh tower of six rectangular boxes stacked on top of each other, each slightly offset from the box below — like someone abandoned a Jenga puzzle. On the right, at 231 Bowery, a wedge of cool-toned laminated glass with a faceted, beveled face that catches and reflects light. The two buildings meet at the upper floors and are physically connected on three levels inside.

The tower on the left was designed by the Japanese firm SANAA — the partnership of Kazuyo Sejima and Ryue Nishizawa, who won the Pritzker Prize, architecture's highest honor, in 2010, in part on the strength of this building. It opened in 2007. The stacked, shifted look was a response to the Bowery itself — for most of the twentieth century, a working-class commercial corridor of restaurant-supply stores, lighting wholesalers, pawn shops. SANAA's building was designed to feel less like a monument than like a stack of boxes temporarily placed on the lot. The wedge on the right was designed by OMA — the Dutch firm founded by Rem Koolhaas, working here with the partner Shohei Shigematsu. It opened in March of 2026 and marks OMA's first cultural building in New York City. Where SANAA's tower is reticent, OMA's wedge is overt — a public plaza at the sidewalk, an atrium visible from the street, and a glass-and-mesh facade that reads as metallic by day and lit-up at night.

New York now has one museum made of two buildings by two different living Pritzker Prize-winning architects, standing connected, with neither one deferring to the other. The museum's director called it "not a wing, not an extension, not an annex — a second building." The museum itself is the only one in New York exclusively dedicated to contemporary art by emerging or under-recognized artists. There is no permanent collection. The exhibitions rotate constantly. The contents of both buildings are completely different from one year to the next.

stops.title: New Museum
stops.caption: Stand on the Bowery just south of Prince Street, facing the two-building site.
stops.transcriptText:
The taller building on the left looks unfinished. It isn't. The shiny one on the right was finished two months ago.

You're standing on the Bowery, looking at the New Museum — which, since March of this year, is two buildings standing side by side. On the left, at 235 Bowery, the aluminum-mesh tower of six rectangular boxes stacked on top of each other, each slightly offset from the box below — like someone abandoned a Jenga puzzle. On the right, at 231 Bowery, a wedge of cool-toned laminated glass with a faceted, beveled face that catches and reflects light. The two buildings meet at the upper floors and are physically connected on three levels inside.

The tower on the left was designed by the Japanese firm SANAA — the partnership of Kazuyo Sejima and Ryue Nishizawa, who won the Pritzker Prize, architecture's highest honor, in 2010, in part on the strength of this building. It opened in 2007. The stacked, shifted look was a response to the Bowery itself — for most of the twentieth century, a working-class commercial corridor of restaurant-supply stores, lighting wholesalers, pawn shops. SANAA's building was designed to feel less like a monument than like a stack of boxes temporarily placed on the lot.

The wedge on the right was designed by OMA — the Dutch firm founded by Rem Koolhaas, working here with the partner Shohei Shigematsu. It opened in March of 2026 and marks OMA's first cultural building in New York City. Where SANAA's tower is reticent, with no recognizable front, OMA's wedge is overt — a public plaza at the sidewalk, an atrium visible from the street, and a glass-and-mesh facade that reads as metallic by day and lit-up at night.

[beat]

What makes this site unusual is that New York now has one museum made of two buildings by two different living Pritzker Prize-winning architects, standing connected, with neither one deferring to the other. The museum's director called it "not a wing, not an extension, not an annex — a second building."

The museum itself is the only one in New York exclusively dedicated to contemporary art by emerging or under-recognized artists. There is no permanent collection. The exhibitions rotate constantly. The contents of both buildings are completely different from one year to the next.

Walk slowly past the front. The site sits at the terminus of Prince Street, which means you can see both buildings clearly from across the Bowery. Almost nobody walks past without slowing down.

tours.title: The Morgan Library & Museum
tours.shortDescription: Two and a quarter minutes at J.P. Morgan's private library — a McKim Mead and White Italian Renaissance villa holding three Gutenberg Bibles, Mozart manuscripts, and one of the great Old Master drawings collections in the world.
tours.longDescription:
A low Italian Renaissance villa sits beside a taller modern glass cube — at 225 Madison Avenue, between 36th and 37th Streets. This is the Morgan Library and Museum. The lower, columned structure on the south side was built between 1902 and 1906 as the private library of J.P. Morgan, the financier and one of the richest Americans of his era. Morgan hired Charles McKim, of McKim Mead and White, to design a building modeled on an Italian Renaissance villa, with limestone walls and a Palladian arched entrance. The library was Morgan's personal collection — manuscripts, rare books, drawings, prints — which he had been assembling for forty years.

Morgan died in 1913. His son, J.P. Morgan Jr., opened the library to the public in 1924, fulfilling his father's wishes. The collection has grown since: today it includes Mozart manuscripts, Beethoven's handwritten scores, original Dickens manuscripts, Mark Twain, three Gutenberg Bibles, and a collection of Old Master drawings rivaled only by a handful of museums in the world. The taller, more modern part of the building was added in 2006. The architect was Renzo Piano — the same architect who designed the Whitney's current building in the Meatpacking District. Piano added a glass-roofed cube of an atrium that connects the original library to two adjacent buildings, also acquired by the museum, providing space for galleries, a reading room, and a café. The expansion roughly doubled the museum's footprint while preserving the original library entirely.

What you go inside for is the East Room — the original library, the room J.P. Morgan built for himself. Three stories of dark wood bookshelves rise to a vaulted ceiling painted with frescoes. There is a fireplace large enough to walk into. The room is, by general agreement, one of the most beautiful private rooms ever built in America. It is also where Morgan's three Gutenberg Bibles are kept. The museum is small, intimate, and rewards time. Take an hour, see what's on display, then sit in the East Room for a few minutes before you leave.

stops.title: The Morgan Library & Museum
stops.caption: Stand on Madison Avenue between 36th and 37th Streets, in front of the Italian Renaissance villa beside the glass cube.
stops.transcriptText:
There are three Gutenberg Bibles inside this building. There are only forty-nine known to exist.

tours.title: The Shed
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes at Hudson Yards' southwest corner — a cultural building whose translucent shell rolls on rails to physically expand and contract, designed by Diller Scofidio + Renfro.
tours.longDescription:
The Shed sits at the southwest corner of Hudson Yards. It has two parts. The fixed part, at the back, is an eight-story tower of dark steel and glass, holding galleries, theaters, and rehearsal spaces. The moving part, at the front, is what most people notice first: a translucent, white-shelled outer envelope mounted on six enormous steel wheels. The shell can roll forward, telescoping over the adjacent plaza to enclose a larger performance space called the McCourt. When the show is over, the shell rolls back. The building, depending on the day, is two different sizes.

The Shed was designed by the New York firm Diller Scofidio + Renfro, in collaboration with the Rockwell Group. The same firm did the High Line, the Lincoln Center renovation, and the recent reimagining of MoMA. The idea of a building that physically grows and shrinks was their response to a problem: how do you build one cultural space that can host symphony concerts, fashion shows, art installations, dance, theater, and large-scale visual art — each requiring radically different rooms? The Shed's answer was to make one building that becomes another building. The structure rolls on rails embedded in the plaza, propelled by motors based on the same technology used to move gantry cranes at shipping ports. The shell weighs over four million pounds. It moves from closed to extended in about five minutes.

The Shed opened in 2019, just before the pandemic. Its early programming was ambitious: a Kanye West concert, an Anne Carson play, large installations. The reception was mixed — some critics called it the most interesting cultural building of its generation, others called it a corporate-philanthropy showpiece without a clear artistic mission. Whatever the politics, the engineering is real. There is no other cultural building in New York that does anything quite like this.

stops.title: The Shed
stops.caption: Stand on the plaza at the southwest corner of Hudson Yards, facing the building with the white shell on rails.
stops.transcriptText:
The building in front of you moves.

Look at the building. It has two parts. The fixed part, at the back, is an eight-story tower of dark steel and glass, holding galleries, theaters, and rehearsal spaces. The moving part, at the front, is what most people notice first: a translucent, white-shelled outer envelope mounted on six enormous steel wheels. tours.title: MoMA PS1
tours.shortDescription: Two minutes outside an 1893 Long Island City public school turned contemporary art space in 1976 — and merged with MoMA in 2000 while keeping its classroom-gallery character.
tours.longDescription:
The brick exterior, the arched windows, the bell tower at the back — all of it was built in 1893 as a public school for the children of nineteenth-century Long Island City. The number on the dedication stone is "PS 1." Many more public schools followed. This was the first. The building stands at 22-25 Jackson Avenue in Long Island City, Queens, and is now one of the most important contemporary art museums in New York.

The school closed in 1963. The building sat empty for nearly a decade. In 1976, an arts organizer named Alanna Heiss leased it from the city for a dollar a year and turned it into a contemporary art space. The original idea was to give artists working in unconventional formats — installation, video, performance, conceptual art — a place to make and show work that wasn't commercial enough for galleries or established enough for museums. The classrooms, with their high ceilings and broken light fixtures, were perfect for it. PS1 quickly became one of the most experimental art venues in the country. By the 1990s, it was famous for showing artists nobody else would — Vito Acconci, Adrian Piper, Jenny Holzer, Carrie Mae Weems. In 2000, PS1 merged with the Museum of Modern Art and became MoMA PS1.

The building has kept its character. The galleries are still the old classrooms — small, idiosyncratic, full of pipes and worn radiators and original tiled floors. PS1 has remained, somehow, the place artists go to do things they can't do anywhere else. In summer, the courtyard out back hosts "Warm Up" — a long-running outdoor concert series featuring experimental musicians, often in front of a temporary architectural installation by an invited firm. It is one of the more interesting weekly events in New York.

stops.title: MoMA PS1
stops.caption: Stand at 22-25 Jackson Avenue in Long Island City, in front of the brick former public school with the bell tower.
stops.transcriptText:
This building was Public School Number One.

The brick exterior, the arched windows, the bell tower at the back — all of it was built in 1893 as a public school for the children of nineteenth-century Long Island City. The number on the dedication stone is "PS 1." Many more public schools followed. This was the first. tours.title: American Museum of Natural History: Four Facades
tours.shortDescription: A walking tour around the outside of the museum — four sides, four architects, four eras. No tickets required.
tours.longDescription: The American Museum of Natural History takes up an entire city block on the Upper West Side. What most visitors never see is the outside: four facades, each designed by a different architect, in a different decade, in a completely different style. This tour walks the perimeter — a Beaux-Arts national temple on Central Park West, an 1890s granite cathedral on 77th Street, a 2023 biomimetic shell on Columbus Avenue, and a glass vitrine housing a floating aluminum sphere on 81st Street. Together they make the most complete history of American museum architecture you can see anywhere in New York City. Allow about 30 minutes to walk it at a comfortable pace.

stops.title: Introduction
stops.caption: Stand on Central Park West in front of the Theodore Roosevelt Memorial entrance before starting.
stops.transcriptText:
Most people experience the Museum of Natural History from the inside. This tour does the opposite.

The American Museum of Natural History takes up an entire city block on the Upper West Side of Manhattan. What most visitors don't realize is that the building's four sides — Central Park West, 77th Street, Columbus Avenue, and 81st Street — were each designed by a different architect, in a different decade, in a completely different style. This tour walks you around the outside of the building, stop by stop, looking at all four. No interior. No exhibits. Just the architecture.

Each side reflects a different era of what a museum was thought to be — a Beaux-Arts national temple, an 1890s stone cathedral, a 2000s glass vitrine, a 2023 biomimetic shell — and together, on one block, they make up the most complete history of American museum architecture you can see anywhere in New York City.

We'll start on Central Park West, at the Theodore Roosevelt Memorial entrance. When you're standing on the sidewalk in front of it, looking up at the limestone arch and Ionic columns, the first stop will begin.

stops.title: Central Park West — Theodore Roosevelt Memorial
stops.caption: The grandest of the four facades: limestone, Ionic columns, and an empty pedestal.
stops.transcriptText:
The pedestal in front of you is empty.

You're standing on Central Park West, on the sidewalk in front of the Theodore Roosevelt Memorial entrance — the grandest of the museum's four facades, and the one most people think of as the main entrance. The architecture is Beaux-Arts: a limestone triumphal arch flanked by Ionic columns, the words "TRUTH KNOWLEDGE VISION" carved across the top, a series of bronze figures along the parapet. It was designed by John Russell Pope and completed in 1936. Pope, in the same decade, designed the Jefferson Memorial and the National Gallery in Washington — this is the same architect, in the same style, building three monumental spaces meant to look like American temples.

The pedestal directly in front of the entrance, where a bronze equestrian statue of Theodore Roosevelt stood for eighty-two years, was emptied in January of 2022. The statue depicted Roosevelt on horseback, flanked by a Native American man and an African man walking beside him on foot. After decades of criticism — and reckoning, in 2020, about which monuments American cities should keep — the museum and the city agreed to remove it. The statue has been moved to North Dakota, to the Theodore Roosevelt Presidential Library. The pedestal stayed.

[beat]

What this side of the museum tells you, in 1936 architectural language, is what a great American institution thought it was — a temple, a memorial, a piece of national monument-building meant to last forever. What the empty pedestal in front of it tells you is that "forever" turns out to be a moving target.

This is the first of four sides of the museum. Each is a different era of architecture, built in a different decade, with a different idea of what a natural history museum is supposed to look like. The next stop is the 77th Street facade — head south down Central Park West to the corner. Take your time. The next part picks up when you're there.

stops.title: West 77th Street — Original Main Facade
stops.caption: The museum's front door for thirty-six years: pink Milford granite, Richardsonian Romanesque.
stops.transcriptText:
This was the front door of the museum for thirty-six years.

You're now on West 77th Street, looking at the original front facade of the American Museum of Natural History. The architecture is Richardsonian Romanesque — pink Milford granite, rough-hewn, with deep arched windows, heavy turrets, and a central entry arch deep enough to feel like the mouth of a cave. It was designed by J. Cleaveland Cady and built in stages between 1892 and 1900. From its completion until the Roosevelt entrance opened in 1936, this was the museum's main entrance. The granite is darker now, the carvings worn by a century of weather, but the shape is unchanged.

Behind this facade, and behind all the others you'll see today, is something older still. The original museum building, completed in 1877, was designed by Calvert Vaux — the same architect who, with Frederick Law Olmsted, designed Central Park, and who also designed the original Metropolitan Museum building across the park: three foundational New York institutions, by the same architect, in the same decade. Vaux's original building is still here, embedded inside the museum like a fossil, but you can't see it from any of the four sides because it was wrapped by later additions, including this one.

[beat]

This is the museum's 1890s vision — natural history as cathedral: dense, fortified, drawn from the same architectural language that built American libraries, train stations, and university buildings in the late nineteenth century. It was a place where serious people went to encounter serious things.

The next stop is around the block. Walk west on 77th to Columbus Avenue, then turn right. Walk at your own pace. The next stop will be waiting.

stops.title: Columbus Avenue — Richard Gilder Center
stops.caption: The newest addition: undulating pale shotcrete, no straight lines, no flat surfaces.
stops.transcriptText:
This building has no straight lines.

You're now on Columbus Avenue, looking at the Richard Gilder Center for Science, Education, and Innovation — the newest addition to the American Museum of Natural History. It opened in May of 2023. The architect was Jeanne Gang, of Studio Gang in Chicago. The cost was four hundred and sixty-five million dollars.

The form is undulating and organic — pale shotcrete, hand-sculpted by workers using small tools, with curving openings that suggest the inside of a slot canyon, or geological erosion, or the interior of a cell. There are no rectangular windows, no columns, no flat surfaces anywhere on the building. Studio Gang's work draws explicitly from biological and geological forms. The Gilder Center is meant to evoke the kinds of natural structures the museum is dedicated to studying. The interior carries the same logic — the central atrium is a five-story biomimetic cavern, with bridges and openings flowing through it.

[beat]

What's worth understanding, standing on Columbus Avenue, is the architectural distance between this and the building you just walked past on 77th Street. Cady's 1890s granite fortress and Gang's 2023 biomimetic shell are, in some sense, two opposite answers to the same question: what should a building about nature look like? Cady's answer was a temple — a place where nature could be put behind glass and contemplated. Gang's answer was a creature — a building that imitates the thing it contains. They are a hundred and thirty years apart, on the same block.

The last stop is around the corner. Walk north on Columbus to 81st Street, then turn right. No rush — the last stop won't start until you're standing in front of it.

stops.title: West 81st Street — Rose Center for Earth and Space
stops.caption: A ninety-five-foot glass cube with an eighty-seven-foot aluminum sphere floating inside it.
stops.transcriptText:
What you're looking at is a glass cube, ninety-five feet on each side, with an aluminum sphere — the Hayden Planetarium, eighty-seven feet across — floating inside it.

You're now on West 81st Street, looking at the Rose Center for Earth and Space — the museum's planetarium and astronomy wing. The structure is precisely what it appears to be: a transparent glass cube, suspended above an open base, with a vast aluminum sphere floating at its center. The sphere weighs four million pounds. The architect was James Stewart Polshek, whose firm is now called Ennead. The building opened in February of 2000 — the first major museum building of the new millennium in New York City.

The cube replaced an earlier planetarium, built in 1935, that had been the museum's astronomy wing for sixty-five years. The old building was a fairly conventional brick structure with a domed roof, of the type any city might have. Polshek's design did the opposite of conventional. He made the entire planetarium visible from the street. At night, lit from inside, the cube and sphere glow against the dark — visible from across Central Park, from a dozen blocks down Columbus Avenue, and from the windows of every building on this stretch of 81st Street. The architecture is, deliberately, a piece of public spectacle.

[beat]

This is the museum's 2000s vision — science as something to be displayed, not hidden. The 1890s building treated the museum as a cathedral, the 1936 building as a national temple, the 2000 building as a billboard or vitrine with its contents on view to anyone walking by, and the 2023 Gilder Center — the one you just left — as a living thing: four ideas, four decades, four sides of a single block.

You've now walked the whole museum — look at the cube one more time. The most complete history of American museum architecture you can see on a single city block is the one you just walked around.

tours.title: Chapel of Souls
tours.shortDescription: Porto's most photographed facade: 15,661 azulejo tiles, a 1929 revival disguised as the 18th century.
tours.longDescription: On Rua de Santa Catarina — Porto's main shopping street — a chapel is covered from ground to roofline in blue and white tiles depicting the lives of Saint Francis of Assisi and Saint Catherine of Alexandria. What almost nobody realises is that the tiles are not old. Eduardo Leite designed them in 1929 as a deliberate revival of the classical azulejo tradition, then had them manufactured at the Viúva Lamego ceramics factory in Lisbon. The chapel itself dates from the early 1700s, but the tiles covering it are two centuries younger — and the deception has held for almost a hundred years. The stained-glass window above the entrance, made in the 19th century, depicts souls in purgatory: the literal explanation for the chapel's name. It was founded as a place of prayer for people who died without family or the means for a proper burial — the unclaimed dead of Porto's streets.

stops.title: Chapel of Souls Facade
stops.caption: Stand on the opposite pavement — the facade only makes sense at a distance.
stops.transcriptText:
Before you do anything else, step back to the opposite pavement, because this facade only makes sense at a distance.

You're on Rua de Santa Catarina — Porto's main shopping street, lined with clothes shops and café windows — and directly in front of you is a chapel covered from the ground to the roofline in blue and white tiles, telling stories in scenes across three hundred and sixty square metres of wall. The scenes depict the lives of two saints: Saint Francis of Assisi on the left side of the building, and Saint Catherine of Alexandria on the right, with each panel showing a moment from their story — the stigmata, the martyrdom, the coronation — arranged across the facade like a comic strip scaled up to the size of a building.

What almost nobody realises, standing here, is that the tiles are not old. They look convincingly eighteenth century — the blue-and-white palette, the style of the figures, the way the decorative borders are composed — because Eduardo Leite designed them in nineteen twenty-nine as a deliberate revival of the classical azulejo tradition, then had them manufactured at the Viúva Lamego ceramics factory in Lisbon. The chapel itself dates from the early seventeen hundreds, but the tiles covering it are two centuries younger, and the whole effect is so thoroughly convincing that most visitors never question it.

Now have a look at the stained-glass window above the entrance before you go in, because it predates the tiles by several decades and depicts something rather specific: souls in purgatory, which is the literal explanation for the chapel's name. The building isn't called the Chapel of Souls for poetic reasons — it was founded as a place of prayer for people who died without family or the means for a proper burial, the unclaimed dead of Porto's streets, and the window was made in the nineteenth century to reflect exactly that purpose.

The chapel is still an active church, and it's free to enter if you'd like to go inside — but the real argument the building makes is on the outside, in those nearly sixteen thousand tiles, where a twentieth-century artist set out to make something that looked like it had always been there, and succeeded so completely that the deception has held for almost a hundred years.

tours.title: Rua do Passeio Alegre 212
tours.shortDescription: Souto de Moura's Cantareira: three concrete boxes that don't line up, and why that's the whole point.
tours.longDescription: On Rua do Passeio Alegre in Foz do Douro — the old neighbourhood at the mouth of the Douro where the river opens into the Atlantic — Souto de Moura completed the Cantareira in 2013. From across the pavement it reads as three concrete boxes stacked on top of each other, each rotated slightly from the one below, so the facade planes shift and lean from floor to floor in a way that has no obvious geometric logic until someone explains what's actually going on. Each level was rotated deliberately, so inside the apartments the rooms don't face the estuary straight on — they look at it from an oblique angle, and a corner window captures the water and the far bank simultaneously in a way a flat-fronted building wouldn't give you. Look closely at the joints between floors: what appears to be three separate volumes is structurally one continuous concrete frame, with a narrow shadow line carved at the edge doing the work of visual separation.

stops.title: Cantareira Facade
stops.caption: Step back far enough to read the whole facade — the floors don't line up.
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about this building, which you'll notice the moment you step back far enough to read the whole facade: the floors don't line up with each other.

You're on Rua do Passeio Alegre, in Foz do Douro — the old neighbourhood at the mouth of the Douro, where the river opens into the Atlantic and where Porto's wealthier families have traditionally kept their houses facing the water. The street has the character that money and time make together: mature trees, well-maintained villas, a particular quiet. Souto de Moura completed the Cantareira in twenty thirteen, and from across the pavement it reads as three concrete boxes stacked on top of each other, each one rotated slightly from the one below, so the facade planes shift and lean and alternate from floor to floor in a way that has no obvious geometric logic until someone explains what's actually going on.

What's actually going on is this: each level was rotated deliberately, which means that inside the apartments the rooms don't face the estuary straight on — they look at it from an oblique angle, and a window at the corner of a rotated room captures the water and the far bank simultaneously in a way that a flat-fronted building simply wouldn't give you.

Now have a closer look at the joints between the floors, because there's a structural sleight of hand happening there that's easy to miss. The building reads as three separate volumes, but Souto de Moura didn't want to double the structure at each level — a floor slab above and a separate ceiling slab below, stacked twice over, would have been wasteful and unnecessarily thick — so instead each concrete slab extends right to the face of the building, and a narrow slot is carved in the concrete at the edge, creating a shadow line that reads as a gap between separate volumes. What looks like three stacked boxes is, structurally, one continuous concrete frame with a shadow doing the work of visual separation.

The bedrooms face the rear courtyard and the living rooms face the street and the estuary, which tells you that the building has a clear opinion about which direction matters — and it just doesn't face it straight.

tours.title: Casa de Chá da Boa Nova
tours.shortDescription: Siza's first building: a tea house on the Atlantic rocks that earned two Michelin stars and a national monument listing.
