stops.caption: Former NYC Pavilion of the 1939 & 1964 World's Fairs, Flushing Meadows Corona Park
stops.transcriptText:
Inside this building is a scale model of New York City that has been continuously updated for sixty years.

You're outside the Queens Museum, in Flushing Meadows Corona Park, in the same building that served as the New York City Pavilion for both the 1939 and 1964 World's Fairs. The building's most famous interior feature is the Panorama of the City of New York, a meticulous three-dimensional scale model of the entire five boroughs at a ratio of one inch to one hundred feet — meaning that a building one hundred feet tall is one inch tall on the model. The Panorama covers nine thousand three hundred and thirty-five square feet and contains approximately eight hundred and ninety-five thousand individual buildings.

The model was originally built for the 1964 World's Fair, when Robert Moses commissioned it as a kind of architectural promotional film made physical — a way for visitors to see, in one room, the scale and ambition of postwar New York. Crews of carpenters and architects worked for three years to build it, using city planning records, aerial photographs, and on-site measurements. When the fair opened, visitors rode an automated "helicopter tour" around the perimeter of the model on a moving platform suspended above it.

The Panorama survived the closing of the fair and has been continuously updated ever since. When buildings are demolished, the museum's staff replaces their models with empty lots, and when new buildings open, model versions are built and dropped into place. The World Trade Center towers were on the model from 1964 to 2002, then the empty footprints were left in place for several years, and now two new towers — One World Trade and Three World Trade — occupy the same scale spot.

The other thing worth knowing about this building is that the United Nations General Assembly met here, in this same room, from 1946 to 1950 — the years when the UN was being founded and its permanent headquarters was being built in Manhattan. The vote to partition Palestine, the early debates of the Cold War, and the founding of UNICEF all happened in what is now the Panorama gallery. A few brass plaques on the floor mark where the delegates' tables stood.

The museum is open Wednesday through Sunday, and the Panorama room alone is worth the trip — it is genuinely impossible to come away from it without noticing something about New York you had never noticed before.

tours.title: Museum of the Moving Image
tours.shortDescription: A museum of film, TV, and digital media built inside Paramount's former East Coast studio, where the Marx Brothers shot their first movie in 1929.
tours.longDescription: The Museum of the Moving Image occupies a building that was part of Paramount's Astoria Studios from 1920 to 1971 — the studio where the Marx Brothers filmed 'The Cocoanuts' (1929) while performing the same show on Broadway each evening. Renovated in 1988 and dramatically expanded by Leeser Architecture in 2011, the museum holds 130,000 artifacts and an interactive permanent exhibition on the craft of making moving images. Kaufman Astoria Studios, the still-active descendant of the same Paramount complex, is directly next door — 'Sesame Street,' 'Orange Is the New Black,' and 'Saturday Night Live' rehearsals all happen within a block.

stops.title: Museum of the Moving Image
stops.caption: Former Paramount Astoria Studios, Astoria, Queens
stops.transcriptText:
The Marx Brothers made their first movie inside this building.

You're in Astoria, Queens, outside the Museum of the Moving Image, in a building that was originally part of the Paramount Famous Players-Lasky Studios — Paramount's East Coast production facility from 1920 until 1971. The studios occupied an entire block of Astoria and produced hundreds of films and television shows during their five decades of operation, including the Marx Brothers' first movie, "The Cocoanuts," in 1929, which was shot here while the brothers were simultaneously performing the same material on Broadway in the evenings.

The studios closed in 1971, after Paramount consolidated its operations in Los Angeles, but the buildings themselves were preserved. In 1988, one of the larger structures was renovated and opened as the Museum of the Moving Image — a museum dedicated to the history, technology, and artistry of film, television, video games, and digital media. The current building was extensively redesigned in 2011 by the New York firm Leeser Architecture, which added blue-glowing translucent surfaces and a sweeping new exterior while preserving the original Paramount-era brick.

The collection inside is roughly one hundred and thirty thousand artifacts — original film cameras, props from movies, makeup molds, animation cels, vintage televisions and video game consoles, costumes from "Star Trek" and "Mad Men" and "The Sopranos." The museum's permanent exhibition, "Behind the Screen," is interactive: visitors can edit a scene from a film, dub a voice over a clip, or animate a short piece of stop-motion. The exhibits are designed less as a passive history than as a working introduction to the technical craft of making moving images.

What makes this museum specifically interesting is its proximity to the still-active film industry in this neighborhood. The Kaufman Astoria Studios — the modern operating descendant of the same Paramount complex — is directly next door, and films and television shows are produced there continuously. "Sesame Street" has been filmed at Kaufman Astoria for most of its run, "Orange Is the New Black" was filmed there, and "Saturday Night Live" rehearses there before going live. So the museum is not just a history of moving image production but a kind of explanation in advance of what is happening, this week, fifty feet to the north.

The museum is open Wednesday through Sunday, and the building exterior, with its electric-blue glow at night, is one of the most striking pieces of recent architecture in Queens.

tours.title: Snug Harbor Cultural Center
tours.shortDescription: Five matched Greek Revival halls built as America's first retirement home for merchant sailors, sold to New York City for $1 and preserved as an 83-acre cultural complex.
tours.longDescription: Sailors' Snug Harbor was founded under the 1801 will of shipping merchant Robert Richard Randall, who left his entire estate to create a home for 'aged, decrepit and worn-out sailors.' The five Greek Revival halls — designed by self-taught architect Minard Lafever — operated as a sailors' retirement community from 1833 to 1976, housing more than 28,000 mariners over that span. Sold to New York City for one dollar in 1976 to prevent demolition, the complex now hosts the Staten Island Museum, Newhouse Center for Contemporary Art, Noble Maritime Collection, Staten Island Botanical Garden, and a Chinese Scholar's Garden.

stops.title: Snug Harbor Cultural Center
stops.caption: Former Sailors' Snug Harbor, north shore of Staten Island
stops.transcriptText:
These buildings were a retirement home for sailors.

You're at Snug Harbor, an eighty-three-acre cultural complex on the north shore of Staten Island. The five matched Greek Revival buildings facing you across the front lawn — white columns, Ionic capitals, pediments, all of them connected by a single covered walkway — were built between 1831 and 1880 as Sailors' Snug Harbor, the first home in the United States for retired and disabled sailors of the American merchant marine. The complex was funded entirely by a New York shipping merchant named Robert Richard Randall, who left his entire estate, in 1801, for the explicit purpose of creating "a home for aged, decrepit and worn-out sailors." Randall had no other heirs and no nautical experience himself.

The buildings were designed by Minard Lafever, a self-taught architect who became one of the leading American practitioners of Greek Revival in the mid-nineteenth century. The five main halls served different functions — chapel, dining hall, dormitories, infirmary — and they were arranged along an axis facing the Kill Van Kull, the strait that separates Staten Island from New Jersey. Retired sailors lived here until the end of their lives, with full board and lodging, in exchange for nothing other than their service at sea.

Sailors' Snug Harbor operated continuously from 1833 to 1976, by which time it had housed more than twenty-eight thousand retired mariners. As the American merchant marine declined through the mid-twentieth century, the population of the home also declined, and by the 1970s the complex was almost empty, expensive to maintain, and slated for demolition. A coalition of preservationists, including the architectural historian Margot Gayle, fought to save the buildings, and in 1976 the entire site was sold to the City of New York for one dollar, on the condition that it be preserved as a cultural complex.

What now occupies the site is a federation of separate institutions: the Staten Island Museum in the largest of the original buildings, the Newhouse Center for Contemporary Art, the Noble Maritime Collection of nineteenth-century maritime artifacts and paintings, the Staten Island Botanical Garden, and a Chinese Scholar's Garden built by Chinese craftsmen in the 1990s using traditional materials. The grounds also include a working farm, a children's museum, and three concert venues.

The site is open every day and is free to walk through. The main hall is occasionally open for tours, and the interior is worth the visit — the original chapel ceiling, painted to imitate a star-filled sky, is one of the most unusual mid-nineteenth-century American interiors that has survived.

tours.title: Yankee Stadium
tours.shortDescription: The 2009 home of the New York Yankees, built directly across the street from the original 'House that Ruth Built,' which stood from 1923 to 2008.
tours.longDescription: The current Yankee Stadium opened in April 2009 on River Avenue in the South Bronx, replacing the original stadium that stood across 161st Street from 1923 to 2008. Designed by Populous to evoke the original's limestone facade, copper frieze, and bronze lettering, the $1.5 billion building was largely financed through public tax-exempt bonds. The original — where Babe Ruth, Lou Gehrig, Joe DiMaggio, Mickey Mantle, and Derek Jeter played — was demolished; its footprint is now Heritage Field, a public park visible from the new stadium's exterior.

stops.title: Yankee Stadium — Main Gate
stops.caption: 161st Street & River Avenue, South Bronx
stops.transcriptText:
This stadium is a careful imitation of the one that used to stand across the street.

You're at 161st Street and River Avenue, in the South Bronx, outside the main gate of Yankee Stadium — the home of the New York Yankees baseball franchise. The current stadium opened in April 2009, on a site directly across 161st Street from where the original Yankee Stadium stood from 1923 to 2008. The architect was the firm Populous, formerly known as HOK Sport, and the new stadium was designed to recall as many features of the original as possible: the limestone facade, the bronze "Yankee Stadium" lettering above the gate, the decorative copper frieze that runs along the top of the upper deck, and the angled wall in right field that gave the original its distinctive shape.

The original Yankee Stadium, which was demolished after the new one opened, was the most famous baseball stadium in the world. Babe Ruth played his first season there, in 1923, and it was Ruth's enormous draw that justified its construction — sportswriter Fred Lieb's nickname for it, "the House that Ruth Built," is the name that stuck. Lou Gehrig, Joe DiMaggio, Mickey Mantle, Yogi Berra, Whitey Ford, Reggie Jackson, Don Mattingly, and Derek Jeter all played most or all of their careers in that building, and the Yankees won twenty-six of their twenty-seven World Series championships either at the old stadium or its 1976 renovation.

The new stadium cost approximately one and a half billion dollars to build, most of it in public subsidies through the city's tax-exempt bond program, and the move across the street was contentious. The original stadium had been a New York City landmark for the better part of a century, the new stadium was largely identical in feel but came with significantly more luxury suites and fewer affordable seats, and the original site was turned into a public park called Heritage Field that now occupies the footprint of the demolished building.

You can see the original site from where you're standing. The ball field at the center of Heritage Field sits in approximately the same position as the field of the old stadium, and during games at the new stadium, the lights from the new stadium illuminate the empty grass of the old one across the street.

The stadium itself is open on game days, with public tours available on most non-game days. The Monument Park area, beyond the center field wall, contains the bronze plaques honoring the most important Yankees players, and is open to ticketholders before game time.

tours.title: Citi Field
tours.shortDescription: The Mets' 2009 stadium designed not to evoke itself but Ebbets Field — a demolished Brooklyn ballpark for a team that left New York half a century earlier.
tours.longDescription: Citi Field opened in 2009 in Flushing Meadows, Queens, replacing Shea Stadium (1964–2008). Designed by Populous — the same firm behind the new Yankee Stadium — it deliberately references Ebbets Field, the demolished Brooklyn home of the Dodgers, who left New York in 1957. The Jackie Robinson Rotunda at the main entrance honours the Dodgers legend who never played in Queens. Shea Stadium's footprint, marked with its original bases and home plate, sits in the adjacent parking lot.

stops.title: Citi Field — Main Entrance
stops.caption: Home of the New York Mets, Flushing Meadows, Queens
stops.transcriptText:
Citi Field was designed to look like a stadium for a team that doesn't exist anymore.

You're in Flushing Meadows, Queens, outside Citi Field — the home of the New York Mets baseball franchise. The stadium opened in 2009, replacing the older Shea Stadium that operated next door from 1964 to 2008. The architect was the firm Populous, the same firm that designed the new Yankee Stadium that opened the same year. But unlike the new Yankee Stadium — which was designed to imitate the old one across the street — Citi Field was designed to imitate a different stadium entirely: Ebbets Field, the legendary Brooklyn home of the Brooklyn Dodgers, which was demolished in 1960.

The Dodgers, as you may know, left Brooklyn in 1957 and moved to Los Angeles. The Mets were founded in 1962 as a National League replacement franchise to fill the void left by the Dodgers and Giants when both moved west. From the team's founding, the Mets cultivated a sense of inheritance from the old Brooklyn franchise — borrowing colors, names, and emotional weight from a team that had been gone for five years. Citi Field extends that lineage in architecture: the brick facade, the arched windows, the rotunda at the main entrance, all of them deliberate references to the architectural vocabulary of Ebbets Field.

The rotunda at the main entrance, the first space you walk into, is named the Jackie Robinson Rotunda, in honor of the Brooklyn Dodgers' Hall of Famer who broke baseball's color barrier in 1947. The walls are inscribed with Robinson's words, and his number 42 is retired across all of Major League Baseball — but it is displayed here with particular prominence, even though Robinson never played a single game in Queens, and never played for the Mets, and was indeed retired before the Mets existed.

What makes Citi Field interesting, standing outside it, is the question of what a stadium is supposed to be. The new Yankee Stadium imitates a stadium that stood next door for eighty-six years. Citi Field imitates a stadium that was demolished nearly fifty years before it was built, for a team that left town a decade before that. Both are nostalgia projects in their way, but only one of them has anything actually to be nostalgic about in this location — Citi Field's nostalgia is borrowed from someone else's borough.

The Mets play here from April through September, and the stadium is open for public tours in the off-season. Shea Stadium's former footprint, in the parking lot just east of here, is now marked with bases and a home plate where the original ones stood.

tours.title: Madison Square Garden
tours.shortDescription: The arena built over Penn Station's ruins — and the reason New York passed America's first landmarks preservation law in 1965.
tours.longDescription: Madison Square Garden (the fourth building to bear the name) opened in 1968 above the platforms of Penn Station, whose Beaux-Arts McKim, Mead & White hall — completed 1910, widely considered the most beautiful building in America — was demolished between 1963 and 1968 to make way for it. The public outcry prompted New York City to pass the Landmarks Preservation Act in 1965, the law that later saved Grand Central Terminal. MSG's operating permit, renewed for only five years in 2023, expires in 2028; multiple proposals for rebuilding Penn Station below — with or without a new MSG above — are under active consideration.

stops.title: Madison Square Garden — 7th Avenue
stops.caption: 7th Avenue & 33rd Street, above Penn Station, Midtown Manhattan
stops.transcriptText:
Madison Square Garden is the reason historic preservation exists in America.

You're at the corner of 7th Avenue and 33rd Street, outside Madison Square Garden. The arena itself — a round, low-slung drum that occupies the entire block between 31st and 33rd, 7th and 8th avenues — is to your west, and the main 7th Avenue entrance, with its glass-and-LED canopy, is just south of you. Behind you, on the corner itself, is a separate glass dome: the East End Gateway, a new entrance to Penn Station that opened in 2020.

The building above you opened in 1968 and is, by general consensus, one of the least distinguished pieces of major architecture in midtown Manhattan. The architect was Charles Luckman; the building's external form is a windowless drum on a concrete plinth; the interior is the working arena where the Knicks and the Rangers play, along with about three hundred and twenty other major events a year. This is the fourth structure to use the name "Madison Square Garden" — the previous three, none of them on this site, were built in 1879, 1890, and 1925.

The reason this particular Garden matters historically is what was destroyed to build it. Under the streets you're standing on is Penn Station, the busiest train station in the Western Hemisphere. The original Penn Station was an enormous Beaux-Arts masterpiece, completed in 1910 by the firm McKim, Mead and White — a travertine and granite hall with vaulted ceilings, classical statuary, and a waiting room modeled on the Baths of Caracalla. It was widely considered one of the most beautiful pieces of architecture in the United States.

In 1962, the Pennsylvania Railroad, which owned the station and was losing money on passenger service, announced that it would demolish the building and sell the air rights over the train platforms to a developer. The developer planned to build Madison Square Garden on top of the platforms. Architects, historians, and preservation groups protested, and the New York Times published an editorial calling the demolition a civic crime, but the city had no legal authority to stop it. The station was demolished between 1963 and 1968, and the new MSG opened on top of the platforms in 1968.

The shock of the loss prompted New York City, in 1965, to pass the Landmarks Preservation Act, the first effective historic preservation law in the United States. It came too late for Penn Station, but it has saved hundreds of buildings since, including Grand Central Terminal — which was nearly demolished in the 1970s and was protected, after a Supreme Court case, by the same Landmarks Act that Penn Station's destruction had inspired.

The building you're looking at is, in most of the ways that matter, not the same one that opened in 1968. Between 2011 and 2013, MSG underwent a billion-dollar renovation — a project the owners called "the Transformation" — that gutted and rebuilt the entire arena interior, with new seating, new concourses, two suspended bridges spanning the playing floor, restored ceiling work, and a fully rebuilt 7th Avenue entrance. The canopy south of you, with two six-hundred-square-foot LED screens set into its ceiling, is part of that entrance — roughly twice the size of the original. And the concrete shell of the 1968 structure, still standing behind the new entrance and around the rest of the building, has since been wrapped in programmable LED lighting that runs along the seams of the facade and around the roof — so the whole building is now lit, at night, in colors that change with the event inside.

What's worth knowing standing here is that the future of the building is, for the first time in decades, genuinely in question. In 2023, the city's special operating permit for the arena was renewed for only five years — half the term of the previous permit, and a deliberate signal from the City Council that it had grown frustrated by the lack of cooperation between MSG's ownership and the agencies trying to redevelop Penn Station beneath the building. The permit expires in 2028, and multiple competing proposals are under active consideration: some that would rebuild Penn Station while keeping MSG above it, others that would move MSG to a new site nearby and finally allow a full Penn Station rebuild below.

The architectural critic Vincent Scully described the original loss with a line that has been quoted ever since: "One entered the city like a god; one scuttles in now like a rat." Whether you read that as a permanent indictment or as a temporary embarrassment depends, more than at any other point in the past sixty years, on what happens between now and 2028.

tours.title: Riverside Church
tours.shortDescription: John D. Rockefeller Jr.'s Gothic Revival skyscraper-church, where Martin Luther King delivered his 1967 'Beyond Vietnam' speech — and America's tallest church tower.
tours.longDescription: Riverside Church was built 1927–1930 on Riverside Drive at 120th Street, funded by John D. Rockefeller Jr. and modelled on Chartres Cathedral. Its 392-foot tower — the tallest church tower in the United States — houses the world's largest carillon (74 bells, including one weighing over 20 tons). The pulpit has hosted Nelson Mandela, Desmond Tutu, the Dalai Lama, and on April 4, 1967 — exactly one year before his assassination — Martin Luther King Jr., who delivered 'Beyond Vietnam: A Time to Break Silence,' now considered one of the most important speeches of 20th-century American rhetoric.

stops.title: Riverside Church
stops.caption: Riverside Drive at 120th Street, Morningside Heights, Manhattan
stops.transcriptText:
Martin Luther King gave his speech against the Vietnam War from the pulpit of this church.

You're on Riverside Drive at 120th Street, outside Riverside Church — a Gothic Revival skyscraper-church built between 1927 and 1930, funded almost entirely by John D. Rockefeller Junior, who also funded The Cloisters museum twenty blocks north and the Riverside campus of Columbia University across the street. The building has a Gothic structural exterior and a steel-frame interior, the tower is three hundred and ninety-two feet tall — the tallest church tower in the United States — and the carillon at the top contains seventy-four bells, the largest in the world, including a single bottom bell that weighs more than twenty tons.

The church was designed by Henry C. Pelton and the firm Allen, Pelton and Collens, and it was deliberately patterned on the Cathedral of Chartres in France, with significant modifications for the New York site and for Protestant rather than Catholic worship. Rockefeller and the Baptist pastor Harry Emerson Fosdick, who served as the church's first preacher, envisioned an ecumenical Protestant congregation that would draw worshippers across denominational lines and across the income spectrum — an explicitly cosmopolitan, urban, modern Protestantism, of the kind that defined much of mainstream American religious thought in the middle of the twentieth century.

The pulpit has been used by an unusual concentration of internationally significant speakers. Reinhold Niebuhr preached here, Nelson Mandela spoke here after his release from prison, Desmond Tutu spoke here, and the Dalai Lama has spoken from this pulpit. Bill Clinton, Cesar Chavez, Kofi Annan, Fidel Castro, and Jesse Jackson have all stood at it.

But the speech that defined the church's twentieth-century identity was Martin Luther King's address on April 4, 1967, exactly one year before he was assassinated. The speech, titled "Beyond Vietnam: A Time to Break Silence," was King's most direct public condemnation of the American war in Vietnam, and it was widely criticized at the time — including by the New York Times — as a misuse of his civil rights authority to oppose government foreign policy. The speech, taken in full, is now considered one of the most important pieces of American twentieth-century rhetoric.

What makes Riverside Church specifically interesting, standing outside it, is its dual identity. It is a religious building, with active congregational life and weekly services, and it is also a civic and political institution — a kind of national pulpit where significant public speech happens on the questions of the moment. The building was designed for both.

Grant's Tomb, the burial place of President Ulysses S. Grant and his wife Julia, is in the small park immediately north of the church, and is open to the public. The church itself is open most days, with daily services and occasional concerts on the carillon.

tours.title: One World Trade Center
tours.shortDescription: The 1,776-foot tower completed in 2014 on the site of the original World Trade Center — the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, and one of the most symbolic skyscrapers ever built.
tours.longDescription: One World Trade Center, completed 2014, stands 1,776 feet — the year of American independence, by deliberate design. Architect David Childs of Skidmore, Owings and Merrill gave it a square base that chamfers progressively into an octagon at mid-height and back to a rotated square at the top, creating a twisting silhouette. The $3.5 billion tower was not built on the original north tower's footprint; those footprints, along with the south tower's, are preserved as the 9/11 Memorial reflecting pools immediately south. The base has 30-foot-thick reinforced concrete walls and no windows below the 20th floor.

stops.title: One World Trade Center
stops.caption: Northwest corner of the World Trade Center site, Lower Manhattan
stops.transcriptText:
The height of this tower is a date.

Look up. The building rising in front of you is One World Trade Center, completed in 2014, at the northwest corner of what was the original World Trade Center complex. The tower is one thousand seven hundred and seventy-six feet tall — not by coincidence, but by deliberate design, in reference to the year of American independence. It is the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, and one of the most heavily symbolic skyscrapers ever built in the United States.

The architect was David Childs of the firm Skidmore, Owings and Merrill — the same firm that designed Lever House across town, and most of the major American corporate skyscrapers of the past seventy years. The building's form is more complicated than it first appears: the base is a square cube, two hundred feet on each side, but as the tower rises, the corners are progressively chamfered, so that the cube transforms into a perfect octagon at the midpoint, and then narrows again toward a smaller square at the top, oriented forty-five degrees from the base. The effect, depending on the angle, is that the building seems to twist as it rises.

Construction took eight years and cost approximately three and a half billion dollars, making it one of the most expensive office buildings ever built. The tower replaced the original World Trade Center's north tower, which collapsed on September 11, 2001 — but it was not built on the original tower's footprint. The footprints of the two original towers, both preserved as reflecting pools, are the centerpieces of the 9/11 Memorial, just south of you, and the new tower was built on a site nearby so that the memorial pools could occupy the original tower locations exactly.

What this building does, standing here looking up at it, is occupy a kind of impossible role. It has to be a working skyscraper with viable commercial office space, and it has to be tall enough to signal that the city has rebuilt. It has to be safe — the building has reinforced concrete walls thirty feet thick at the base, no windows below the twentieth floor, and is engineered to be one of the most blast-resistant buildings in the world. And it has to coexist, every day, with the memorial directly beside it — a fact that shapes nearly every aspect of its design, from the height to the orientation to the symbolic numbers.

The observation deck at the top, called One World Observatory, is open to the public, and the building's main entrance is on the south side, facing the memorial plaza. The ground floor of the building includes the West Concourse, a long underground passageway connecting to the Oculus and the various subway lines.

tours.title: Porto Tram Museum

stops.title: Porto Tram Museum
stops.caption: Inside the former Massarelos thermoelectric power station — trams arranged chronologically across the floor; start at the oldest, on the left.
stops.transcriptText:
The building you're standing in used to generate the electricity that powered the trams outside, which means the machines that moved the city were fed by this building, and now the building holds the machines — and there is a neatness to that circularity that makes it one of the more satisfying museum premises in Porto.
You're in the former Massarelos thermoelectric power station, built in nineteen fifteen on the bank of the Douro, and the main floor is a large industrial hall with a high ceiling, exposed ironwork, and the particular smell of old wood and metal that comes from vehicles that stopped running decades ago but have been cared for ever since. The trams are arranged across the floor roughly in chronological order, with the oldest to your left and the most recent toward the far end, so walk slowly toward the oldest one first and let the building organise the history for you.
The first vehicle you'll reach is the Carro Americano — the American Car — a horse-drawn tram whose design came from the United States and whose specific body was built by the Starbuck Car and Wagon Company in Birkenhead, England, in eighteen seventy-two, and which was pulled through Porto's streets by mules for the better part of thirty years before the last one was retired in nineteen oh four. It is, standing in front of it, a genuinely strange object: wooden benches, open platforms, and a name that commemorates an American design tradition built in an English industrial town for a Portuguese city that would eventually replace it with electric vehicles inspired by the same American model, only without the animal.
Continue further into the hall toward the electric trams, because the dates here matter.
In eighteen ninety-five, Porto became the first city on the entire Iberian Peninsula to electrify its tram network — six years before Lisbon, which is a fact Porto is entirely justified in being pleased about — and the electricity for those early cars came from this building, the one you're standing in now. At the network's peak in nineteen fifty-eight, the city was running a hundred and ninety-two trams across eighty-one kilometres of track, which covered every neighbourhood and every hill, and then came the car, and the lines closed through the sixties and seventies with most of the fleet scrapped, until only three heritage routes survived through the old city.

What's worth noticing, before you leave, is what the trams themselves look like up close — the polished wood interiors, the brass fittings, the windows scaled for a narrower city — because these weren't simply transit vehicles but the physical infrastructure around which Porto's streets were originally built: the hills, the tight corners, the cobblestones, the whole texture of the old city was shaped partly by the width of a tram car and the radius it could turn, and Line One stops right outside if you'd like to feel what that radius actually is.

tours.longDescription: The Porto Tram Museum occupies the former Massarelos thermoelectric power station, built in 1915 on the bank of the Douro — the building that once generated the electricity that powered Porto's trams now holds them. A large industrial hall with high ceiling and exposed ironwork, the floor laid out chronologically: oldest to your left, most recent toward the far end. The earliest vehicle is the Carro Americano, a horse-drawn tram built in Birkenhead by the Starbuck Car and Wagon Company in 1872 and pulled through Porto's streets by mules for thirty years. In 1895, Porto became the first city on the Iberian Peninsula to electrify its tram network — six years before Lisbon, a fact Porto is justifiably pleased about — and the early electric cars drew their power from this building. At the network's 1958 peak the city ran 192 trams across 81 kilometres of track; the lines closed through the sixties and seventies, leaving only three heritage routes through the old city. Line One stops directly outside.
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes inside the former Massarelos thermoelectric power station — the building that once generated electricity for Porto's trams now holds them.

tours.title: Church of Santa Maria

stops.title: Church of Santa Maria
stops.caption: On the forecourt platform facing the main facade — a 17.5-metre square of white concrete with two projecting towers and ten-metre steel doors between them.
stops.transcriptText:
This church exists because a parish priest made a phone call, and the fact that it ended up as one of the most discussed pieces of religious architecture of the twentieth century begins with that single decision.

Father Nuno Higino decided personally to commission Álvaro Siza — no competition, no institutional process, just a direct approach and a complete willingness to trust him — and Siza's first visit to the site left him, by his own account, genuinely disturbed: a steep escarpment, a noisy highway running in front and to the side, height differences that made any conventional placement seem impossible. He spent a long time studying one nearby building, which he described as not particularly beautiful but geometrically precise, and placed the church at right angles to it, which became the foundation of every other decision that followed.

You should be standing on the forecourt platform, facing the main facade, having come up a ramp or a flight of stairs from street level, which is four metres below where you're standing now, and from here the church already tells you what it intends to be: the road is behind you, the building turns its back on it entirely, and what you're facing is a square of white concrete seventeen and a half metres on each side with three sections, two projecting towers, and ten-metre steel doors between them that open the full height of the facade.

There is no ornament on the walls, which is worth stating plainly because it takes a moment to fully register: no carvings, no applied decoration, no colour, nothing — just the concrete surface and the geometry, and the very deliberate implication that if you want the building to give you something, you'll have to go inside to find it.

Go inside, and turn right toward the long window at the base of the nave wall.

It runs the full width of the space at floor level, a horizontal strip of light low to the ground rather than above the congregation's heads, and in most churches light enters from high windows or clerestories so that the congregation looks up toward it — whereas here it enters from below eye level, washing the floor and the lower walls, so that the light doesn't lead you upward but spreads horizontally through the space at the level of your feet, which produces an atmosphere that is completely unlike any conventional ecclesiastical interior and entirely dependent on the concrete walls having nothing on them to interrupt it.

[beat]

"Directly below the assembly hall — in the same building, reached from outside, designed as part of the original commission — is the mortuary chapel, so the church is literally built over its own funeral space, stacking the two functions vertically in a way that, in Portugal, where the relationship between the Church and death has been continuous for a thousand years, Siza treated not as a provocation but as the natural structural logic of a building that takes both things seriously. The building won the Grande Prémio Nacional de Arquitectura in nineteen ninety-three, and the jury cited specifically ""the use of light in the poetic conformation of architecture,"" which is, when you're standing here with the floor-level light crossing in front of you, a rather precise description of what's actually happening."

tours.longDescription: The Igreja de Santa Maria de Marco de Canaveses exists because parish priest Father Nuno Higino made one phone call — no competition, no institutional process, just a direct approach to Álvaro Siza and complete willingness to trust him. The site is a steep escarpment with a noisy highway running in front, and Siza placed the church at right angles to a nearby building he described as not particularly beautiful but geometrically precise — the foundational move from which every other decision followed. The main facade is a 17.5-metre square of white concrete with two projecting towers and ten-metre steel doors between them, four metres above street level. No carvings, no applied decoration, no colour — just the concrete surface and the geometry, with the deliberate implication that if you want the building to give you something, you'll have to go inside to find it. Inside, a long window runs the full width of the nave at floor level, a horizontal strip of light low to the ground rather than above the congregation's heads — light enters from below eye level, washing the floor and lower walls, producing an atmosphere unlike any conventional ecclesiastical interior. The mortuary chapel is stacked directly beneath the assembly hall, the church built literally over its own funeral space — for Siza, the natural structural logic of a building that takes both things seriously. Awarded the Grande Prémio Nacional de Arquitectura in 1993, with the jury citing 'the use of light in the poetic conformation of architecture.'
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes at Álvaro Siza's modernist parish church in Marco de Canaveses — commissioned by a single phone call, awarded the 1993 Grande Prémio Nacional de Arquitectura.

tours.title: Fundação Livraria Lello

stops.title: Fundação Livraria Lello
stops.caption: Facing the main church facade with the fortified tower on your left — arrow slits and crenellations that were functional, not ornamental.
stops.transcriptText:
The ground beneath this monastery has been a place of worship for roughly two thousand years, with a Roman temple to Jupiter occupying the site before Christianity arrived and built over it — which is the kind of origin story that puts a fourteenth-century fortified church very firmly in its place.

You should be facing the main church facade, with the fortified tower on your left carrying arrow slits and crenellations that are not ornamental but were genuinely functional: the Knights Hospitaller, a military order whose original purpose was to protect pilgrims travelling to Jerusalem, were given this site in the twelfth century and built it to work, because the monastery sat directly on the Portuguese route of the Camino de Santiago and the Knights were responsible for the safety of everyone who passed through. Before you go inside, find the circular structure to the side of the main entrance — Álvaro Siza's contribution to the recent renovation — which is a former storage silo that has been converted into an open fountain where visitors are invited to remove their shoes and stand in it, a gesture that references the practice of foot-washing before entering a sacred space, which runs across multiple religious traditions and which Siza, now in his nineties, chose to make present here on one of the great pilgrimage roads of medieval Europe.

Go in through the main entrance when you're ready, and take a moment in the cloister before moving into the church.

The monastery's most historically charged interior moment happened in thirteen seventy-two, when the King of Portugal, Fernando the First, married Leonor Teles here in secret, having broken off his engagement to a Spanish princess in circumstances that nearly triggered a war between the two kingdoms — and the cloister you're walking through was already old when that happened, having been built in the decades following the monastery's founding in the twelfth century, which gives the space a layered quality that's difficult to describe but very easy to feel when you're standing in it.

[beat]

The monastery is still an active church and the building is still garrisoned — the Serra do Pilar Artillery Regiment uses part of the complex — but since twenty twenty-two it has also been the headquarters of the Fundação Livraria Lello, the charitable foundation of the family that owns the famous bookshop on Rua das Carmelitas in Porto. The connection between a fourteenth-century fortified monastery on a medieval pilgrimage road and one of the world's most visited bookshops is not immediately obvious, and the foundation doesn't particularly insist on explaining it, which is perhaps the most interesting thing about the arrangement.

tours.longDescription: The ground beneath this monastery has been a place of worship for roughly two thousand years — a Roman temple to Jupiter preceded the medieval church. The Knights Hospitaller, the military order originally tasked with protecting pilgrims to Jerusalem, were given the site in the twelfth century and built it to work: the monastery sits directly on the Portuguese route of the Camino de Santiago, and the fortified tower carries arrow slits and crenellations that were functional, not ornamental. Álvaro Siza's recent contribution — a former storage silo converted into an open fountain where visitors are invited to remove their shoes and stand in it — references the practice of foot-washing before entering a sacred space, present across multiple religious traditions and made present here by the architect, now in his nineties, on one of medieval Europe's great pilgrimage roads. The most charged interior moment came in 1372, when King Fernando I of Portugal married Leonor Teles here in secret, having broken off his engagement to a Spanish princess in circumstances that nearly triggered war between the two kingdoms. The monastery is still an active church and still partly garrisoned by the Serra do Pilar Artillery Regiment — but since 2022 it has also been the headquarters of the Fundação Livraria Lello, the charitable foundation of the family that owns the famous bookshop on Rua das Carmelitas. The connection between a fourteenth-century fortified monastery on a medieval pilgrimage road and one of the world's most visited bookshops is not immediately obvious, and the foundation doesn't insist on explaining it.
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes at the fortified 14th-century monastery on the Portuguese Camino — Knights Hospitaller stronghold, now headquarters of the Fundação Livraria Lello.

tours.title: Livraria Lello

stops.title: Livraria Lello
stops.caption: On Rua das Carmelitas facing the carved-granite facade — Art on the left, Science on the right, and Decus in Labore across the stained glass above.
stops.transcriptText:
The queue outside is for a bookshop — a real one, still operating, still selling books — and that fact is worth holding onto as you wait, because it's easier than you might think to forget once you're inside.
You're on Rua das Carmelitas, and the building in front of you is two floors of carved granite with a large stained-glass window across the top, flanked by two painted figures: Art on the left, Science on the right, and between them across the glass a motto in Latin — Decus in Labore, which translates as Dignity in Work, and which was the Lello brothers' operating principle stated publicly on the front of the building rather than kept as an internal aspiration. When you get to the entrance, look at the whole facade from across the street if you can, because it's only at distance that you can read the figures and the lettering together as a single composed argument.
Go inside when you can, and stop at the foot of the staircase before you move any further.
The staircase is the reason most people are here, and it is extraordinary, but what makes it extraordinary is not what the photographs suggest: it's a forked curve in red that appears to float from the ground floor to the gallery above, the balustrades in carved wood, the whole thing apparently defying the expectation of how much a staircase should weigh, and what you're looking at is the first staircase built in reinforced concrete in Portugal. The engineer — and note that: an engineer, not an architect — was Francisco Xavier Esteves, who designed and built it in nineteen oh six and then concealed the concrete so completely inside the carved ornament that the signature he carved into the base was only discovered during restoration work more than a century after he put it there, which is either a remarkable act of professional modesty or a very long-delayed calling card, depending on how you read it.
Now look up at the ceiling.
The skylight running the full length of the building above you is stained glass, made by Viúva Lamego — Lisbon's oldest stained-glass workshop — and it bears the Lello monogram in the central panels; and the carved arches, the wood-panelled shelves running from floor to ceiling, and the busts of Portuguese writers set into the columns were all designed to produce exactly the feeling you're likely having right now, which is that buying a book here is a more serious act than buying a book anywhere else, which is not an accident but a deliberate piece of commercial architecture.

There is a well-travelled story that J. K. Rowling, who lived in Porto in the early nineteen nineties while teaching English, was inspired by this staircase when she was writing Harry Potter, and the story has been repeated so often that it now functions as part of the building's identity regardless of its accuracy — which is fortunate, because Rowling has publicly denied it, saying she never came inside. The staircase is still the first reinforced concrete staircase in Portugal, still extraordinary, and in no need of the borrowed mythology whatsoever.

tours.longDescription: Livraria Lello is still a working bookshop — that fact is easy to forget once you're inside. Two floors of carved granite on Rua das Carmelitas, a large stained-glass window across the top flanked by painted figures of Art and Science, and a Latin motto across the glass: Decus in Labore — Dignity in Work — the Lello brothers' operating principle stated publicly on the front of the building rather than kept as an internal aspiration. The forked red staircase that floats from ground floor to gallery is the reason most people are here, and it is the first staircase built in reinforced concrete in Portugal — designed in 1906 by engineer Francisco Xavier Esteves, who concealed the concrete so completely inside the carved ornament that the signature he carved into the base was only discovered during restoration more than a century after he put it there. The skylight running the full length of the building is stained glass by Viúva Lamego — Lisbon's oldest stained-glass workshop — and carries the Lello monogram in the central panels. The well-travelled story that J.K. Rowling was inspired by this staircase while writing Harry Potter has been publicly denied by Rowling, who has said she never came inside — but the story has been repeated so often that it now functions as part of the building's identity regardless of its accuracy, which is fortunate, because the staircase is in no need of the borrowed mythology whatsoever.
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes inside Porto's famous 1906 Art Nouveau bookstore on Rua das Carmelitas — the first reinforced concrete staircase in Portugal, hidden inside carved wood.

tours.title: Parque de São Roque

stops.title: Parque de São Roque
stops.caption: Upper terrace at Travessa das Antas — children's playground first, then descend through the camellia garden, boxwood maze and grotto.
stops.transcriptText:
The children playing in front of you are inside a Port wine family's private estate, and they almost certainly have no idea.
The slides and climbing frames occupy the upper terrace of what was, until nineteen seventy-nine, the Quinta da Lameira — a walled garden, vineyard, and hunting lodge that had belonged to the Calém family since seventeen fifty-nine, when they first established themselves here on the proceeds of exporting Port wine down the Douro and out through the mouth of the river. You entered from Travessa das Antas, the upper entrance, which puts you on the terrace that the family would have used as the working agricultural heart of the estate, and the park continues downward through several more levels as you descend the slope — and that is where it gets considerably more interesting.
Head down through the park when you're ready, and take your time on the way.
The garden descending below the playground was designed between nineteen hundred and nineteen eleven by Jacinto de Matos, who was at that point Porto's most prominent landscape designer, and what he built here reflects exactly the kind of ambitions that a prosperous Port wine family would have had in the early twentieth century: around two hundred species of camellia, which still bloom every winter along the stone paths; a formal maze cut from Buxus sempervirens — the same slow-growing boxwood used in the great gardens of northern Europe — whose pattern is perfectly legible from the terrace above and completely disorienting from inside; and a grotto, which is worth a small explanation.
Find the grotto below the maze level, because it rewards a moment's attention.
In the nineteenth century, no properly ambitious romantic garden in Europe was considered complete without an artificial cave containing a lake, which served as a stage set for what garden writers of the period called the agreeable sensation of melancholy — a kind of theatrical dampness, architecturally induced, that was considered both fashionable and philosophically improving. The Calém family had one, which tells you that Porto's Port wine merchants were reading the same design magazines, and absorbing the same ideas about landscape and sensibility, as their counterparts in England and France.

Near the lower entrance there is a chapel that wasn't built here — it was dismantled from its original site at Largo Actor Dias, transported across the city, and reconstructed inside the park in nineteen ninety, which is the kind of logistical undertaking that tends to be invisible once it's done. The city bought the entire estate in nineteen seventy-nine, the playground arrived later, and the grotto, the maze, the camellias, and the two hundred and sixty years of accumulated wealth that shaped all of them were already here long before either.

tours.longDescription: The children playing on the upper terrace are inside what was, until 1979, the Quinta da Lameira — a walled garden, vineyard, and hunting lodge that had belonged to the Calém family since 1759, when they first established themselves on the proceeds of exporting Port wine down the Douro. The garden descending below the playground was designed between 1900 and 1911 by Jacinto de Matos, Porto's most prominent landscape designer of the era. Around 200 species of camellia bloom every winter along the stone paths; a formal maze cut from Buxus sempervirens — the same slow-growing boxwood used in the great gardens of northern Europe — is perfectly legible from the terrace above and completely disorienting from inside; and below the maze sits a grotto, the artificial cave with lake that no properly ambitious romantic garden of the nineteenth century considered itself complete without, designed to produce what garden writers of the period called the agreeable sensation of melancholy — theatrical dampness, architecturally induced, fashionable and philosophically improving in equal measure. Near the lower entrance stands a chapel that wasn't built here: it was dismantled from Largo Actor Dias, transported across the city, and reconstructed inside the park in 1990. The city bought the estate in 1979; the playground arrived later. The grotto, the maze, the camellias, and the 260 years of accumulated wealth that shaped them were already here long before either.
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes through a Port wine family's former private estate — camellia garden, boxwood maze, and a romantic grotto, now a public park in eastern Porto.

tours.title: Pavilhão Multiusos de Gondomar

stops.title: Pavilhão Multiusos de Gondomar
stops.caption: Approaching the main entrance — 520m² of concrete suspended overhead as a single flat plane hovering above the threshold.
stops.transcriptText:
This is not the famous Seeza building — it's the other one, and that distinction is worth holding onto for a moment.
If you've been following this tour, you've already stood at his swimming pools at Leça da Palmeira, his museum at Serralves, and the architecture school at Campo Alegre, all of which appear regularly in the architectural press and on the shortlists that get compiled whenever someone wants to argue about the best buildings in Portugal. This building — a multipurpose arena in Gondomar, five minutes by motorway from the Estádio do Dragão — does not appear on those shortlists, opened in two thousand and seven, holds up to eight thousand four hundred people standing, and is used primarily for sporting events, concerts, and congresses by audiences who are almost certainly thinking about the event rather than the building they're sitting in.
You should be approaching the main entrance, where the first thing worth registering is the canopy.
Five hundred and twenty square metres of concrete, suspended over the forecourt as a single flat plane hovering above the arriving visitor — and Seeza has done something like this before, most conspicuously at the Portuguese Pavilion for Expo ninety-eight in Lisbon, where an entire ceremonial square was covered by a thin pre-stressed concrete slab described at the time as a sheet of paper resting on two bricks. The logic at Gondomar is the same: the cover as arrival, the concrete held in suspension above the threshold, a signal that someone thought carefully about what it means to walk in from the weather and into something significant, even if most of the people doing so are thinking about the football.
Go inside, and look up.
The main nave is elliptical, and what you won't expect in a building of this scale — a building that holds eight thousand people and is wrapped in a double reinforced concrete wall within a steel lattice — is the light: courtyards run along the longitudinal axis of the complex, invisible from the exterior, pulling daylight down into the interior spaces so that a building designed for events that mostly happen in the dark has, nevertheless, been given light from above as a matter of principle rather than necessity, which is the kind of decision that reveals something about what an architect believes buildings are for rather than what they're used for.

The acoustic isolation was engineered from the beginning — the elliptical form distributes sound in ways that a rectangular room wouldn't, and the double concrete wall within the steel lattice keeps the interior separated from the motorway five minutes away — and if the doors close while you're inside, you'll feel that separation very directly. The cities give the major commissions to the famous architects, and the famous architects, it turns out, take the minor commissions just as seriously.

tours.longDescription: This is not the famous Siza building — it's the other one, and that distinction is worth holding onto. The major buildings appear on every shortlist: the swimming pools at Leça da Palmeira, the Serralves museum, the architecture school at Campo Alegre. This multipurpose arena in Gondomar — five minutes by motorway from Estádio do Dragão — does not. Opened in 2007, it holds up to 8,400 people standing, and is used primarily for sporting events, concerts, and congresses by audiences almost certainly thinking about the event rather than the building they're sitting in. The first thing worth registering at the entrance is the canopy: 520 square metres of concrete suspended over the forecourt as a single flat plane hovering above the arriving visitor — the same logic Siza applied at the Portuguese Pavilion for Expo'98 in Lisbon, where an entire ceremonial square was covered by a thin pre-stressed concrete slab described at the time as a sheet of paper resting on two bricks. The cover as arrival, the concrete held in suspension above the threshold, a signal that someone thought carefully about what it means to walk in from the weather and into something significant. The main nave is elliptical, wrapped in a double reinforced concrete wall within a steel lattice, with courtyards running along the longitudinal axis pulling daylight down into the interior — a building designed for events that mostly happen in the dark, given light from above as a matter of principle rather than necessity. The cities give the major commissions to the famous architects, and the famous architects, it turns out, take the minor commissions just as seriously.
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes at Álvaro Siza's Gondomar arena — the lesser-known one — a 520m² floating concrete canopy over the entrance, elliptical interior holding 8,400.

tours.title: New York City Hall
tours.shortDescription: The oldest city hall still in continuous use in the United States — and the story of why its back was built cheaper than its front.
tours.longDescription:
You're in City Hall Park, looking north at the main facade of New York City Hall. The building is small by the standards of nineteenth-century civic architecture — only two stories above a partial basement, no enormous dome, no monumental portico — but the proportions are crisp, the limestone white, and the detailing is meticulous. It was designed by two architects working in unusual collaboration: a French-born émigré named Joseph-François Mangin, who provided the French Renaissance facade and the overall plan, and a New Yorker named John McComb Jr., who handled the engineering and the more restrained Federal-style interior. The cornerstone was laid in 1803, and the building opened in 1812 — it is the oldest city hall still in continuous use anywhere in the United States.

The famous story about this building is what's behind it. When City Hall was being built, the northern edge of New York's developed area was at about Houston Street, twenty blocks south. The architects and the city council assumed nobody would ever view the back — so the south, east, and west sides were faced in expensive Massachusetts marble, but the north facade was originally faced in cheap brownstone. The city kept growing north, and within thirty years the brownstone back was facing as much pedestrian traffic as the marble front. The mismatched facades persisted for over a hundred years, until the entire exterior was re-clad in matching Alabama limestone in the mid-1950s.

City Hall has been the seat of New York government for more than two hundred years. Lincoln lay in state in the rotunda upstairs in April 1865, on his way back to Springfield. The Governor's Room contains portraits of every governor of New York State, along with George Washington's writing desk, and is open to the public on weekday tours. The Tweed Courthouse — named after the corrupt Tammany Hall boss who oversaw its construction — is just behind City Hall, and the Surrogate's Courthouse, with its Beaux-Arts facade, is across Chambers Street to the east.

stops.title: New York City Hall
stops.caption: City Hall Park, Manhattan

tours.title: St. Paul's Chapel
tours.shortDescription: The colonial-era chapel where Washington prayed at his inauguration — and the building that survived September 11 intact.
tours.longDescription:
You're on Broadway, outside St. Paul's Chapel — a small Georgian church in brown stone, with a colonnaded portico, a slim white steeple, and a graveyard wrapping around the sides. The chapel was built in 1766 by the Anglican congregation of Trinity Church, as a "chapel of ease" for parishioners who lived too far from the main Trinity site on Wall Street. It is the oldest surviving church building in Manhattan, and one of the few remaining colonial-era buildings of any kind in lower Manhattan. The architect is traditionally attributed to Thomas McBean, a Scottish student of James Gibbs — the same architect who had designed St Martin-in-the-Fields in London, which St. Paul's was deliberately modeled on.

The chapel has survived two extraordinary events. The first was the Great Fire of 1776, which destroyed about a quarter of New York in the weeks after the British occupied it during the Revolutionary War. The fire burned through the surrounding blocks, including the original Trinity Church a few blocks south, but St. Paul's was spared. Thirteen years later, on April 30, 1789, George Washington walked here from his inauguration at Federal Hall with members of the new Congress, and held the first official religious service of the American presidency in this building. Washington continued to worship here for the two years New York City served as the capital of the new country.

The second extraordinary survival was September 11, 2001. The east edge of the original World Trade Center complex was directly across the street, and when the towers fell, the chapel and its graveyard were buried in dust and debris. Steel and concrete landed in the graveyard; the mature trees absorbed most of the falling debris and shielded the building behind them. When the dust cleared, the chapel was structurally intact, with only one broken window. For the next nine months, St. Paul's became the round-the-clock relief station for the recovery workers at Ground Zero — meals, music, medical care, rest. The chapel earned a nickname: "The Little Chapel That Stood."

stops.title: St. Paul's Chapel
stops.caption: Broadway at Fulton Street, Manhattan

tours.title: The Ansonia
tours.shortDescription: The Beaux-Arts residential hotel where Caruso, Toscanini, and Babe Ruth all lived — and where the owner once kept a bear on the roof.
tours.longDescription:
You're on Broadway, between 73rd and 74th Streets, outside the Ansonia — a seventeen-story Beaux-Arts residential building with twin corner towers, a Parisian mansard roof, and an exuberantly ornamented facade that has earned it the local nickname "the wedding cake of the Upper West Side." The building was constructed between 1899 and 1904, and at the time of its completion it was the largest residential hotel in New York City — three hundred and forty suites, more than five hundred thousand square feet, with the world's largest indoor swimming pool in the basement, Turkish baths, a fountain with live seals in the lobby, and what is generally credited as the first air conditioning system in any New York hotel.

The driving force behind the building was a man named William Earle Dodge Stokes — a Phelps-Dodge copper heir, an early advocate for Broadway as a grand thoroughfare to rival Fifth Avenue, and one of the more eccentric New York millionaires of his generation. Stokes conceived the Ansonia as something close to a self-sufficient utopia, and the rooftop farm was part of that vision: five hundred chickens, several ducks, six goats, a pig named Nanki-Poo, and a small bear. Bellhops collected eggs every morning and delivered them to the tenants. The Department of Health was not amused. In 1907, after residents complained about the smell and the noise, the city ordered the farm shut down — and Stokes and his teenage son spent the night before the inspection moving the animals down the freight elevators and hiding them in the basement.

What made the Ansonia famous in the decades that followed was not the farm but the walls. Stokes had specified extraordinarily thick interior partitions throughout the building — thick enough to make individual apartments effectively soundproof. That single architectural detail made the Ansonia the favored residence of every working musician in New York: Enrico Caruso lived here, Arturo Toscanini lived here, Igor Stravinsky composed here, and the young Plácido Domingo lived here in the early years of his career. Babe Ruth lived here during his Yankees years, Theodore Dreiser wrote here, and Jack Dempsey kept an apartment here. Several members of the Chicago White Sox are said to have met in a suite here in September 1919 to plan throwing the World Series — the Black Sox scandal. The building was converted to residential condominiums in 1992. The bear, the pig, and the seals are long gone.

stops.title: The Ansonia
stops.caption: 2109 Broadway, Upper West Side

tours.title: The Chelsea Hotel
tours.shortDescription: The Victorian Gothic hotel on 23rd Street that housed a century of American literary and artistic life — and two of its most notorious tragedies.
tours.longDescription:
You're on West 23rd Street, outside the Chelsea Hotel — a twelve-story Victorian Gothic building in dark red brick, with wrought-iron balconies running across the entire facade and a small red neon sign reading "HOTEL CHELSEA" above the entrance. The building was completed in 1884, designed by the architect Philip Hubert, and at the time it was the tallest building in New York City. It opened as the first private apartment cooperative in the United States, intended for upper-middle-class families who wanted a more communal alternative to the brownstone houses then dominant on the West Side.

The cooperative model failed within twenty years. The wealthy moved north, the neighborhood declined, and in 1905 the building was converted into a hotel. From that point forward, the Chelsea became the central residence of the American literary and artistic avant-garde for almost a century. Among the writers: Thomas Wolfe wrote "You Can't Go Home Again" in one of the upstairs rooms; Arthur Miller lived here in the 1960s; Arthur C. Clarke wrote "2001: A Space Odyssey" on the top floor. Among the musicians: Bob Dylan wrote "Sad-Eyed Lady of the Lowlands" in one of the rooms; Patti Smith and Robert Mapplethorpe shared an apartment here in the early 1970s; Leonard Cohen wrote "Chelsea Hotel No. 2" about a brief encounter with Janis Joplin somewhere upstairs. Andy Warhol filmed "Chelsea Girls" inside, and Madonna lived here in the 1980s.

Two of the building's most famous moments were tragedies. In November 1953, the Welsh poet Dylan Thomas collapsed after a long night of drinking and died a few days later in a nearby hospital. In October 1978, Nancy Spungen, the girlfriend of Sex Pistols bassist Sid Vicious, was found stabbed to death in their room. Sid Vicious was charged with her murder but died of a heroin overdose four months later, before the case came to trial. In 2011 the building was sold, closed to new guests, and underwent an eleven-year renovation contested repeatedly in court by long-term residents. The Chelsea reopened in stages from 2022 as a luxury boutique hotel. The lobby is open to the public, and the wrought-iron staircase that climbs the central atrium is one of the most distinctive interiors in any New York hotel.

stops.title: The Chelsea Hotel
stops.caption: 222 West 23rd Street, Chelsea

tours.title: Park Avenue Armory
tours.shortDescription: The Gilded Age fortress on Park Avenue — built entirely with private money, with interiors by Tiffany, Stanford White, and the leading designers of the American Aesthetic Movement.
tours.longDescription:
You're at Park Avenue and 66th Street, outside the Park Avenue Armory — a brick-and-granite building that takes up the entire block between 66th and 67th, and that looks, from the outside, like a medieval Italian fortress. It was built between 1877 and 1881 as the Seventh Regiment Armory, designed by the architect Charles W. Clinton, who was himself a veteran of the regiment. The exterior evokes a fortified palazzo from a thirteenth-century Italian city-state — flat surfaces of red pressed brick, granite trim, and three crenellated towers along the Park Avenue side.

The Seventh Regiment of the New York National Guard was, in the late nineteenth century, one of the most socially prestigious militia units in the United States — known as the "Silk Stocking Regiment," with members including Vanderbilts, Roosevelts, Harrimans, and most of the other established old-money families of New York. When the regiment decided to build a new armory in the 1870s, they did not seek state or federal funding — they paid for the entire building themselves by private subscription, making this the only armory in the United States ever built and furnished entirely with private money.

What the regiment did with that money is what makes the building unique. The drill hall is one of the largest unobstructed indoor spaces in New York: fifty-five thousand square feet under an eighty-foot-high barrel-vaulted iron roof, with no internal columns. The reception rooms were commissioned individually from the most prominent designers of the American Aesthetic Movement. The Veterans Room was designed in 1879 by Louis Comfort Tiffany — working with the twenty-seven-year-old Stanford White, the textile designer Candace Wheeler, and the painter Samuel Colman — a dense, eclectic interior of carved wood screens, hammered copper, blue mosaic tile, and stained glass borrowing from Islamic, Celtic, Japanese, and Egyptian sources. Tiffany was thirty-one and had not yet made his famous lamps. The New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission has described the Armory as containing "the single most important collection of nineteenth-century interiors to survive intact in one building" in the United States.

In 2007 the building's military function was retired and it was converted into a cultural center, using the vast Drill Hall for major art installations, concerts, and immersive performances. An ongoing restoration by the Swiss firm Herzog and de Meuron is still in progress. The Veterans Room and several other interiors are open to the public on regular tour days.

stops.title: Park Avenue Armory
stops.caption: 643 Park Avenue, Upper East Side

tours.title: Batalha Centro de Cinema

stops.title: Batalha Centro de Cinema
stops.caption: Praça da Batalha — Art Deco facade with the restored stainless-steel hammer on the lateral bas-relief.

tours.longDescription: Inaugurated 3 June 1947 on Praça da Batalha — and on the same opening night the Estado Novo censors ordered the destruction of a mural in the foyer and the removal of a hammer and sickle from the facade. Artur Andrade's Art Deco design integrated art and sculpture as structural elements rather than applied decoration: the bas-relief panel on the lateral facade by sculptor Américo Braga depicts a labourer with a hammer and a farmer with a sickle. The hammer was destroyed by the censors because, together with the sickle, the regime read it as a Communist motif. The 2022 renovation restored it in stainless steel as a symbol of permanent return. Inside, the foyer murals by Júlio Pomar — one of the most significant Portuguese painters of the twentieth century — depicted festive scenes from Porto's São João celebrations. After Pomar was arrested for his political activities, the regime ordered the murals covered. They stayed under six to eight layers of paint for nearly eight decades, until the team working for Alexandre Alves Costa and Sérgio Fernandez of Atelier 15 began stripping the walls in 2019 and discovered they were still there. The €5m Porto City Council renovation reopened the Batalha in December 2022 with two projection rooms, a film installation space, and a programme of auteur cinema, archive screenings, and cine-concerts. One of the very few cultural venues in Portugal where the censorship history of the building is not merely commemorated but actively present in the spaces where you sit and watch films.
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes on Praça da Batalha — the 1947 Art Deco cinema whose hammer-and-sickle facade was destroyed by the Estado Novo censors, restored eight decades later under six layers of paint.

tours.title: Building in Senhora da Luz

stops.title: Building in Senhora da Luz
stops.caption: Rua da Senhora da Luz, Foz do Douro — exposed-concrete grid on the east and west elevations.

tours.longDescription: Three families live here, one per floor, and if you look at the building carefully enough you can work out which direction each of them faces every morning. The site is in Foz do Douro, where the Douro estuary opens into the Atlantic and where Porto's older merchant class traditionally built their houses to face the water. Souto de Moura completed the building in 2016, following the neighbourhood's constructive logic without apology — narrow facade, deep plan, built to the party walls on either side — then introducing an exact and even grid of exposed concrete on the east and west elevations, repeating across both faces with the kind of precision that requires total control of the formwork, and reading in a street of render, tile, and stone as a declaration rather than a decoration. The plan is where the building's real argument lives: living rooms face west toward the Atlantic, so every sitting room shares the same view of afternoon light and water beyond the rooftops; bedrooms face east toward the quieter back street; and the kitchens and vertical circulation occupy the centre. Every room is placed according to what it needs to face rather than what is most convenient structurally — a distinction that sounds simple until you consider how rarely it is actually followed through. The same instinct visible at the Cantareira Building in Foz do Douro and the Burgo Tower on Boavista: Souto de Moura consistently organising his buildings around which direction matters and then putting the right room there, across every scale from a national office tower to three apartments and a rooftop terrace facing the estuary.
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes in Foz do Douro at a Souto de Moura apartment building — three families, one per floor, organised by which direction each room needs to face.

tours.title: Mosteiro Santo Agostinho da Serra do Pilar

stops.title: Mosteiro Santo Agostinho da Serra do Pilar
stops.caption: Terrace overlooking the Douro and Porto's historic centre — where Wellington spotted the wine barges in 1809.

tours.longDescription: You're standing where Wellington stood on the morning of 12 May 1809, and the problem he was trying to solve from this exact vantage point was not abstract: Marshal Soult's French army held Porto across the river, had destroyed every bridge, and confiscated every boat on the south bank, leaving the Douro between them — tidal and deep and fast — with no obvious means of crossing. From this terrace, or one very close to it, Wellesley spotted four wine barges that local people had hidden upstream before the French could find them, and crossed in broad daylight — thirty soldiers per barge, fully visible to the city on the opposite bank — and the French didn't notice until it was too late. Porto was retaken in a matter of hours. The monastery behind you was started in 1538 and took 132 years to finish, partly for lack of money and partly because Portugal spent a significant portion of that period under Spanish rule. When it was finally completed in 1670 the result was something genuinely unusual: both the church and the cloister are circular, making it the only circular cloister of its kind in Portugal, with a colonnade of 36 Ionic columns running around the cloister perimeter and 72 tombstones paving the floor beneath them — the monks who spent their lives here and are buried in the ground you're walking on. The monastery has been in the hands of the Portuguese army since the Napoleonic Wars, and the Serra do Pilar Artillery Regiment is still garrisoned here. At sunset — which is the reason most people make the climb — face northwest: Porto's historic centre fills the entire view, the cathedral on the hill, the iron arch of the Dom Luís I Bridge below you, the Ribeira waterfront, and the Atlantic somewhere beyond all of it. UNESCO listed.
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes on the terrace where Wellington spotted the wine barges in 1809 — Portugal's only circular cloister, 132 years in the making, with one of the great views over Porto.

tours.title: Teatro Rivoli

stops.title: Teatro Rivoli
stops.caption: Praça Dom João I — Art Deco facade across the square from Porto City Hall.

tours.longDescription: Three different things in one life on this square — a national theatre, a cinema, and a municipal arts centre — and the fact that it still looks like one coherent building is largely the achievement of a single architect given ten years' worth of decisions to make in the 1920s. The Rivoli opened in 1913 as a National Theatre. Changes in the urban centre forced a reconsideration of what the building should be, and in 1923 it was refurbished and adapted as a cinema while retaining a programme of opera, dance, theatre, and concerts, with architect and engineer Júlio Brito signing the project. The site sits on Praça Dom João I, directly across from Porto City Hall — one of the most formally composed public spaces in the city, looking at the seat of municipal power from across a well-maintained set of cobblestones. In 2015 the theatre became the Teatro Municipal do Porto, the city formally assuming permanent responsibility for the building it had been eyeing from across the square for over a century. The Art Deco redesign introduced geometric forms, clean lines, and ornamental details that set it apart from Porto's predominantly neoclassical and baroque cityscape; the concrete construction enabled a spacious auditorium and expansive foyer. The building's golden period came in the 1940s and 50s, when Maria Borges — from a prominent Porto banking and industrial family — took on the theatre as a personal project after her father's death and directed it for more than two decades.
