What remains when you take the palace away — the question Porto's gardeners have been answering since 1951 — is that the gardens were the durable thing all along: the old olive trees, the rose garden on the south terrace, the peacocks that patrol the paths with complete self-assurance, and the views across the Douro that were here before any of the buildings and will outlast the concrete dome too.

stops.title: Jardins do Palácio de Cristal
stops.caption: Get your bearings near the Super Bock Arena dome, then head for the south and west terraces where the views open across the Douro.
stops.transcriptText:
There is no crystal palace here, and there hasn't been since nineteen fifty-one — but the name stayed because people were too attached to it to let it go, which is a reasonable measure of how much the original building meant to the city.
Take a moment to get your bearings: you're in the gardens on the hill above the Douro, west of the city centre, with the concrete dome of the Super Bock Arena visible somewhere to your right or ahead of you — that dome, which replaced the original glass-and-iron palace and has been called a great many things over the years, none of them particularly flattering, is nonetheless the reason the gardens are here at all. The park's main viewing terraces are to the south and west, where the land drops away toward the river and the views open across the Douro toward Vila Nova de Gaia and the bridges, from the Dom Luís I Bridge in the east to the Arrábida Bridge further west — and on a clear day the Douro mouth and the Atlantic are visible beyond them.
The original Crystal Palace was completed in eighteen sixty-five, designed by the British architect Thomas Dillen Jones who took his inspiration directly from the Crystal Palace in Hyde Park — London's great glass exhibition hall from eighteen fifty-one — and it was built specifically to host the International Exhibition of Porto that year, a global event that drew over three thousand exhibitors from around the world and was Porto's way of announcing itself as a city of industrial and commercial ambition on the European stage. The gardens were designed alongside the palace by the German landscape architect Emílio David, and they have outlasted by seven decades everything else that was built at the same time.
Find the small chapel in the gardens before you leave, because it is older than everything around it.
It predates both the palace and the gardens, having been built as a memorial to Carlos Alberto, the King of Sardinia, who came to Porto in eighteen forty-nine after abdicating his throne following the First Italian War of Independence and died here in exile — making Porto's hilltop garden the resting place, at least temporarily, of a deposed king who found the city hospitable enough to die in, which is a particular kind of posthumous compliment. The chapel was here when Thomas Dillen Jones arrived to build his glass palace, and it's still here now that the glass palace has gone.
[beat]
What remains when you take the Crystal Palace away — and this is the question the gardeners of Porto have been answering since nineteen fifty-one — is that the gardens turned out to be the durable thing all along: the old olive trees, the rose garden on the south terrace, the peacocks that wander the paths with a proprietary air that suggests they are entirely aware of their own charm and have no intention of being modest about it, and the views across the Douro that were here before any of the buildings and will be here long after the concrete dome has had its own turn at being demolished.

tours.title: Matosinhos Beach
tours.shortDescription: An 800-metre Atlantic surf beach created by accident — its fine sand a side effect of the Leixões port seawalls — beside Europe's densest concentration of fish restaurants and Janet Echelman's billowing 'She Changes' sculpture.
tours.longDescription:
The Praia de Matosinhos runs along the Atlantic ten kilometres north of central Porto, bookended by the container port and fishing harbour of Leixões to the north and the 17th-century Castelo do Queijo to the south. The beach itself was created by accident: the massive seawalls protecting the Leixões port entrance interrupted the natural coastal erosion, so sand that would have been carried away accumulated instead — producing eight hundred metres of unusually fine, soft sand, noticeably softer than the nearby beaches at Foz.

Matosinhos has fished these waters since at least the 11th century, when it appears in the records as Matesinus, and a thousand years of maritime industry have left it with the highest density of fish restaurants per square metre in Europe — around 600 of them, most concentrated on Rua Heróis de França, where the catch comes off the fishing pier at the end of the same street each morning. At the southern end of the promenade stands She Changes, known locally as Anémona — a fifty-metre work by the American artist Janet Echelman, unveiled in 2004, made of high-tech fibre netting that billows and reshapes in the wind above a traffic roundabout, commissioned to commemorate the city's fishing heritage and now its symbol.

The Atlantic here is powerful enough for serious surfing and forgiving enough for beginners, the metro from central Porto reaches Matosinhos Sul in about twenty minutes, and the lunch question answers itself on Rua Heróis de França — grilled fish that was in the water this morning, at a price only possible in a city with a working fishing fleet two hundred metres from the restaurant door.

stops.title: Matosinhos Beach
stops.caption: Stand on the promenade with Leixões port to the north and Castelo do Queijo to the south; look for Echelman's 'She Changes' sculpture at the southern end.
stops.transcriptText:
The beach you're standing on was created by accident — a consequence of industrial infrastructure that was built to do something else entirely and happened, as a side effect, to produce eight hundred metres of unusually soft sand.
Spend a moment getting the full picture of where you are: the Praia de Matosinhos runs along the Atlantic coast ten kilometres north of Porto's city centre, with the container port and fishing harbour of Leixões forming the northern boundary and the Castelo do Queijo marking the southern edge, so the beach is bookended by a seventeenth-century fort on one side and one of Portugal's busiest industrial ports on the other, which gives it a setting that is less picturesque than most beaches of its quality and considerably more interesting. The massive seawalls that protect the entrance to the Leixões port — extending far out into the Atlantic to shelter the harbour mouth — interrupted the natural pattern of coastal erosion along this stretch of coast, and the sand that would otherwise have been carried away by the current accumulated instead, producing the beach that is now here, with its characteristic fineness that locals note is noticeably softer than the nearby beaches at Foz.
Matosinhos has been fishing these waters since at least the eleventh century, when the area appears in historical records under its ancient name of Matesinus, and the consequence of a thousand years of maritime industry concentrated in one place is that the city now has the highest density of fish restaurants per square metre in Europe — approximately six hundred of them, according to local estimates — with the greatest concentration on Rua Heróis de França, where most of the restaurants source their fish from the fishing pier at the end of the same street every morning, which means the distance between the catch and the plate is measured in minutes and metres rather than hours and kilometres.
Before you leave the beach area, look for the roundabout sculpture at the southern end of the promenade — a fifty-metre-tall work by the American artist Janet Echelman, unveiled in two thousand and four, made of high-tech fibre netting that billows and changes shape in the wind above the road, and which is called She Changes, or locally Anémona, the Sea Anemone.
Echelman was commissioned to create a piece that commemorated Matosinhos's fishing heritage, and what she produced is a structure that moves the way a fishing net moves in water — suspended in the air, responding to the Atlantic wind, never the same shape twice — and it has since become the symbol of the city as thoroughly as any historic monument, which is a considerable achievement for a piece of contemporary public art installed in the middle of a traffic roundabout.
[beat]
The Atlantic here is powerful enough for serious surfing and regular enough for beginners, the metro from central Porto runs to Matosinhos Sul in around twenty minutes, and the lunch question answers itself on Rua Heróis de França, where you can eat grilled fish that was in the water this morning at a price that would be implausible in any city that didn't have a functioning fishing fleet two hundred metres from the restaurant door.

tours.title: Mercado Municipal de Matosinhos
tours.shortDescription: A modernist market hall designed in 1936 but not opened until 1952 — a monumental concrete vault, azulejo panels by Américo Soares Braga, and seventy unbroken years of selling fish and vegetables.
tours.longDescription:
This market won its architectural competition in 1936 with what the judges called an innovative project, but it did not open until 1952 — the Second World War interrupted construction, and iron, which the building needed a great deal of, became impossible to source. On the north facade on Rua Conde São Salvador are azulejo panels by Américo Soares Braga, the sculptor who also made the labourer-and-farmer bas-relief destroyed by Estado Novo censors on the opening night of the Batalha cinema — so two of the most significant modernist buildings in the Porto area share a ceramic artist, and neither's description usually mentions it. It was designed by the ARS practice — Fortunato Cabral, Morais Soares and Cunha Leão — three architects in their thirties.

Inside, the defining gesture is the monumental vaulted ceiling: a long arched concrete span running the full length of the hall, bringing in light from above and giving the interior a civic generosity most municipal markets never achieved. The builder, Luís José Oliveira, came to the contract as a second choice after the original winner withdrew, and formed the arches with a timber framework that slid along rails, advancing through the hall as each section cured. The building also handles its sloping site cleverly: fish on the lower floor, the general market above, and a transversal road between them so vehicles can unload directly into both.

Classified a Monument of Public Interest since 2013, it has operated continuously in its original function since 1952 — avoiding the fate of most modernist markets, conversion into a food hall or cultural venue, by simply continuing to sell fish and vegetables for over seventy years. It became the kind of building that eventually gets protected for doing the ordinary thing it was always doing, rather than for stopping and becoming something more photogenic.

stops.title: Mercado Municipal de Matosinhos
stops.caption: Pause at the entrance on Rua Conde São Salvador to read the azulejo panels on the north facade, then go inside and stand beneath the vault.
stops.transcriptText:
This market was designed in nineteen thirty-six, won its architectural competition with what the judges called an innovative project, and didn't open until nineteen fifty-two — because the Second World War interrupted construction and iron, which the building needed a great deal of, became impossible to source.
Settle yourself at the entrance on Rua Conde São Salvador and look at the north facade before going in, because it carries something worth knowing about: azulejo ceramic panels designed by Américo Soares Braga, the sculptor who also created the bas-relief of the labourer and the farmer that was destroyed by Estado Novo censors on the opening night of the Batalha cinema — which means that two of the most architecturally significant modernist buildings in the Porto metropolitan area share the same ceramic artist, and neither building's description usually mentions it. The building occupies the site of a demolished city block, sits between the Rua Brito Capelo and the avenue running alongside the Leixões port docks, and was designed by the ARS architectural practice — Fortunato Cabral, Morais Soares, and Cunha Leão, three architects in their thirties who won the commission in nineteen thirty-six when they were young enough for a vaulted concrete market hall to seem like a reasonable thing to attempt.
Go inside and stand under the vault.
The monumental vaulted ceiling that covers the interior is the building's defining gesture — a long arched concrete span that runs the full length of the market hall, bringing in natural light from above and giving the interior a civic generosity that most municipal markets of the period never achieved. The builder who constructed it, Luís José Oliveira, arrived at the contract because the original winner withdrew at signing, which meant that the man who built the vault did so as a second-choice contractor working on a pioneering structure, in wartime, with restricted materials — and the method he used to form the arches was a timber framework that slid along rails as each section of the vault was completed, advancing through the hall as the concrete cured.
The building is also unusual in how it handles the sloping site: the lower floor is dedicated entirely to fish, the upper floor to the general market, and a transversal access road between them allows vehicles to unload directly into both floors from the interior, which is a piece of logistics thinking so embedded in the building's structure that you'd only notice it if someone pointed it out.
[beat]
The market has been classified as a Monument of Public Interest since two thousand and thirteen, and it has been operating continuously in its original function since nineteen fifty-two, which means it avoided the fate of most modernist market buildings of its generation — conversion to a food hall, a cultural venue, or a boutique commercial space — by simply continuing to sell fish and vegetables without interruption for over seventy years, and thereby becoming the kind of building that eventually gets protected for doing the ordinary thing it was always doing, rather than for stopping and being converted into something more photogenic.

tours.title: Museu de História Natural e da Ciência da Universidade do Porto
tours.shortDescription: The University of Porto's natural history and science museum in the historic Rectory building — 19th-century specimens of species now extinct, a 120,000-specimen herbarium, and a teaching-instrument collection that surprises everyone.
tours.longDescription:
The museum holds specimens of species that no longer exist anywhere else, gathered from Portugal and its former colonies in the 19th century, when taxonomy was actively discovering the world and before mass extinction was an everyday idea. Its core collections are housed in the Historic Building of the University of Porto's Rectory on Campo dos Mártires da Pátria, beside the Jardim da Cordoaria and within sight of the Clérigos tower. The Rectory has been under extensive refurbishment since 2019, hosting temporary exhibitions rather than the full permanent display — so check what is currently accessible before you go in; its full reinstallation will be one of Porto's most significant museum openings when it arrives.

The collections span geology, palaeontology, zoology, archaeology, ethnography, botany, physical anthropology, numismatics and scientific instruments — not separate museums merged on paper but a genuine accumulation of scientific practice the university has built since the late 19th century. The herbarium alone, assembled largely by the botanist Gonçalo Sampaio, holds an estimated 120,000 specimens of vascular plants, bryophytes, lichens, fungi and algae. The scientific-instruments collection is the section that surprises people most: physics apparatus, mathematical models and chemistry equipment used in teaching across the 19th and 20th centuries, each decade's purchases a precise index of what the institution believed science was.

The museum's second site, the Biodiversity Gallery at Casa Andresen in the Botanical Garden, is open and worth the separate trip — the first university museum in Portugal formally tied to a Ciência Viva science centre. Among its holdings is a whale skeleton referenced by Sophia de Mello Breyner Andresen, who grew up in the house, in writings that predate the museum by decades. The collection and the literary memory of the building arrived separately, across about a century, and ended up in the same room.

stops.title: Museu de História Natural e da Ciência da Universidade do Porto
stops.caption: Stand outside the University Rectory on Campo dos Mártires da Pátria, by the Jardim da Cordoaria; check what is open during the refurbishment before going in.
stops.transcriptText:
This museum holds specimens of species that no longer exist anywhere else, collected from Portugal and its former colonies in the nineteenth century, at a moment in natural history when the discipline of taxonomy was actively discovering the world and before the idea of mass extinction had become an everyday concept.
The building you're outside is the Historic Building of the University of Porto's Rectory, on Campo dos Mártires da Pátria, with the Jardim da Cordoaria — the public garden with the old cordage factory — immediately to your side and the Clérigos tower visible a short distance away. The museum's core collections are housed inside this Rectory building, which has been undergoing extensive refurbishment since two thousand and nineteen, during which it has been hosting temporary exhibitions rather than the full permanent display — so verify what is currently accessible before you go in, as the collection's full installation will be one of the most significant museum openings in Porto when it arrives. The museum's second site, the Biodiversity Gallery at Casa Andresen in the Botanical Garden, is open and worth the separate trip.
Once inside, the breadth of what the museum holds becomes clear rather quickly.
The collections span geology, palaeontology, zoology, archaeology, ethnography, botany, physical anthropology, numismatics, and scientific instruments associated with physics, mathematics and chemistry teaching — not as separate museums that have been merged administratively, but as a genuine accumulation of scientific practice that the University of Porto has been building since the late nineteenth century. The herbarium alone, assembled largely through the work of the botanist Gonçalo Sampaio, contains an estimated one hundred and twenty thousand specimens of vascular plants, bryophytes, lichens, fungi and algae, which is a number that takes a moment to hold: a hundred and twenty thousand individual plant specimens, pressed and catalogued, many of them collected from places and at times when the plants in question were considerably more common than they are today.
The scientific instruments collection is the section that tends to surprise people most.
Physics apparatus, mathematical models, chemistry equipment — all used in teaching at the University of Porto across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, and now objects of historical interest in their own right, because the instruments that a university buys for its students in any given decade are a precise index of what that institution believed science was and what it believed students needed to understand it. The museum also holds maritime charts, photographs, films and manuscripts, which extends its scope from natural history into the broader documentary history of Portuguese scientific observation.
[beat]
The Biodiversity Gallery at Casa Andresen — the second site, in the Botanical Garden on Rua do Campo Alegre — was the first university museum in Portugal to be formally associated with a Ciência Viva science centre, and it holds, among other things, a whale skeleton that was specifically referenced by Sophia de Mello Breyner Andresen, who grew up in the house the gallery now occupies, in writings that predate the museum by several decades. The collection and the literary memory of the building it's in arrived separately, across about a century, and ended up in the same room — which is the kind of institutional coincidence that only becomes legible in retrospect.

tours.title: Parque da Cidade do Porto
tours.shortDescription: Portugal's largest urban park — 83 hectares running all the way to the Atlantic, built partly from the granite of demolished Porto buildings, with sight lines the rest of the city never gives you.
tours.longDescription:
Planned in the 1960s and not built until the 1990s, the Parque da Cidade benefited from the thirty-year gap — by the time it arrived the city had grown large enough to need it properly. It covers 83 hectares, the largest urban park in Portugal, running from the residential edge of Porto's western suburbs all the way to the Atlantic — a park that ends at the ocean rather than a road or fence, which is genuinely unusual. The second phase, completed in 2002, was the extension that closed the gap to the sea. About ten kilometres of paths run through a landscape built partly from the granite of demolished Porto buildings, giving its walls and paving a material continuity with the city.

The landscape architect was Sidónio Pardal, working from 1991 on a concept first drawn up three decades earlier by the French planner Robert Auzelle. Pardal's own description reframes what you are walking through: the landscape, he wrote, is an end in itself and does not attempt to imitate nature, but exists for direct use as a public space for contemplation and recreation. That is a more rigorous position than it sounds — a park that does not imitate nature must find a reason for every stone, path and sight line — and Portugal's Order of Engineers named it one of the hundred most remarkable works built in the country in the 20th century. Near one of the lakes stands the Pavilhão da Água, a water museum built for Expo '98 in Lisbon and relocated here in 2002.

The park is sometimes called Porto's lungs, which undersells it slightly. What really sets it apart is the sight lines — four hundred metres of open ground before you reach treeline, a distance the rest of Porto never offers. The city outside the entrance is intimate, vertical and stone; the city inside is horizontal, open, made of grass and granite and Atlantic light — and the two versions of the same place are different enough that an hour in one changes how you read the other on the way out.

stops.title: Parque da Cidade do Porto
stops.caption: Enter from the eastern, city side and head west toward the lakes and the coast; look for the Pavilhão da Água water museum near one of the lakes.
stops.transcriptText:
This park was planned in the nineteen sixties and not built until the nineteen nineties — and the thirty-year gap turned out to be useful, because it meant the city had grown large enough to need it properly by the time it arrived.
Find your bearings before you walk anywhere: you're somewhere in Portugal's largest urban park, eighty-three hectares of designed landscape that runs from the residential edge of Porto's western suburbs all the way to the Atlantic coastline — and that last part is the detail worth holding onto, because urban parks that end at the ocean rather than a road or a fence are genuinely unusual, and the second phase of the park completed in two thousand and two was specifically the extension that closed the distance between the city and the sea. The main lakes are toward the centre, the Atlantic is to the west, and the paths run for approximately ten kilometres through a landscape that was built partly from the granite stones of demolished Porto buildings, which gives the walls and paving a material continuity with the city that constructed them.
The landscape architect who executed the park was Sidónio Pardal, working from nineteen ninety-one, though the original concept had been drawn up three decades earlier by the French urban planner Robert Auzelle — and Pardal's description of his own approach is worth knowing before you walk through it, because it reframes what you're looking at: the park's landscape, he wrote, is an end in itself and doesn't attempt to imitate nature, but has no purpose other than direct use as a public urban space for contemplation and recreation. That is a more rigorous design position than it sounds, because a park that doesn't attempt to imitate nature has to find a different reason for every stone and every path and every sight line, and Pardal's park was selected by Portugal's Order of Engineers as one of the hundred most remarkable works constructed in the country in the twentieth century on precisely those grounds.
Look for the Pavilhão da Água somewhere within the park, a low building near one of the lakes that functions as a water museum.
It wasn't built here: it was constructed for the Expo ninety-eight in Lisbon, where it stood in the Parque das Nações alongside the Portuguese Pavilion, and was dismantled and relocated to Porto in two thousand and two when the second phase of the park was completed — which means that the building you're walking past has already had a previous life as an exhibition hall at a world's fair, and now stands quietly among the trees as an educational museum about the water cycle, which is either a significant career change or a very logical one, depending on how you think about water.
[beat]
The park is sometimes described as Porto's lungs, which is accurate but undersells it slightly, because the thing that sets it apart from most urban green spaces is the sight lines — four hundred metres of open ground before you reach treeline, which is a distance the rest of Porto never gives you. The city on the other side of the park entrance is intimate and vertical and stone; the city in here is horizontal and open and made of grass and granite and Atlantic light, and the two versions of the same place are different enough that spending an hour in one genuinely changes how you read the other when you come back out.

tours.title: Browers Beato
tours.shortDescription: Just under two minutes inside a microbrewery and restaurant in a converted military power station — Souto de Moura's first Lisbon restaurant interior.
tours.longDescription:
The Browers Beato occupies the former power station of the Manutenção Militar — the Portuguese Army's nineteenth-century logistics headquarters on the eastern waterfront, now the Hub Criativo do Beato, Lisbon's largest creative and innovation complex. The Portuguese army once generated electricity here; now the Super Bock Group brews beer in the same building, an entirely typical sequence of events for the Beato district.

The interior was designed by Eduardo Souto de Moura with Nuno Graça Moura, the original facade and structure preserved while the 700-square-metre space was reconfigured into a three-million-euro microbrewery and restaurant that opened in July 2024. The whole plan turns on a single long counter on the longitudinal axis — public areas to the east, service to the west, and the brewery raised about seventy centimetres above the restaurant floor so the fermentation vessels stay visible over the counter while you eat. It is Souto de Moura's first completed restaurant interior in Lisbon, and the logic of making production visible is more precise spatial thinking than most brewery conversions manage.

stops.title: Browers Beato
stops.caption: Stand inside the Browers Beato, in the old power-station building of the Hub Criativo do Beato. Look at the long counter running the length of the space.
stops.transcriptText:
The Portuguese army used to generate electricity here, and now the Super Bock Group brews beer in the same building — which is, in the specific context of Lisbon's Beato district, an entirely typical sequence of events.
You're inside the Hub Criativo do Beato, Lisbon's largest creative and innovation complex, built on the former Manutenção Militar — the Portuguese Army's logistics and supply headquarters, a vast nineteenth-century industrial complex on the eastern waterfront that was in military use until the twenty-first century. The Browers Beato occupies the old power station building within that complex, designed by Eduardo Souto de Moura and Nuno Graça Moura — the latter a former collaborator of Souto de Moura who was invited as co-author — with the original facade, windows, and relevant structural elements preserved while the seven-hundred-square-metre interior was reconfigured for a three-million-euro microbrewery and restaurant operation run by The Browers Company, a spin-off of the Super Bock Group.
Look at the counter that runs the length of the space.
The long counter on the longitudinal axis is the design decision the whole interior is organised around — public areas to the east, service areas to the west, and the brewery itself raised about seventy centimetres above the restaurant floor on the western side, so you can see the fermentation vessels over the counter while you eat. That seventy-centimetre elevation is not incidental: it's the building making the production visible to the person consuming the product, which is a more precise piece of spatial thinking than most brewery conversions manage.
[beat]
The building opened in July twenty twenty-four, and Souto de Moura's involvement here — his first completed restaurant interior in Lisbon — is the reason to look carefully at the details before ordering, because the decisions that seem obvious in a space like this usually aren't.

tours.title: A Ginjinha
tours.shortDescription: A little over three minutes at a wardrobe-sized bar that has poured sour-cherry liqueur on the same Lisbon square since 1840.
tours.longDescription:
A Ginjinha Espinheira is about the size of a large wardrobe and has been pouring its sour-cherry liqueur continuously since 1840. It was founded by Francisco Espinheira, a Galician immigrant who, on the advice of a friar from the nearby Church of Santo António, made the once-expensive cherry liqueur cheap by infusing morello cherries in aguardente with sugar, cinnamon, and water. When you order you'll be asked one question — com elas ou sem elas, with or without the cherries — and the drink comes deep ruby, sweet, slightly tart, and warming, for around two euros, a price that in real terms hasn't moved far since the nineteenth century.

Step inside if there's room and the Belle Époque glass paintings on the walls carry sayings about the drink's virtues and references to gold medals won in Rio in 1923 and Macau in 1926 — a surprising geographic reach for a liqueur from a tiny bar behind Rossio. Older Lisboetas still mention parents and grandparents prescribing small shots for colds and sore throats, and the bar runs to this day on the same square, in the same building, serving the same recipe made by a descendant of its founder — a continuity several of the grand stone buildings around Largo de São Domingos never managed.

stops.title: A Ginjinha
stops.caption: Find the open-fronted counter on the east side of Largo de São Domingos, just behind Rossio — look for the cluster of people on the pavement holding small glasses.
stops.transcriptText:
The bar selling this drink is about the size of a large wardrobe — and it has been operating continuously since eighteen forty, which makes it one of the smaller institutional arrangements to have lasted nearly two centuries in Lisbon.
Look for the open-fronted counter on the east side of Largo de São Domingos, which is the small square immediately behind Rossio and to the left of the Igreja de São Domingos — you'll find it by the cluster of people standing outside holding small glasses, because there is not enough room inside the bar for more than two or three people to stand at the counter, and the rest of the experience happens on the pavement. The bar's full name is A Ginjinha Espinheira, named after its founder Francisco Espinheira, a Galician immigrant who arrived in Lisbon in the eighteen thirties and opened this specific establishment in eighteen forty after a friar from the nearby Church of Santo António advised him that cherry liqueur, which had previously been an expensive drink available only to wealthier Lisboetas because fermenting it directly from the fruit was slow and costly, could be made more cheaply and quickly by infusing the cherries in aguardente — Portuguese brandy — and adding sugar, cinnamon, and water, a process that produced something the friar apparently considered equivalent and the public considered rather better.
When you step up to order, you'll be asked one question: com elas ou sem elas — with or without.
With or without the cherries, that is, which have been soaking in the liqueur and carry a concentrated hit of it when eaten, a detail that matters more than it sounds at the point of consumption. The drink itself is a deep ruby red, sweet, slightly tart from the morello cherries, and warming from the aguardente, served in a small ceramic cup or a glass depending on the time of day and the preferences of whoever is behind the counter — and it costs approximately two euros, which has not changed dramatically since the nineteenth century in real terms, and which is one of the reasons it has remained a drink for everyone rather than becoming a heritage experience priced accordingly.
Step inside briefly if there's room, and look at the walls.
The interior holds Belle Époque glass paintings bearing sayings about the virtues of the drink — in Portuguese, naturally, and unapologetically promotional — and references to the Gold Medals the liqueur won at the International Exhibition in Rio de Janeiro in nineteen twenty-three and in Macau in nineteen twenty-six, which gives you a sense of the geographic reach that a small sour-cherry liqueur from a wardrobe-sized bar in central Lisbon had achieved by the nineteen twenties.
[beat]
Older Lisboetas will sometimes mention, unprompted, that their parents or grandparents gave them small shots of ginjinha as a cure for colds, sore throats, and a range of other ailments whose treatment by cherry brandy the medical establishment has not formally endorsed but has also not apparently managed to disprove to the satisfaction of the families concerned — and the bar has been running since eighteen forty on this square, in this same building, serving the same drink made by a descendant of Francisco Espinheira to the same mix of locals and visitors, which is a form of institutional continuity that the large stone buildings around Largo de São Domingos have, in several cases, not managed to match.

tours.title: Arco da Rua Augusta
tours.shortDescription: Under two minutes at the triumphal arch closing the Baixa — 114 years in the building, crowned by Glory, Genius, and Valour.
tours.longDescription:
Construction of the Arco da Rua Augusta began in 1759 and finished in 1873 — and in between, still unfinished, it served as a ceremonial gate for the 1862 royal wedding of King Luís I. It stands at the northern end of Praça do Comércio, on the axis of Rua Augusta, where the rebuilt Pombaline Baixa meets the Tagus. Six columns carry four statues by Vítor Bastos — Viriato, Vasco da Gama, the Marquis of Pombal, and Nuno Álvares Pereira — while at the very top the French sculptor Célestin Calmels shows Glory crowning Genius and Valour. A clock was added to the north face in 1941, and a lift reaches an interior Clock Room with a small exhibition on the arch's construction.

The figure of Pombal rewards a moment's attention: he both organised the reconstruction the arch celebrates and was controversial enough — governing by decree, expelling the Jesuits, eventually exiled — that honouring him in his own lifetime would have been unthinkable. Putting him up here in 1873, nearly a century after his death, is the particular Portuguese way with complicated historical figures. Go up to the terrace if you can: the view reads the entire Pombaline grid in one direction and the river in the other, which is exactly what the arch was built to frame.

stops.title: Arco da Rua Augusta
stops.caption: Stand at the northern end of Praça do Comércio, on the axis of Rua Augusta, looking up at the triumphal arch where the Baixa meets the river.
stops.transcriptText:
Construction began in seventeen fifty-nine and the arch was completed in eighteen seventy-three — and in between, it was used as a ceremonial gate for the royal wedding of King Luís the First in eighteen sixty-two, while it was still unfinished, which is either a statement about the occasion or about the pace of the project.
The arch stands at the northern end of Praça do Comércio, directly on the axis of Rua Augusta, where the rebuilt Pombaline Baixa meets the river — and the six columns supporting it carry four statues by Vítor Bastos representing Viriato, Vasco da Gama, the Marquis of Pombal, and Nuno Álvares Pereira, while the figures at the very top, by the French sculptor Célestin Calmels, show Glory crowning Genius and Valour, which is the kind of allegorical confidence that mid-nineteenth-century public monuments rarely resisted. The arch also contains a clock on its north face, installed in nineteen forty-one, and an interior accessible by lift where the Clock Room holds a small exhibition about the building's construction history.
The figure of Pombal on the columns is worth a moment's attention, because he is both the man who organised the reconstruction that the arch was built to celebrate and a figure sufficiently controversial — he governed by decree, eliminated rivals, expelled the Jesuits, and was eventually exiled — that putting him on a triumphal arch in his own lifetime would have been unthinkable, and putting him there in eighteen seventy-three, nearly a century after his death, represents the particular Portuguese relationship with complicated historical figures.
[beat]
Go up to the terrace if you can, because the view from the top reads the entire Pombaline grid of the Baixa in one direction and the Tagus in the other — which is, in a way, exactly what the arch was designed to frame.

tours.title: Belém Tower
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes at the Manueline tower that replaced a warship — UNESCO-listed, and home to Europe's first carved rhinoceros.
tours.longDescription:
The Torre de Belém replaced a thousand-tonne man-of-war, the Grande Nau, that had guarded the Tagus approach until the stone fort was ready. Built between 1514 and 1519 by Francisco de Arruda, it originally sat on its own island midstream; the 1755 earthquake shifted the river enough to close the gap, which is why a causeway now links it to the shore.

Walk the exterior before going in — the stonework is why it became a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Manueline style, named for King Manuel I, carves twisted ropes and cables up the corner columns, armillary spheres in relief, and the cross of the Order of Christ along the battlements. On the river-facing bastion, find the animal gargoyle: it is the earliest surviving sculptural rhinoceros in European art, carved from a description of the live rhino Manuel received in 1515 and later shipped to the Pope, only for it to drown in a wreck before arriving.

Inside is far more austere — five storeys, a spiral stair, a basement dungeon whose tidal watermark shows how far the Tagus once rose. Napoleon's forces dismantled the top two storeys in 1807 to build a gun platform; they were rebuilt in 1845, so the top of the tower is thirty-eight years younger than the bottom, and the consistency of the stonework across that gap is one of the more underappreciated achievements of nineteenth-century Portuguese heritage work.

stops.title: Belém Tower
stops.caption: Stand on the Belém waterfront, on Avenida de Brasília, looking out at the Torre de Belém in the river — with the Jerónimos Monastery on the bank behind you.
stops.transcriptText:
The tower you're looking at replaced a warship — a thousand-tonne man-of-war called the Grande Nau that had been moored in the Tagus estuary, guarding the approach to Lisbon, until the stone fort was ready to take its place.
Picture where you are physically: you're on the western waterfront of Belém, on Avenida de Brasília, and the Torre de Belém stands in the river ahead of you — or rather, nearly so. When it was built, between fifteen fourteen and fifteen nineteen, it sat on its own small island in the middle of the Tagus, accessible only by boat, positioned midstream to command the harbour approach from both directions, with the Jerónimos Monastery visible on the bank behind you. The tower is where it is today — connected to the riverbank by a narrow causeway — because the earthquake of seventeen fifty-five shifted the Tagus enough to close the gap between the island and the shore, which is one of those quietly extraordinary consequences of a disaster that destroyed most of Lisbon above ground while rearranging its waterfront below.
Walk around the exterior before you go in, because the stonework is the reason this building became a UNESCO World Heritage Site and not just a functional fort.
The Manueline style in which Francisco de Arruda built the tower is named after King Manuel I and blends late Gothic with maritime ornament in a way that had no precedent elsewhere in Europe: twisted ropes and cables carved in stone running up the corner columns, armillary spheres — the navigational instruments Portugal's sailors used to plot their position at sea — carved in relief on the surfaces, and the cross of the Order of Christ decorating the battlements, the same military religious order whose funds had supported much of the Portuguese maritime expansion. Now look specifically at the bastion that extends into the water at the base of the tower, and find the animal gargoyle facing the river — because what you're looking at is the earliest surviving sculptural representation of a rhinoceros in European art, carved from a description of the animal that King Manuel had received as a live gift from the Governor of Portuguese India in fifteen fifteen, had displayed in Lisbon to considerable public sensation, and had subsequently packed onto a ship bound for the Pope in Rome, where it died in a shipwreck before arriving.
Inside, the fort is considerably more austere than the exterior suggests — five storeys, a spiral staircase, a dungeon in the basement, the Governor's Chamber and King's Chamber on the upper floors — and the dungeon is worth visiting not for its atmosphere, which is damp and extremely low-ceilinged, but for the tidal watermark on the walls, which shows how far the Tagus rose at high tide when the tower was an island and the dungeon sat below the waterline.
[beat]
Napoleon's forces, during the eighteen oh seven occupation of Lisbon, dismantled the upper two storeys of the tower to build a wooden gun platform in their place, and those storeys were only reconstructed in eighteen forty-five — which means that the top of the tower you're climbing was rebuilt thirty-eight years after the bottom, by a different generation, in a different political context, for a different purpose entirely, and the consistency of the stonework between the original sections and the restored ones is, frankly, one of the more underappreciated achievements of nineteenth-century Portuguese heritage practice.

tours.title: Carmo Archaeological Museum
tours.shortDescription: Three and a half minutes inside the roofless Gothic nave Lisbon chose never to rebuild after 1755 — now Portugal's first archaeological museum.
tours.longDescription:
The roof of the Carmo Convent fell in during All Saints' Day Mass on the morning of 1 November 1755, and Lisbon eventually decided it should stay that way. The convent was founded in 1389 by Nuno Álvares Pereira — the commander whose victory at Aljubarrota secured Portugal's independence — who then renounced his wealth, joined the Carmelites, and built it as thanksgiving. By 1755 it was the largest and grandest church in Lisbon, which made the decision not to rebuild it so significant: a Neo-Gothic reconstruction stalled, was halted by the 1834 dissolution of the religious orders, and was abandoned once it became clear the roofless nave had become a better memorial than any restored building could be.

Through to the sacristy, which survived intact, is the museum — founded in 1864, the first museum of art and archaeology in Portugal. Its collection arrived by donation and rescue: pre-Columbian ceramics, Egyptian sarcophagi, Visigothic buckles, Roman epigraphy, the tomb of King Fernando I, and two upright sixteenth-century Peruvian mummies that most visitors come specifically to see.

In April 1974 the building briefly became a command post for the Carnation Revolution, and the surrender of the dictator Caetano to General Spínola was negotiated within these walls — so this roofless ruin has been, in turn, the largest church in Lisbon, an earthquake memorial, Portugal's first museum, and the place a forty-eight-year dictatorship ended.

stops.title: Carmo Archaeological Museum
stops.caption: Enter through the doorway on Largo do Carmo in Chiado, beneath the roofless Gothic arches of the ruined Carmo Convent.
stops.transcriptText:
The roof fell in during the All Saints' Day Mass on the morning of the first of November, seventeen fifty-five, and Lisbon decided — not immediately, but eventually — that it should stay that way.
The nave entrance is through the doorway on Largo do Carmo in the Chiado district, and the square itself is on a small plateau you'll have reached either by walking up from the Baixa or via the Santa Justa Lift, whose upper bridge connects — through a gated path to the right of the convent entrance that most visitors miss entirely — directly to this square at no extra charge and without the queue that forms at the lift's lower entrance. Once through the main door, the building announces itself immediately: roofless Gothic arches rising fifteen metres above you, the Lisbon sky framed between the stone ribs, and the length of the nave stretching ahead, open to weather and light in a way that no functioning church ever would be.
The convent was founded in thirteen eighty-nine by Nuno Álvares Pereira — the military commander whose victory at the Battle of Aljubarrota in that same year secured Portugal's independence from Castile, and who subsequently renounced his wealth, joined the Carmelite Order, and built this convent on the hill across from the São Jorge Castle as an act of thanksgiving — and at the time of the seventeen fifty-five earthquake it was the largest and grandest church in Lisbon, which is what made the decision not to rebuild it so significant.
When reconstruction was attempted the following year in a Neo-Gothic style, it stalled, was halted by the dissolution of the religious orders in eighteen thirty-four, and was eventually abandoned not through indifference but through the realisation that the roofless nave had become something that a rebuilt roof would destroy — a space where the architecture and the event that shaped it were the same thing, visible simultaneously, and which served better as a memorial than as a restored building.
Go inside and through to the sacristy, which survived the earthquake intact and now houses the museum collections.
The Carmo Archaeological Museum, founded in eighteen sixty-four, was the first museum of art and archaeology in Portugal, and its collections arrived through a combination of deliberate donation and rescue — objects saved from other buildings damaged in the earthquake, pre-Columbian ceramics, Egyptian sarcophagi, Visigothic buckles, medieval tombstones, Roman epigraphy, and the tomb of King Fernando the First, which is the largest tomb in the collection. The two Peruvian mummies from the sixteenth century, which stand upright in glass cases in the museum's interior rooms, are the objects most visitors come specifically to see, and they are genuinely strange — preserved in a technique that has kept them intact for five centuries while everything around them in the earthquake-damaged church has long since been consolidated, restored, or left as ruins.
[beat]
The convent became briefly famous in April nineteen seventy-four, when the Portuguese military units that staged the Carnation Revolution used the building as a command post during the coup — and the surrender of the secret police director Caetano to General Spinola was negotiated from within these walls, which means this roofless Gothic ruin has been, in its history, the largest church in Lisbon, an earthquake memorial, Portugal's first archaeological museum, and the location where a forty-eight-year dictatorship ended.

tours.title: Castelo de São Jorge
tours.shortDescription: Under two minutes at the hilltop castle every power in Lisbon's history fortified — Roman, Visigoth, Moor, and Portuguese.
tours.longDescription:
Every significant power in Lisbon's history built on this hill — Romans, Visigoths, Moors, Portuguese — less from cultural continuity than from geography: it is the highest, most defensible point in the city. The walls date largely from the eleventh-century Moorish occupation, expanded after King Afonso Henriques took the fortress in 1147 with the help of Northern European crusaders passing through on their way to Jerusalem. It then served as a royal residence for three centuries, until the court moved to the waterfront Ribeira Palace in the sixteenth, and what you walk today is partly medieval and partly a substantial twentieth-century restoration that rebuilt more than it found.

Near the keep, look for the archaeological site: excavations uncovered a Moorish neighbourhood — houses, streets, everyday objects — continuously occupied from before the Romans through every later conquest, the layers in the ground a more complete account of Lisbon than any wall above them. The peacocks that roam the grounds arrived at some point and decided to stay, which makes them, in that one respect, indistinguishable from every other occupying force this hill has received in two thousand years.

stops.title: Castelo de São Jorge
stops.caption: Arrive at the main entrance on Rua de Santa Cruz do Castelo in Alfama — the highest point in the city, reachable by the number 28 tram.
stops.transcriptText:
Every significant power in Lisbon's history built something on this hill — the Romans, the Visigoths, the Moors, the Portuguese — which is less a matter of cultural continuity than of simple geography, because this is the highest point in the city, and the highest point is always the most defensible.
The castle is in the Alfama district, reached from the Baixa by the number twenty-eight tram or by a fairly sustained climb through the oldest streets in the city, and when you arrive at the main entrance on Rua de Santa Cruz do Castelo, the walls you're looking at date largely from the Moorish occupation of the eleventh century, expanded and modified after King Afonso Henriques captured the fortress from the Moors in eleven forty-seven with the help of Northern European crusaders who were passing through Lisbon on their way to Jerusalem. The castle then became a royal residence for three centuries, housing Portuguese kings from the thirteenth until the sixteenth, when the court moved to the Ribeira Palace on the waterfront — and what you're walking around today is partly medieval, partly the result of a significant twentieth-century restoration that rebuilt more than it found.
Inside, look for the archaeological site near the castle keep.
Excavations there uncovered a Moorish neighbourhood — houses, streets, and everyday objects — that had been continuously occupied from before the Romans and through every subsequent conquest, and the layers visible in the ground represent a more complete account of Lisbon's history than any of the walls above them.
[beat]
The peacocks that roam the castle grounds arrived at some point and appear to have decided to stay, which makes them, in that specific respect, indistinguishable from every other occupying force this hill has received over the past two thousand years.

tours.title: Centro de Arte Moderna Gulbenkian (CAM)
tours.shortDescription: Three and a half minutes at the Gulbenkian's modern-art museum — Kengo Kuma's first Portuguese building, born of one oil magnate's will.
tours.longDescription:
Calouste Gulbenkian was an Armenian oil magnate who arrived in Lisbon during the Second World War to escape the Nazis, never left, and on his death in 1955 left his entire fortune and art collection to Portugal — a country he had lived in for only eleven years. Everything here follows from that. The Foundation's eighteen-acre woodland campus opened in 1969; the Centro de Arte Moderna was added in 1983 to house Portuguese modern and contemporary art.

The long curving canopy sweeping from the building into the garden — 107 metres long, surfaced in 3,274 white azulejo tiles above and overlapping ash timber below — was completed in September 2024 and is Kengo Kuma's first finished building in Portugal. The 1983 building had been described, candidly, in the renovation brief as 'an imposition' on the garden; Kuma answered with the engawa, the sheltered transitional walkway of traditional Japanese houses, so the canopy reads as a gradation rather than a threshold.

Inside, the collection runs to nearly twelve thousand works, strongest in Portuguese art from the 1900s on — including Paula Rego's dark figurative work — alongside international figures such as Robert Delaunay. The Foundation has become one of Europe's most significant philanthropic institutions, and the freely accessible campus is the most visible expression of what one decision, made in a Lisbon hotel room in 1955, set in motion.

stops.title: Centro de Arte Moderna Gulbenkian (CAM)
stops.caption: Stand on the Gulbenkian Foundation campus in northern Lisbon, by the long curving canopy that sweeps from the modern-art museum out into the garden.
stops.transcriptText:
Calouste Gulbenkian was an Armenian oil magnate who died in Lisbon in nineteen fifty-five and left his entire personal fortune — his art collection, his assets, and his estate — to Portugal, a country he had lived in for only eleven years, having arrived during the Second World War to avoid the Nazis and simply never left.
Everything you're about to see is the consequence of that decision. You're on the Gulbenkian Foundation campus in northern Lisbon, an eighteen-acre woodland park that opened in nineteen sixty-nine with a headquarters building and museum designed by Portuguese architects Ruy d'Athouguia, Alberto Pessoa, and Pedro Cid, and with gardens laid out by landscape architect Gonçalo Ribeiro Telles — and the Centro de Arte Moderna, which sits in the southern part of those gardens and is the building you're here for, was added in nineteen eighty-three to house a separate collection of modern and contemporary Portuguese art. The canopy you can see extending from the building into the garden — a long curving wooden and ceramic structure sweeping out over the path between the museum and the trees — was completed in September twenty twenty-four and represents Kengo Kuma's first completed building in Portugal.
Walk under the canopy before you go inside, because the structure is the argument the renovation is making.
"The original nineteen eighty-three building by Leslie Martin was described, in the brief for the twenty twenty-four renovation competition, as having become ""an imposition"" on the garden rather than a part of it — which is an unusually candid thing for an institution to say about one of its own buildings — and Kengo Kuma's response was to draw inspiration from the engawa, the sheltered transitional walkway that runs around the perimeter of traditional Japanese dwellings and belongs neither entirely to the interior nor entirely to the outdoors. The canopy is a hundred and seven metres long and fifteen metres wide, rising from two metres at the garden's edge to ten metres at the building facade, so it is a gradation rather than a threshold — you pass under it and through it and the building's edge becomes ambiguous, which is, in Kuma's own articulation, the point. The canopy roof is surfaced in three thousand two hundred and seventy-four square white azulejo tiles and the underside in overlapping ash timber, the ceramic above and the wood below each responding to a different Portuguese material tradition."
Now go inside, and give the collection the time it deserves.
The museum holds nearly twelve thousand works, with the permanent collection focusing on Portuguese modern and contemporary art from the nineteen hundreds onwards, and among the most significant holdings are works by Paula Rego, whose dark figurative work you'll have encountered if you've visited the Casa das Histórias in Cascais, alongside international figures including Robert Delaunay, whose relationship with the Lisbon art world through his wife Sonia Delaunay gave the collection a connection to European modernism that most Portuguese institutions of the same period lacked.
[beat]
The foundation Gulbenkian created in his will has since become one of the most significant philanthropic institutions in Europe — funding art, science, education, and civic projects across Portugal and internationally — and the campus you're standing in, freely accessible to the public as a park on weekdays and weekends alike, is the most visible expression of what a single person's decision, made in a Lisbon hotel room in nineteen fifty-five, set in motion.

tours.title: EDP Sede II
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes at EDP's second headquarters — Pritzker laureate Alejandro Aravena's civic-minded concrete cantilever over the waterfront.
tours.longDescription:
Alejandro Aravena is the Chilean architect who won the Pritzker in 2016 for half-built social housing residents could complete themselves — so EDP, one of Portugal's largest corporations, commissioning him for a headquarters deserves a moment's thought. This building, completed in 2024, is the second of two EDP headquarters on the same waterfront campus, sitting on the second row of the grid directly across a public square from the Aires Mateus towers of 2016. Two glass volumes are sandwiched between monolithic board-formed concrete slabs.

The entrance is placed on the south side rather than facing the square — a deliberate decision, in Aravena's team's words, so that a citizen does not feel a company is doing them the favour of using a quality space. The slabs were designed to erase the notion of floors, reading as civic monumentality rather than corporate facade, and the building folds the ground of the public square to keep a north-south pedestrian route while connecting the two volumes beneath it.

It also cantilevers toward the river, gaining enough height to lean over the Aires Mateus building and win a clear view of the Tagus from the second row — an assertive solution from an architect better known for restraint, and a reminder that the river view from inside is the whole reason to build on this stretch of waterfront at all.

stops.title: EDP Sede II
stops.caption: Stand on the public square on Avenida 24 de Julho, between the two glass volumes of the second EDP headquarters — across from the Aires Mateus towers.
stops.transcriptText:
Alejandro Aravena is the Chilean architect who won the Pritzker Prize in twenty sixteen for designing social housing for communities who couldn't afford to build the whole house — so they built half, and left the other half for the residents to complete when they could afford to. That EDP, one of Portugal's largest corporations, commissioned him to build their headquarters is a conjunction that deserves a moment's thought.
The building you're looking at is the second of two EDP headquarters on the same waterfront campus, directly across a public square from the Aires Mateus towers completed in twenty sixteen — the two buildings forming a pair that EDP commissioned separately, a decade apart, from two very different architectural sensibilities. This one was completed in twenty twenty-four, and it sits on the second row of Lisbon's urban grid, set back from the river behind the first EDP building, on Avenida Vinte e Quatro de Julho. The two glass-fronted volumes are sandwiched between monolithic concrete slabs — substantial, horizontal bands of board-formed concrete that run the full width of each volume — and the square between this building and the one across the street from it is public, accessible to anyone, with a café at ground level and the building's own entrance placed on the south side rather than facing the square.
That last detail — where the entrance is — is the building's most explicit civic statement, and Aravena's team were precise about it: it was a deliberate decision not to put the access in the square so that a citizen does not feel that a company is doing them the favour of using a quality space.
Now look at the concrete slabs, and then look at the square they create between the two volumes.
The slabs were designed, in Elemental partner Juan Cerda's own words, to evoke timelessness — by erasing the notion of floors, you feel and see civic monumentality rather than the language of a corporate office building, which is a specific architectural ambition that requires considerable restraint to achieve, because the instinct in a headquarters building is almost always to assert the company's identity through the facade rather than to erase it. The building also had to solve what Aravena described as a paradox: the masterplan required north-south continuity of public space through the campus, which meant splitting the building into two volumes, while the building itself needed to function as a single entity requiring east-west connection between those volumes — and the solution was to fold the ground of the public space with a slight slope that maintains the north-south pedestrian route while connecting the two volumes beneath the highest point of that folded ground.
[beat]
The building also cantilevers toward the river, gaining enough height to lean over the Aires Mateus building across the street and achieve a clear view of the Tagus from what is technically the second row of the urban grid — which is the kind of problem that sounds purely geometric until you realise that the view of the river from inside a building is not a luxury detail but the primary reason to build on this particular stretch of waterfront at all, and that solving it required a concrete cantilever projecting over someone else's building, which is an assertive solution from an architect better known for restraint.

tours.title: Graça Funicular
tours.shortDescription: Ninety seconds on Lisbon's newest funicular, opened in 2024 on a line first built in 1893 and abandoned for 120 years.
tours.longDescription:
The Ascensor da Graça opened in 2024 — one of Lisbon's newest pieces of public infrastructure — but it runs on a line first built in 1893, decommissioned in 1904 when a tram made it seem redundant, and then left without a replacement for a hundred and twenty years. The lower station is on Rua dos Lagares at the edge of Mouraria; the upper connects to the Miradouro da Graça.

For such a short line the engineering was significant: to anchor it, Atelier Bugio dug a basement shaft more than nine metres down through the geological and archaeological layers Lisbon's hills accumulate. The ride is seventy-four metres of track at a 31-degree gradient, climbing forty-four metres in ninety seconds, passing through the Jardim da Cerca da Graça where the design frames the greenery rather than blocking it — four distinct architectural moments in a journey that's over before most people have put their phone away. The yellow cars match Lisbon's historic funiculars, carry fourteen passengers, and accept bikes and dogs.

stops.title: Graça Funicular
stops.caption: Board at the lower station on Rua dos Lagares, at the edge of Mouraria — the line climbs to the Miradouro da Graça at the top of the hill.
stops.transcriptText:
The funicular you're about to board opened in twenty twenty-four — which makes it one of Lisbon's newest pieces of public infrastructure — but it's running on a line that was first built in eighteen ninety-three, decommissioned in nineteen oh four when a tram route made it seem redundant, and then left without a replacement for a hundred and twenty years.
The lower station is on Rua dos Lagares, at the edge of Mouraria, and the upper station connects to the Miradouro da Graça at the top of the hill — and if you're wondering why such a short line required significant engineering, the answer is visible once you're in the lower station: to anchor the structure on this site, the architects at Atelier Bugio had to dig a basement shaft more than nine metres into the ground, navigating geological and archaeological layers that Lisbon's hills tend to accumulate over two thousand years of continuous occupation. The journey itself is seventy-four metres of track at a thirty-one degree gradient, overcoming a forty-four metre elevation change, and takes ninety seconds.
On the way up, the funicular passes through the Jardim da Cerca da Graça, where the design team chose to work with an existing high retention wall rather than against it, framing the garden's greenery rather than blocking it — so the ninety-second ride offers four distinct architectural moments rather than a single sustained view, which is an unusual amount of spatial variety to fit into a journey that is over before most people have put their phone away.
[beat]
The cars are painted yellow to match Lisbon's historic funiculars, carry fourteen passengers, and accept bikes and dogs, which suggests that Lisbon's approach to public transport has, in at least some respects, moved in a reasonable direction since nineteen oh four.

tours.title: Jardim Botânico da Ajuda
tours.shortDescription: Three and a half minutes in Portugal's oldest botanical garden — built in 1768 beside the wooden palace a king preferred after the earthquake.
tours.longDescription:
This garden was built next to a wooden palace: after the 1755 earthquake the king refused to sleep under a stone roof, so Pombal had a timber residence raised on the hill above the ruins. Completed in 1768 by the Italian naturalist Domingos Vandelli, it is the oldest botanical garden in Portugal — a two-level geometric design, the upper terrace of ponds and tree collections offering a clear view over the Tagus toward the 25 de Abril bridge without a museum admission, the lower a baroque box garden around a central fountain.

That fountain, the Fonte das Serpentes — the Fountain of Forty Nozzles — carries serpents, winged fish, seahorses, frogs, and mythological figures, dedicated to Nossa Senhora da Ajuda. The garden was built both as a pleasure ground for royal grandchildren and as Portugal's first museum-nursery for botanical species, with expeditions to Angola, Brazil, and Cape Verde bringing back seeds whose study informed how the Crown managed its territories. On the upper terrace, look for the dragon tree — a Dracaena draco already growing here when Vandelli arrived, estimated at four hundred years old, which has outlived the wooden palace, the French invasion, the monarchy, and the Republic that replaced them.

In 1808 Napoleon's troops carried more than fifteen hundred specimens off to Paris, a loss the collection never fully recovered from, and the garden wasn't opened to the public until 1910. What you walk today is the result of a 1994–97 restoration aiming to restore its botanical origins while preserving the spirit of scientific enquiry the explorers brought home in their specimen cases.

stops.title: Jardim Botânico da Ajuda
stops.caption: Climb Calçada da Ajuda from the direction of Belém; the formal garden gateway is on your left, leading into a two-level geometric garden above the Tagus.
stops.transcriptText:
This garden was built next to a wooden palace — because after the seventeen fifty-five earthquake, the king refused to sleep under a stone roof, and Pombal had a timber residence constructed for the royal family on the hill above the ruins, which is the kind of administrative decision that tends to produce unexpected consequences.
Calçada da Ajuda runs uphill from the direction of Belém, and the garden entrance is on the left as you climb — a formal gateway leading into a two-level geometric garden that you'll have to yourselves on quieter mornings, since it remains considerably less visited than the Jerónimos and the Tower below despite being the oldest botanical garden in Portugal and one of the few places in Lisbon where the upper terrace offers a clear view over the Tagus toward the Ponte Vinte e Cinco de Abril without charging a museum admission price for the privilege. The garden was completed in seventeen sixty-eight by Domingos Vandelli, an Italian naturalist from Padua commissioned by Pombal, who designed the upper level around geometric ponds and tree collections and the lower level as a baroque box garden with a central fountain, nurseries, and herbs.
Walk down to the lower level first, and find the fountain at its centre.
The Fonte das Serpentes — the Fountain of Forty Nozzles — is decorated with carvings of serpents, winged fish, seahorses, frogs, and mythological figures, and dedicated to Nossa Senhora da Ajuda following a local tradition of apparitions in the area, which means the garden's central water feature combines natural history iconography with Marian devotion in the specific way that eighteenth-century Portuguese royal commissions tended to do. The garden was originally built with two purposes: as a pleasure ground and place of learning for the royal family's grandchildren, but also as the first institution in Portugal specifically designed to act as a museum and nursery for botanical species collected from around the world — with expeditions sent to Angola, Brazil, and Cape Verde to bring back seeds and plants whose study informed Portuguese understanding of the territories the Crown was attempting to manage.
Now go back up to the upper terrace and look for the dragon tree on your way.
The oldest living thing in this garden is not a plant that arrived in one of those eighteenth-century expeditions but a dragon tree — a Dracaena draco — that was already growing on this hillside when Vandelli arrived to design the garden around it, and is estimated to be four hundred years old, which places its germination somewhere around the early seventeenth century and means it has outlived the wooden palace, the French invasion, the monarchy, and the Republic by which all of those things were eventually replaced.
[beat]
In eighteen oh eight, Napoleon's troops took more than fifteen hundred botanical specimens from this garden to Paris, a loss from which the collection never fully recovered — and the garden was not opened to the public until nineteen ten, after the establishment of the Portuguese Republic, having spent its first hundred and forty-two years as an exclusively royal and scientific resource. What you're walking through today is the result of a nineteen ninety-four to nineteen ninety-seven restoration that aimed to restore the garden's botanical origins while preserving the spirit of scientific enquiry that the Portuguese explorers brought back in their specimen cases, which is a more precise institutional ambition than most gardens have thought to articulate.

tours.title: Jardim do Torel
tours.shortDescription: Under two minutes at a quiet hillside garden that looks straight across Avenida da Liberdade at the viewpoint opposite.
tours.longDescription:
The Jardim do Torel sits at the top of the Santana hill and looks directly at another viewpoint — the Miradouro de São Pedro de Alcântara on the São Roque hill opposite — the two facing each other across Avenida da Liberdade with the mutual awareness of things that have been looking at each other a long time. Reach it by the Elevador do Lavra, Lisbon's oldest funicular, which stops just below, or by climbing Rua do Telhal. Once a private estate named for the Visconde de Torel, it only opened to the public in the 1960s and stays far quieter than most Lisbon viewpoints at the same height, despite a direct view over the Baixa, the full length of the avenue, and the Tagus beyond.

The garden splits in two: an upper section shaded by mature trees, and a lower terrace with a café, a pond that becomes a small swimming pool in summer, and a bust of the pianist Vianna da Mota, one of the most celebrated Portuguese musicians of the late nineteenth century, who on the evidence of his placement here spent the afterlife with a reasonably good view. The aristocratic mansions around the garden declined over the twentieth century; two have since reopened as the Torel Palace Hotel.

stops.title: Jardim do Torel
stops.caption: Stand at the viewpoint atop the Santana hill — reached by the Elevador do Lavra or by climbing Rua do Telhal from Avenida da Liberdade.
stops.transcriptText:
The viewpoint you're standing at looks directly at another viewpoint — the Miradouro de São Pedro de Alcântara on the São Roque hill opposite — and the two of them face each other across Avenida da Liberdade with the particular mutual awareness of things that have been looking at each other for a long time.
The garden occupies the top of the Santana hill and is reached either via the Elevador do Lavra — Lisbon's oldest funicular, which stops immediately below — or by climbing Rua do Telhal from the direction of Avenida da Liberdade. Originally a private estate, named after the Visconde de Torel whose family owned it, the garden only opened to the public in the nineteen sixties and remains considerably less visited than most Lisbon viewpoints at the same altitude, despite having a direct view over Baixa, the full length of Avenida da Liberdade, and the Tagus beyond it on clear days.
The garden splits into two levels, with the upper section shaded by mature trees and the lower terrace holding a café, a pond — in summer converted to a small pool where people actually swim — and a bust of the pianist Vianna da Mota, who was one of the most celebrated Portuguese musicians of the late nineteenth century and who, on the evidence of his placement here, spent the afterlife with a reasonably good view.
[beat]
The aristocratic mansions surrounding the garden fell into disrepair over the twentieth century and two have since been restored as the Torel Palace Hotel — so the hill that declined and the garden that somehow didn't decline together now occupy the same hilltop, which is an accurate description of how Lisbon tends to manage its heritage when nobody is paying close enough attention to get in the way.

tours.title: Jerónimos Monastery
tours.shortDescription: Nearly four minutes at the Manueline monastery a pepper tax built — Vasco da Gama's tomb, an empty king's sarcophagus, and the EU's Treaty of Lisbon.
tours.longDescription:
The Jerónimos Monastery was funded by a pepper tax — a five percent levy on the spice trade, with pepper, cinnamon, and cloves pointedly exempted so the Crown could keep that income. Vasco da Gama kept vigil on this exact ground before sailing for India in 1497; on his successful return, King Manuel I petitioned the Pope to build a monastery in thanks, broke ground on 6 January 1501, and paid for it with the wealth the voyage made possible. It took over a century and four principal architects to finish, in 1600.

Before the south portal, stand back and read the sculpture — designed by João de Castilho, who took over in 1517, it is one of the masterpieces of Manueline stone carving, saints and explorers and maritime motifs and Manuel's armillary sphere carved at a density without precedent in Portugal. Inside, the two-storey cloister of 1544 is among the finest spaces in the country, and its quiet marvel is that no two columns are decorated alike — an encyclopaedia of Manueline ornament you can read column by column.

In the church find three tombs: Vasco da Gama on the right, the poet Luís de Camões on the left, and between them an empty carved sarcophagus for King Sebastian, who led fifteen thousand men into Morocco in 1578, lost at Ksar El Kebir, and whose body was never recovered — so his tomb has stood empty for 447 years. In 2007 the Treaty of Lisbon, the founding reform document of the European Union, was signed here, in a building funded by the trade routes that first connected Europe to Asia and Africa.

stops.title: Jerónimos Monastery
stops.caption: Stand on Praça do Império in Belém, facing the long south facade of the Mosteiro dos Jerónimos — the south portal to your right, the western entrance further left.
stops.transcriptText:
The building you're about to enter was funded by a pepper tax — specifically a five percent levy on the spice trade, known as the Vintena da Pimienta, but with pepper, cinnamon, and cloves deliberately exempted because those were the most valuable spices and the Crown wanted to keep that income for itself.
Everything here begins on this ground: you're on Praça do Império in Belém, with the south facade of the Mosteiro dos Jerónimos directly facing you — a horizontal expanse of golden Lioz limestone that stretches nearly three hundred metres east to west along the waterfront, with the south portal at your right as you face the building and the western entrance further left. Vasco da Gama and his crew spent the night in vigil on this exact spot, at the small hermitage that stood here before the monastery, before departing for India in fourteen ninety-seven — and when he returned successfully, King Manuel I petitioned the Pope for permission to build a great monastery in thanks, began construction on the sixth of January fifteen oh one, and funded it with the wealth that the voyage had made possible. The building took over a century to complete, passing through the hands of four principal architects, and was finally finished in sixteen hundred.
Before you go through the south portal, stand back from it and read the sculpture.
The portal, designed by João de Castilho, who took over from the original architect Diogo de Boitaca in fifteen seventeen, is considered one of the masterpieces of Manueline stone carving in Portugal: saints, explorers, maritime motifs, royal symbols, and the armillary sphere that was King Manuel's personal emblem are all carved into the stone archway at a density of detail that has no precedent in Portuguese architecture before it. The Manueline style — named after Manuel I, blending Gothic structure with Renaissance decoration and maritime ornament — was invented, in a meaningful sense, in the process of building this monastery, as successive architects pushed the decorative programme further with each new section they completed.
Go through the south door and straight to the cloister.
