stops.title: M+ Museum | M+博物館
stops.caption: Stand with Victoria Harbour behind you, facing the tower and its LED facade.
stops.transcriptText:
Look at the tower. The entire harbour-facing wall is a screen.

Stand with Victoria Harbour behind you and the building's main facade ahead: two volumes — a long, flat horizontal podium running left to right, and a slender eighteen-storey tower rising from its centre — intersecting to form an inverted T. The tower's harbour-facing side is covered in sixty-six metres of LED panels, which after dark display artworks, images, and moving image sequences visible from across the water. The architects — Swiss firm Herzog & de Meuron, working with Hong Kong studio Farrells — described this as a deliberate retort to the commercial billboard culture of the Kowloon skyline: a cultural institution inserting itself into the visual noise of the harbour on the city's own terms.

M+ opened on the twelfth of November twenty twenty-one as Asia's first museum dedicated to contemporary visual culture — covering visual art, design and architecture, and moving image, with a collection drawn from Hong Kong, greater China, Asia, and the wider world. The building holds thirty-three galleries across sixty-five thousand square metres, and the collection runs to around eight thousand works. The largest single component is the Sigg Collection: one thousand five hundred and ten works of contemporary Chinese art donated by Uli Sigg, a Swiss diplomat and collector who assembled what became one of the most significant collections of twentieth and twenty-first century Chinese art outside of China, and gave it here.

Worth knowing, standing in front of it: M+ opened while Hong Kong's National Security Law was already in force, and was described at the time as a litmus test for what a cultural institution in Hong Kong could show and say. That question has not entirely resolved itself, and the museum operates in a context that gives its existence a weight that purely aesthetic institutions rarely carry. Nearly three million Hong Kongers registered to visit in the first eighteen months — during which international travel restrictions meant the museum was experienced almost entirely by the city it was built for, rather than the world it was built to address.

The West Kowloon waterfront promenade runs east from the museum toward the Avenue of Stars — the harbour walk is good at any hour, and the view back toward Hong Kong Island from the water's edge is worth the walk.

tours.title: Murray Building | 美利大廈
tours.shortDescription: A 1969 modernist government tower with windows angled to keep the sun out — Ron Phillips's passive-solar landmark, now the Murray hotel.
tours.longDescription:
There's a curious thing about this building — it received an environmental award in 1994 for design decisions made in 1969. Face the Cotton Tree Drive facade: a twenty-seven-storey tower in white concrete, sitting above a row of eleven-metre arches at street level, with deep-set windows arranged in a precise geometric grid. The windows are not square-on to the street — each is angled to deflect direct sunlight away from the interior, a passive solar strategy that Ron Phillips embedded into the structure at a time when air conditioning was becoming standard and almost no one was thinking about reducing the need for it. The effect is that the building reads differently depending on where you stand and the hour you visit.

Phillips was a British civil-servant architect working in the government's Architectural Office when he designed this in the mid-1960s. It was the tallest government structure in Hong Kong when it opened in 1969, and a landmark of Hong Kong modernism — breezy, rational and precise without being cold. It closed as a government office in 2011 and was converted by Foster + Partners into the 336-room Murray hotel, which opened in 2018; Phillips, then ninety, was consulted throughout. Hong Kong Park sits directly behind, on the former site of Victoria Barracks, and the walk through it toward the Peak Tram lower terminus is a good one.

stops.title: Murray Building | 美利大廈
stops.caption: Face the Cotton Tree Drive facade and its grid of deep-set, angled windows.
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about this building — it received an environmental award in nineteen ninety-four for design decisions made in nineteen sixty-nine.

Face the Cotton Tree Drive facade and take in what Ron Phillips built for the Hong Kong Public Works Department: a twenty-seven-storey tower in white concrete, sitting above a row of eleven-metre arches at street level, with a facade of deep-set windows arranged in a precise geometric grid. The windows are not square-on to the street — they're angled, each one skewed to deflect direct sunlight away from the interior, a passive solar strategy Phillips embedded into the building's structure at a time when air conditioning was becoming standard and almost no one was thinking about reducing the need for it. The angled fins along the facade serve the same purpose. The effect is that the building reads differently depending on where you stand and what hour you visit it — the shadows shift, the depth of the openings changes, the white surface catches and releases light across the day.

Phillips was a British civil servant architect working in the government's Architectural Office when he designed this in the mid-nineteen-sixties, and the building he produced was, if you ask me, considerably more considered than a government office block had any obligation to be. It was the tallest government structure in Hong Kong when it opened in nineteen sixty-nine, and rather a landmark of Hong Kong modernism — breezy, rational, and precise, without being cold.

The building closed as a government office in two thousand and eleven and was converted by Foster + Partners into a three-hundred-and-thirty-six-room luxury hotel — the Murray — which opened in twenty eighteen. Phillips, then aged ninety, was consulted throughout the renovation. Foster + Partners' own architects described his original passive design as pioneering work done thirty years before sustainability became an architectural conversation.

Hong Kong Park is directly behind the building, and the walk through it toward the Peak Tram lower terminus is a good one — the park sits on the former site of Victoria Barracks, and the contrast between the formal gardens and the tower blocks pressing in on all sides is worth the short detour.

tours.title: Stone Slab Street | 石板街
tours.shortDescription: A stepped granite street laid for sedan-chair carriers in colonial Hong Kong — uneven by design, named for the colony's first governor, known to locals as daam baan gaai.
tours.longDescription:
It's 1842, and Henry Pottinger has just negotiated the Treaty of Nanking — the agreement that handed Hong Kong Island to Britain and created the colony. He became its first governor the following year, stayed fourteen months, and left. The street under your feet is named after him. The steps descend from Hollywood Road down to Queen's Road Central — roughly two hundred metres, climbing fifty metres of elevation. The granite slabs are laid in an alternating raised-and-lowered pattern that looks almost accidental but isn't: the unevenness let sedan-chair carriers and the coolies moving goods on shoulder poles find purchase on the wet granite, and channelled rainwater between the stones rather than letting it sheet down the slope.

The Chinese nickname, daam baan gaai — Stone Slab Street — captures the materiality the English name misses. Small wooden kiosks along the steps sell seasonal goods that shift with the Chinese calendar, traders who have held the same positions for decades, and beneath the surface a no-longer-accessible Second World War air-raid shelter still remains. At the top, immediately to your left on Hollywood Road, is Tai Kwun — the former Central Police Station compound and Victoria Prison — whose public courtyard makes a fitting contrast with the colonial granite you've just climbed.

stops.title: Stone Slab Street | 石板街
stops.caption: Stand on the stepped granite street between Hollywood Road and Queen's Road Central.
stops.transcriptText:
It's eighteen forty-two, and Henry Pottinger has just negotiated the Treaty of Nanking — the agreement that handed Hong Kong Island to Britain and created the colony. He became its first governor the following year, stayed fourteen months, and left. The street under your feet is named after him.

The steps descend from Hollywood Road at the top of the slope all the way down to Queen's Road Central below — roughly two hundred metres, climbing fifty metres of elevation, the gradient steep enough that you'll notice the texture underfoot changes as you move. The granite slabs are laid in an alternating pattern: raised and lowered, irregular, uneven in a way that looks almost accidental. It isn't. The unevenness was deliberate — designed so that the sedan chair carriers and the coolies moving goods on shoulder poles up and down the hill could find purchase on the wet granite without losing their footing, and so that rainwater would drain between the stones rather than sheeting straight down the slope. The design is practical engineering for a specific economy, and it is quite unchanged from the day it was laid.

The Chinese nickname for the street is daam baan gaai — Stone Slab Street — and the name captures what the English one doesn't: the texture, the materiality, the physical fact of the stone underfoot rather than the colonial administrator it was formally named for. Along the steps you'll find small wooden kiosks selling seasonal goods — costumes and decorations that shift with the Chinese calendar — traders who have occupied the same positions on the same steps for decades.

Beneath the surface, a World War Two air-raid shelter was built to protect civilians during Japanese bombing raids. It is no longer accessible, but it is still there.

Tai Kwun — the former Central Police Station compound and Victoria Prison — is at the top of the steps, immediately to your left as you reach Hollywood Road. The courtyard inside is open to the public, and the contrast between the colonial granite of the prison walls and the street you've just climbed is worth a few minutes of your time.

tours.title: 1881 Heritage | 前水警總部
tours.shortDescription: The former Marine Police Headquarters of 1884 — a hilltop anti-piracy base with a time-ball tower, now a luxury mall above wartime tunnels.
tours.longDescription:
Set back behind a slope and a row of mature trees on Canton Road, 1881 Heritage is a complex of red-brick buildings in white stucco — deep verandahs, granite columns, a stable block, and a small cylindrical signal tower with a metal ball on a rod near the top. It was the headquarters of the Hong Kong Marine Police, the anti-piracy squad that had patrolled the harbour since 1846. The government laid the foundation in 1881 — hence the name — though the building wasn't finished until 1884, and the earlier date stuck.

That signal tower, the Round House, dropped its ball every day at one o'clock to give ships an accurate time signal, occasionally doubling as a typhoon warning. During the Japanese occupation the compound became a naval base, and tunnels were dug beneath the lawn — sections still visible inside. The Marine Police left in 1996; the headquarters is now a boutique hotel and shopping mall, with one restaurant set inside a former prison cell. Land reclamation has since pushed the harbour the building was built to watch over well out of sight.

stops.title: 1881 Heritage | 前水警總部
stops.transcriptText:
This building is called 1881 Heritage. It was finished in eighteen eighty-four.

The compound sits on Canton Road, set back behind a gentle slope and a row of mature trees — a complex of red brick buildings finished in white stucco, with deep verandahs and granite columns, a stable block, and a small cylindrical signal tower with a metal ball mounted on a rod near the top. The main block was originally two storeys and extended to three in the nineteen twenties. The site was the headquarters of the Hong Kong Marine Police, known originally as the Water Police, who had been operating as an anti-piracy squad on the harbour since eighteen forty-six. After a fire severely damaged their patrol vessel, the John Adams, the government laid the foundation for a new headquarters in eighteen eighty-one — which is where the name comes from — though the building itself wasn't actually completed until eighteen eighty-four. Quite a long three years between foundation and finish, and the name stuck to the earlier date regardless.

That signal tower — known as the Round House — dropped its metal ball every day at one o'clock, giving ships in the harbour an accurate time signal, and occasionally doubled as a typhoon warning, which caused the odd bout of confusion among residents who weren't sure which announcement they were looking at. During the Japanese occupation in the nineteen forties, the complex became a Japanese naval base, and a network of tunnels was dug beneath the lawn — sections are still visible inside today.

Well, here's the layer worth knowing: this building was constructed specifically to watch over Victoria Harbour, on a bluff with a commanding view of the water. Land reclamation later pushed the harbour back, and the stretch where the Space Museum and Art Museum now stand sits between this compound and the water it was built to oversee. The Marine Police left in nineteen ninety-six. The headquarters is now a luxury shopping mall and boutique hotel, and one of the restaurants operates inside a former prison cell.

The shelter tunnels from the Japanese occupation are partially visible within the compound — ask at the Heritage Hall, where a short exhibition covers the site's full history, including the bits the shopfronts don't mention.

tours.title: Apliu Street Flea Market | 鴨寮街
tours.shortDescription: A Sham Shui Po lane of electronics in every state of age and function — a post-war repair economy built by craftsmen who arrived with nothing but their skills.
tours.longDescription:
The market runs along Apliu Street between Nam Cheong and Kweilin Streets, a pedestrian lane in Sham Shui Po lined with stalls selling electronics old and new without apology: phone cables, capacitors, vintage film cameras, mechanical alarm clocks, second-hand radios, retro consoles, and cable in every gauge and colour imaginable. At the far end it gives way to fabric and tailoring supplies — the textile traders have been here as long as the electronics, sharing the same pavement.

The name comes from a village long buried under Kowloon's urbanisation. The market grew up after the Second World War, when skilled engineers arriving from mainland China settled in this poorer district and put their knowledge to work repairing and reselling the electronics of a modernising economy. The repair culture is the most interesting thing here — technicians working at folding tables are a direct continuation of that post-war improvisation, an economy built from displacement that proved extraordinarily durable. Prices on unlabelled goods remain negotiable.

stops.title: Apliu Street Flea Market | 鴨寮街
stops.transcriptText:
Somewhere in the stalls around you, someone is repairing a circuit board on a folding table, surrounded by components so small they need a magnifier to place them. That is the sound and the spirit of this street.
The market runs along Apliu Street between Nam Cheong Street and Kweilin Street — a pedestrian lane in Sham Shui Po, Kowloon, lined on both sides with stalls and shopfronts selling electronics in every state of age and function. New components and old ones sit beside each other without apology: mobile phone cables, capacitors, vintage film cameras, alarm clocks with mechanical movements, second-hand radios, antique watches, retro gaming consoles, and quantities of cable in every gauge and colour you can imagine. At the far end the stalls give way to fabric and tailoring supplies — the textile traders of Sham Shui Po have been here as long as the electronics, and the two economies share the same pavement without much ceremony.
The name Apliu comes from a village that once stood nearby, long since buried under the urbanisation of Kowloon. The market itself grew up in the years after the Second World War, when waves of skilled craftsmen and engineers arrived from mainland China following the civil war, settled in Sham Shui Po — then as now one of the poorer districts of Hong Kong — and put their technical knowledge to work repairing, modifying, and reselling the electronics that Hong Kong's rapidly modernising economy was generating. I find the repair culture the most interesting thing here, actually: the technicians working on the pavement are a direct continuation of that post-war improvisation, an economy built from displacement that turned out to be extraordinarily durable.
The market has a long history as a place where the provenance of goods was not always asked about too carefully. It has cleaned up considerably. But the haggling culture remains, and prices on unlabelled items are always negotiable.
The Golden Computer Arcade is a five-minute walk, on the opposite side of Cheung Sha Wan Road — a covered building of computer hardware and gaming stalls that operates at a different register from Apliu but shares the same neighbourhood logic. Worth the short walk if you have time.

tours.title: Court of Final Appeal | 香港終審法院
tours.shortDescription: The 1912 Old Supreme Court on Statue Square, by Buckingham Palace's own architects — crowned by a blindfolded Themis who has outlasted every crisis the building survived.
tours.longDescription:
Facing west across Statue Square from Jackson Road, this three-storey granite building has a colonnade of Ionic columns, a central pediment carrying the British royal coat of arms, and a 2.7-metre bronze statue of a blindfolded Themis — goddess of justice, holding scales and sword — standing above the roofline since the building opened on 15 January 1912. Its architects, Sir Aston Webb and Ingress Bell, completed the Buckingham Palace facade the following year; the Old Supreme Court is the third corner of that triangle, built for a colony on the other side of the world.

Governor Lugard said at the opening that the courts should "stand firm though the skies fall." The skies did fall: the Japanese used it as Kempeitai headquarters during the occupation; MTR tunnelling beneath the square in 1978 cracked the foundations and forced the Supreme Court out; from 1985 to 2011 it housed the Legislative Council. In September 2015 the Court of Final Appeal moved in, returning the building to its original purpose — every crisis it survived having become one more demonstration of Lugard's claim.

stops.title: Court of Final Appeal | 香港終審法院
stops.transcriptText:
The architects who designed this building also designed the eastern facade of Buckingham Palace.
The building faces west across Statue Square from its position on Jackson Road — a three-storey granite structure with a colonnaded facade of Ionic columns, a central pediment carrying the British royal coat of arms, and above the roofline, a two-point-seven-metre bronze statue of a blindfolded woman holding scales and a sword. That is Themis, the Greek goddess of justice, and she has been standing up there since the building opened on the fifteenth of January, nineteen twelve. Sir Aston Webb and Ingress Bell, the architects, were at the peak of their practice — they completed the Buckingham Palace facade in nineteen thirteen, the year after this building opened, and the Victoria and Albert Museum's Cromwell Road frontage a few years earlier. The Old Supreme Court is the third corner of that triangle, built for a British colony on the other side of the world.
"At the opening ceremony, Governor Sir Frederick Lugard said the courts of justice should ""stand firm though the skies fall"" — built, as he put it, as an outward symbol of justice that would surpass all other structures in durability. Well, the skies did fall, on several occasions. The Japanese took the building as the headquarters of the Kempeitai — the military police — during the occupation from nineteen forty-one to forty-five. In nineteen seventy-eight the MTR tunnelling directly beneath the square cracked the foundations badly enough to force the Supreme Court out entirely. From nineteen eighty-five to twenty eleven it housed the Legislative Council, the chamber in which Hong Kong debated the terms of its own political future. Then, in September twenty fifteen, the Court of Final Appeal moved in, and the building returned to its original purpose."
If you ask me, Lugard's words have aged rather better than he deserved. Not because the building was undamaged, but because every crisis it survived turned out to be one more demonstration of the claim.
The interior is open for special public tours and on designated open days — the heritage gallery inside is worth the visit, and the courtroom itself is occasionally accessible during legal open days. Check the Court of Final Appeal's website before you visit.

tours.title: Flower Market | 花墟
tours.shortDescription: Three hundred metres of Mong Kok that smell of cut flowers — a wholesale trade that began with colonial-era farmers and peaks in the frantic hours before Lunar New Year.
tours.longDescription:
Flower Market Road runs east to west through the northern edge of Mong Kok, about 300 metres between Prince Edward Road and Boundary Street, with nearly a hundred florists in permanent shopfronts. The stock runs from cut roses, carnations, lilies and chrysanthemums to potted orchids, kumquat trees, bonsai, cacti, seeds and soil. The wholesale trade at the eastern end starts early, with deliveries arriving from mainland China from around nine in the morning.

The street has been the centre of Hong Kong's flower wholesale trade since the 1970s, though the habit of growing flowers here goes back to the 1860s, when farmers cultivated blooms on marginal land for a new colonial market. By the 1970s the growers had given way to merchant traders — a market that began with farmers and ended with dealers. Lunar New Year is its most dramatic version of itself: kumquat trees, pussy willow, peach blossom and narcissus fill the pavement, and the custom of hang fa shi — walking the flower market — draws crowds in the final hours before midnight, when unsold stock is simply discarded. Fresh flowers for a fresh year.

stops.title: Flower Market | 花墟
stops.transcriptText:
Somewhere in the middle of one of the densest urban districts on earth, this street smells of cut flowers. That contrast has not worn off.
Flower Market Road runs east to west through the northern edge of Mong Kok, stretching about three hundred metres between Prince Edward Road and Boundary Street, with nearly a hundred retail and wholesale florists lining both sides in permanent shopfronts. The stock runs from cut flowers — roses, carnations, lilies, chrysanthemums — to potted orchids, kumquat trees, bonsai, cacti, seeds, soil, and every piece of gardening equipment a flat-dweller without a garden could require. The shops are open daily, and the wholesale trade at the eastern end starts early, with deliveries arriving from mainland China from around nine in the morning.
The street has been the centre of Hong Kong's flower wholesale trade since the nineteen seventies, though the habit of growing and selling flowers in this part of Kowloon goes back considerably further — to the years after the British took possession of Kowloon in eighteen sixty, when local farmers began cultivating flowers on marginal land to meet a new colonial market for ornamental blooms. By the nineteen seventies the original growers had largely given way to merchant traders, and I find that transition quietly emblematic: a market that began with farmers and ended with dealers, the product getting more elaborate and more international as the city got richer.
The Lunar New Year week is the market's most compressed and dramatic version of itself. Kumquat trees — symbolising prosperity — fill the pavement, alongside pussy willow branches, peach blossoms, and narcissus. The custom of hang fa shi — walking the flower market — draws enormous crowds in the final hours before midnight on New Year's Day, when sellers try to move the last of their stock before the new year begins and what isn't sold is actually discarded. Fresh flowers for a fresh year. The logic is, once you hear it, rather satisfying.
The Bird Garden is directly adjacent at the eastern end of the street — the two markets share a wall, and in the early morning especially, the combination of birdsong drifting over from the garden and the scent of cut flowers on the street is worth the early start.

tours.title: Former French Mission Building | 前法國外方傳道會大樓
tours.shortDescription: An Edwardian Neoclassical landmark on Battery Path with a domed chapel corner — French missionaries, Japanese military police, and eighteen years as Hong Kong's highest court.
tours.longDescription:
Where Battery Path curves up through the lower slopes of Government Hill, the Former French Mission Building stands in granite and red brick — a domed chapel at the northwest corner, green louvres across the facade, and Roman columns along the first-floor verandah. The louvres are original to the 1917 design, a practical answer to the subtropical climate before air conditioning. It was built by the French Society of Foreign Missions on the site of an earlier Victorian mansion, and is one of the finer surviving examples of Edwardian Neoclassical architecture in Central.

Its résumé across the following century is compressed: French missionaries until the war; Japanese military police headquarters during the occupation; provisional British government headquarters in the weeks after the surrender; a Jesuit language school; the Education Department; the District and Supreme Courts. From 1 July 1997 — the day of the handover — it became the seat of the Court of Final Appeal until 2015. The chapel was converted to a courtroom for those eighteen years and the verandah glassed in for the judges; both alterations were reversed during restoration, the building returned as far as possible to its 1917 form.

stops.title: Former French Mission Building | 前法國外方傳道會大樓
stops.transcriptText:
It's the twenty-third of August, nineteen forty-five. The Japanese have surrendered, and the first act of the restored British administration is to set up the provisional government headquarters in this building.
Battery Path curves up from Ice House Street through the lower slopes of Government Hill, and the Former French Mission Building sits at its eastern edge — three storeys in granite and red brick, with a domed chapel at the northwest corner, green louvres across the facade, and Roman columns along the first-floor verandah. You'll notice the louvres immediately: they are original to the nineteen seventeen design, a practical response to the subtropical climate long before air conditioning existed. The building was constructed by the French Society of Foreign Missions as its Hong Kong administrative headquarters, on the site of an earlier Victorian mansion, and it is one of the finer examples of Edwardian Neoclassical architecture surviving in Central.
The building's résumé across the following century is rather compressed. French missionaries until the Second World War; Japanese military police headquarters during the occupation; provisional British government headquarters in the weeks after the surrender; a Jesuit language school; the Education Department; the Victoria District Court; the Supreme Court; the Government Information Services. Then, from the first of July nineteen ninety-seven — the day of the handover of sovereignty from Britain to China — it became the seat of the Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal, the city's highest court, which it remained until September twenty fifteen, when the CFA relocated to the larger Old Supreme Court Building on Statue Square.
The chapel in the northwest corner was converted to a courtroom for those eighteen years. The verandah was glassed in and air-conditioned to give the judges a sheltered passage between their offices and the bench. Both alterations were reversed during the subsequent restoration, the building returned as far as possible to its nineteen seventeen form — mosaics reinstated in the floors, glass removed from the verandah, louvres back in the sun.
The Old Supreme Court Building at eight Jackson Road — the current seat of the Court of Final Appeal — is ten minutes on foot downhill, on the eastern side of Statue Square. Its foundation rests on hundreds of Chinese fir tree trunks driven into reclaimed harbour silt, and it is worth the walk for the building alone.

tours.title: Fringe Club | 藝穗會
tours.shortDescription: A Victorian cold-storage dairy in "blood and bandages" brickwork — empty for a decade, now four decades of free arts space in the middle of Central.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Lower Albert Road and Wyndham Street, the facade stops you first: alternating bands of red Canton brick and white stucco, a pattern that earned the nickname "blood and bandages." It was built in 1892 for the Dairy Farm Company as a cold storage warehouse and depot — the firm founded six years earlier by Sir Patrick Manson, the Scottish surgeon who helped establish that mosquitoes transmit malaria, with five Hong Kong businessmen, to supply fresh milk to a colonial population that had managed without it.

Dairy Farm expanded the building in 1913 to include a dairy shop, meat smoking room, cold storage and a manager's residence, used it as headquarters until the 1970s, then left it empty. The Foreign Correspondents' Club took the north block in 1982; in 1984 Benny Chia was given the south block and opened the Fringe Club — "Where else can you find a loft like this outside Soho?" Surrounded by law firms, private banks and five-star hotels, a not-for-profit has provided free exhibition and performance space to Hong Kong artists for over forty years. The basement — once the ice-handling and meat-processing space — still has its original ceramic tile walls.

stops.title: Fringe Club | 藝穗會
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about this building — it looks like a Victorian dairy, because it is one.
"The building occupies the corner of Lower Albert Road and Wyndham Street, and the facade stops you before anything else does: alternating bands of red Canton brick and white-painted stucco running horizontally across the exterior, a pattern that earned it the nickname ""blood and bandages."" The architectural style is late Victorian eclectic — moulded cornices, bull's-eye windows, pilasters, keystones — all produced in 1892 for the Dairy Farm Company as a cold storage warehouse and distribution depot. The company was founded six years earlier by Sir Patrick Manson, a Scottish surgeon who would later become one of the first scientists to establish that mosquitoes transmit malaria, and five Hong Kong businessmen, to supply fresh milk to a colonial population that had previously managed without it."
"The building expanded in nineteen thirteen to include a dairy shop, a meat smoking room, cold storage for winter clothing, and a residence for the general manager — a range of functions that explains the scale of the thing. Dairy Farm used it as its company headquarters until the nineteen seventies, then moved out and left it empty. In nineteen eighty-two the Foreign Correspondents' Club moved into the north block; in nineteen eighty-four Benny Chia, who had been looking for a home for an experimental arts organisation, was given the south block and opened the Fringe Club. His reported reaction on first seeing the space was: ""Where else can you find a loft like this outside Soho?"""
What the Fringe has done with it, actually, is the more interesting story. Surrounded by law firms, private banks, and five-star hotels, a not-for-profit arts organisation has been providing free exhibition and performance space to Hong Kong artists in the middle of Central for over forty years — if you ask me, one of the more quietly determined acts of cultural persistence in the city.
The basement — the Fringe Underground — was once the building's ice handling and meat processing space, and still has the original ceramic tile walls and a freight elevator sign warning passengers not to use it. It's worth heading down if the space is open.

tours.title: Goldfish Street | 金魚街
tours.shortDescription: A Mong Kok street where live fish hang from the shopfronts in oxygen-filled bags — pets bought less for company than for the financial atmosphere of a home.
tours.longDescription:
The market occupies a 300-metre stretch of Tung Choi Street North between Bute and Nelson Streets — twenty to thirty specialist shops, each with stock displayed outside in oxygen-pumped plastic bags hanging in rows under blue and red lights, species and price written on the plastic in marker. The fish go back into the tanks at the end of the day; the bags exist because the shopfronts are tiny and let vendors show far more stock than tanks would. The effect is a wall of moving colour unlike any other market street in the city.

The trade is older than the street: breeders from the New Territories were selling goldfish from Boundary Street as early as the 1950s, arriving by train before dawn and trading from buckets until sunrise. That pre-dawn wholesale market still runs outside Mong Kok Stadium. The feng shui logic matters here — goldfish represent prosperity, the word sounding close to "gold and jade," and flowing water is associated with wealth energy. Families buy goldfish not merely as pets but as a considered addition to a home's financial atmosphere, a function a pet shop elsewhere simply doesn't carry.

stops.title: Goldfish Street | 金魚街
stops.transcriptText:
Look at what's hanging from the shopfronts. Every one of those transparent bags contains a live fish.
The market occupies a three-hundred-metre stretch of Tung Choi Street North between Bute Street and Nelson Street — around twenty to thirty specialist shops, each with its stock displayed outside in oxygen-pumped plastic bags hanging in rows under blue and red lights, the price and species written in marker on the plastic. The bags are not permanent housing; the fish go back into the tanks at the end of the day. The display method exists because the shopfronts are extremely small and the bags allow vendors to show far more stock than any tank arrangement would permit. The effect, whatever its logic, is quite something — a wall of moving colour and light that doesn't resemble any other market street in Hong Kong.
The trade here is older than the street. Fish breeders from the New Territories were selling goldfish from Boundary Street as early as the nineteen fifties, arriving by train in the early hours and trading from buckets on the pavement until sunrise. That pre-dawn wholesale market still operates today outside Mong Kok Stadium — fish breeders setting up at four in the morning, gone before most of the city wakes up — while the retail shops on this street open later in the morning for the general public.
"The feng shui logic behind all of this is worth understanding. Goldfish in Chinese culture represent prosperity and abundance, partly because the pronunciation of 金魚 — goldfish — sounds similar to ""gold and jade,"" and partly because flowing water is associated with wealth energy. I always think the practical result is that Hong Kong families buy goldfish not merely as pets but as a considered addition to the financial atmosphere of a home — which gives this street a quite specific cultural function that a pet shop in most other cities simply doesn't carry."
The Mong Kok Bird Garden is a ten-minute walk north on Yuen Po Street — a covered garden of stalls selling songbirds in ornate bamboo cages, which operates on similar principles of auspiciousness and is worth the short walk.

tours.title: Happy Valley Racecourse | 跑馬地馬場
tours.shortDescription: A racetrack named, with Victorian irony, after the cemetery it replaced — a drained swamp ringed by apartment towers and six burial grounds on the slopes above.
tours.longDescription:
Happy Valley is a natural bowl, the racetrack ringed by dense apartment towers rising almost off the rail, with stands holding up to 55,000 people. The valley was originally swampland — Wong Nai Chung, the yellow mud stream — and in Hong Kong's earliest colonial years the death rate from disease was high enough that it became a burial ground for the British dead. Settlers gave it the cheerful name as polite cover; the name outlasted the graves. In 1845 the British drained the swamp and built a racecourse, the first race running in December 1846, and the Hong Kong Jockey Club was established in 1884.

The place earned its name twice over on 26 February 1918, when an overcrowded temporary grandstand of bamboo and matting collapsed onto food stalls cooking below; the resulting fire killed several hundred people, one of the deadliest disasters in the city's history. The permanent brick-and-concrete stands were built the following year in response. The cemetery never quite left — six burial grounds stand on the slopes immediately above the track, close enough that on a Wednesday race night the crowd's noise carries up among the gravestones. Those floodlit Wednesday nights, a tradition since 1973, are the ones to come for.

stops.title: Happy Valley Racecourse | 跑馬地馬場
stops.transcriptText:
Happy Valley is named that because it used to be a cemetery. The name is a euphemism, not a description.

You're looking down into a natural bowl, the racetrack ringed by dense apartment towers rising almost directly off the rail, with stands on one side holding up to fifty-five thousand people. The valley was originally swampland — Wong Nai Chung, the yellow mud stream — and in Hong Kong's earliest colonial years the death rate from disease here was high enough that the area became a burial ground for the British dead. Settlers gave it the cheerful name as a kind of polite cover, the way Victorians often did with cemeteries, and the name outlasted the graves: in eighteen forty-five the British drained the swamp, banned the local rice farming that had used the land, and built a racecourse on it instead. The first race ran in December eighteen forty-six, and the Hong Kong Jockey Club, which still runs the track today, was formally established in eighteen eighty-four.

You'll see why the place earned its name twice over if you know the date the twenty-sixth of February, nineteen eighteen. During the annual Derby meeting, an overcrowded temporary grandstand made of bamboo and matting collapsed onto food stalls cooking below, and the resulting fire killed several hundred people — one of the deadliest disasters in the city's history. The permanent brick and concrete stands you're looking at now were built the following year, in direct response.

Worth knowing: the cemetery never actually left the neighbourhood. Six burial grounds — Jewish, Hindu, Parsee, the main Hong Kong Cemetery, Catholic, and Muslim — stand on the slopes immediately above the track, close enough that on a Wednesday race night the noise of the crowd carries up among the gravestones. The valley has never quite stopped being two things in the same small space.

Wednesday nights, when the floodlights are on and the track is open for racing, are the ones to come for — a tradition running since nineteen seventy-three, with beer gardens and food stalls turning the whole bowl into something closer to a street party than a sporting fixture.

tours.title: Hong Kong Tram | 香港電車
tours.shortDescription: The world's only all-double-decker tram fleet, running since 1904 in an official shade called HK Tram Green — licensed transport, not a museum, that keeps winning the argument.
tours.longDescription:
Standing at a stop along the northern shore of Hong Kong Island, you're on the route that has run continuously between Kennedy Town and Shau Kei Wan since 1904. The trams are double-decker, narrow, built to a track gauge barely wider than a metre, and all the same dark green — a colour that began in the 1940s as leftover wartime paint and was eventually given an official name: HK Tram Green, registered by the Pantone Color Institute in 2021.

The system opened on 30 July 1904, when a woman known to history only as Mrs Jones — wife of the director of public works — drove the first car out of the Causeway Bay depot, her son ringing the bell beside her. The first fleet of twenty-six cars was single-deck; the double-decker arrived in 1912, open-topped at first, fully enclosed only by 1925. Hong Kong Tramways is now the only tram system anywhere in the world running exclusively on double-deckers. The MTR's Island Line runs almost the same route, sometimes directly beneath the road — built to move people faster between the same two points — yet the tram, licensed transport rather than a protected exhibit, has simply kept winning the argument: a flat fare, 200,000 riders a day, and a 1998 decision not to replace the fleet.

stops.title: Hong Kong Tram | 香港電車
stops.transcriptText:
Look at the colour. Every tram on this line is painted the same shade of green — and that shade has an official name.

You're standing at a tram stop somewhere along the northern shore of Hong Kong Island, on the route that has run continuously between Kennedy Town in the west and Shau Kei Wan in the east since nineteen oh four. The trams themselves are double-decker, narrow, and built to a track gauge barely wider than a metre — and you'll notice they're all the same dark green, a colour that began in the nineteen forties simply as leftover wartime paint and was eventually given an official name: HK Tram Green, registered by the Pantone Color Institute in twenty twenty-one to mark its place in the city's identity.

The system opened on the thirtieth of July, nineteen oh four, when a woman known to history only as Mrs Jones — wife of the director of public works — drove the first tramcar out of the Causeway Bay depot, her son ringing the bell beside her the whole way. The first fleet of twenty-six cars was entirely single-deck, ten reserved for first class passengers and sixteen for third. The double-decker design didn't arrive until nineteen twelve, open-topped at first, with a canvas roof added the following year, and the fully enclosed upper deck you'd recognise today only appeared in nineteen twenty-five. Hong Kong Tramways is now the only tram system anywhere in the world that runs exclusively on double-deckers — the largest fleet of its kind still operating.

A bit of context worth having: the MTR's Island Line runs almost the same route as this tram, sometimes tunnelling directly beneath the very road you're standing on, built specifically to move people faster between the same two points. The tram has had every commercial reason to be quietly retired and none of the protection that usually saves this sort of thing — it's licensed transport, not a museum exhibit. It has simply kept winning the argument. Flat fare, two hundred thousand riders a day, and in nineteen ninety-eight, when the city actually considered replacing the whole fleet with modern light rail, it chose not to.

Ride the full route west to east if you have the time — Kennedy Town to Shau Kei Wan takes around ninety minutes through Sheung Wan, Central, Wan Chai, and Causeway Bay, and the upper deck gives you the best moving view of the island's northern shore that exists.

tours.title: Former Kowloon-Canton Railway Clock Tower | 前九廣鐵路鐘樓
tours.shortDescription: All that remains of the terminus where a train from Calais once pulled in — 44 metres of red brick that survived the demolition of the station around it.
tours.longDescription:
At the southern end of the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront, between the Star Ferry pier and the Cultural Centre, the tower rises 44 metres in red Canton brick and grey granite — Edwardian Classical Revival, with an octagonal domed belfry and a seven-metre lightning rod above. The clock faces and bell have been here since 1921. The bell rang on the hour until 1950, then fell silent and was removed; it spent thirty-five years in a railway depot before returning in 2010, and chimes again, though the original mechanism is long gone.

The tower is all that survives of the Kowloon-Canton Railway Terminus, which occupied this waterfront from 1916 to 1975 — the southernmost point of a continuous rail connection that ran, in theory, from the English Channel to Kowloon through the whole width of Eurasia. The station closed when the line moved to Hung Hom and was demolished in 1977-78 despite a preservation campaign that petitioned the Queen. The campaign failed; the tower was kept. Six salvaged columns now stand, without much context, in a garden in Tsim Sha Tsui East, while the Cultural Centre, Space Museum and Museum of Art occupy the station's footprint.

stops.title: Former Kowloon-Canton Railway Clock Tower | 前九廣鐵路鐘樓
stops.transcriptText:
It's nineteen twenty-one. A train has just completed a journey from Calais, France — across Europe, through Siberia, down through Manchuria — and pulled in to the terminus at your feet, on the edge of Victoria Harbour.
The tower stands at the southern end of the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront, between the Star Ferry pier to your west and the Cultural Centre to your east — forty-four metres of red Canton brick and grey granite in the Edwardian Classical Revival style, with an octagonal domed belfry at the top and a seven-metre lightning rod above that. The clock faces on all four sides have been here since nineteen twenty-one, when the bell also went into service. The bell rang on the hour until nineteen fifty, then fell silent, and was eventually removed from the tower entirely. It spent thirty-five years in a railway depot before being returned to its original home in twenty ten. It now chimes again, though the original mechanical movement is long gone.
The tower is all that remains of the Kowloon-Canton Railway Terminus that occupied this waterfront from nineteen sixteen to nineteen seventy-five. Designed by Arthur Benison Hubback, the FMS Government Architect based in Malaya, the terminus was the southernmost point of a continuous rail connection running from the English Channel — Calais to Kowloon, by train, through the whole width of the Eurasian continent. The station closed when the KCR relocated to Hung Hom, and the building was demolished in nineteen seventy-seven and seventy-eight despite a preservation campaign that included a petition to the Queen. The campaign failed. The station came down. The tower was kept, and six columns from the original building were moved to a garden in Tsim Sha Tsui East, where they stand today without much context or ceremony.
Worth knowing is that the site is not empty — the Hong Kong Cultural Centre, the Space Museum, and the Museum of Art now occupy the station's footprint. The city demolished one building and replaced it with three others. Whether that is a reasonable exchange is, perhaps, still being decided.
The six salvaged columns from the original station are in the Urban Council Centenary Garden, a five-minute walk east along Salisbury Road — a rather understated end for the pillars of a station that was the gateway to the whole of China.

tours.title: Ladies' Market | 女人街
tours.shortDescription: A kilometre of bargaining on a street named after a vegetable — Hong Kong's first authorised hawking zone, born of the 1970s oil crisis in the world's densest district.
tours.longDescription:
Tung Choi Street means Water Spinach Street — before the buildings came, this was a paddy field on the edge of Mong Kok Village. The market occupies its southern kilometre between Argyle and Dundas Streets, stalls running along both sides with a narrow channel of pedestrians through the middle: clothing, accessories, handbags, watches, toys, souvenirs and household goods, prices negotiable, density considerable. The "Ladies'" name came from the 1970s, when stalls here concentrated on women's wear for the factory workers and housewives of Kowloon. In 1975 the government formalised it as an authorised hawking zone — the first of its kind in Hong Kong — partly to regulate the traders who had set up in response to the oil crisis and the unemployment that followed.

The economics are straightforward: Mong Kok holds around 130,000 people per square kilometre, a density that has at times earned it the Guinness record for the world's most populated district, and commerce fills every available surface. The parallel streets complete the picture — Fa Yuen Street sells sneakers, Sai Yeung Choi Street electronics, and the northern end of Tung Choi Street becomes Goldfish Street, where live fish are sold in plastic pouches.

stops.title: Ladies' Market | 女人街
stops.transcriptText:
The street you're standing on is named after a vegetable. Tung Choi Street means Water Spinach Street — because before the buildings came, this was a paddy field on the edge of Mong Kok Village, growing water spinach.
"The market occupies the southern kilometre of Tung Choi Street between Argyle Street to your north and Dundas Street to your south, with stalls running along both sides and a narrow channel of pedestrian traffic moving through the middle. The goods are broadly what the name suggests: clothing, accessories, handbags, watches, toys, souvenirs, and household items, with the prices negotiable and the density considerable. The ""Ladies'"" name came from the nineteen seventies, when the stalls here concentrated on women's wear and accessories for the factory workers and housewives of Kowloon. In nineteen seventy-five, the government formalised the area as an authorised hawking zone — the first of its kind in Hong Kong — partly to regulate the wave of informal traders who had set up in response to the oil crisis and the unemployment that followed it."
Well, the economics of the thing are rather straightforward. Mong Kok holds around a hundred and thirty thousand people per square kilometre — a density that has at various points earned it the Guinness World Record for the world's most populated district — and in a neighbourhood where this many people live this close together, commerce fills every available surface. The parallel streets within a few minutes' walk give you the full picture: Fa Yuen Street to the east sells almost exclusively sneakers; Sai Yeung Choi Street to the west is electronics; and the northern end of Tung Choi Street itself becomes the Goldfish Street, where live fish are sold in individual plastic pouches, goldfish being considered auspicious.
If the density here is beginning to feel like a lot, the Mong Kok Flower Market on Flower Market Road is ten minutes north on foot — a long street of florists selling cut flowers, plants, and orchids, which operates on entirely different sensory terms from this one.

tours.title: Lan Fong Yuen | 蘭芳園
tours.shortDescription: The Central stall credited with inventing silk-stocking milk tea — named for a straining cloth so tea-stained that customers assumed it was an old stocking.
tours.longDescription:
The stall sits on the corner of Gage and Cochrane Streets, beneath the Mid-Levels Escalator, with two queues running in different directions — one for dine-in on Gage Street, one for takeaway along Cochrane Street. The original green stall front, dating from when this was a proper dai pai dong — an open-air street stall, increasingly rare now — is still preserved at street level, even though most of the operation has moved indoors.

The founder, Lam Muk-ho, arrived from a Chaozhou family as a teenager and opened his first tea stall at twenty-four near the old Sheung Wan pier, serving dockworkers. The tea strainers of the time were poor, so he had his wife tailor better ones from cotton-padded jacket fabric. Repeated straining — exactly three times per batch — turned the cloth dark brown, and customers assumed they were looking at old silk stockings. The name stuck, and is now recorded in Hong Kong government heritage documents as the genuine origin of silk-stocking milk tea, alongside the tea-and-coffee blend yuanyang. A drink now treated as a symbol of the city began as one man's practical fix for bad equipment — the brusque service and plastic stools are the same DNA the tea came from.

stops.title: Lan Fong Yuen | 蘭芳園
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about the tea you're about to queue for — it's named after a stocking, because for years, everyone assumed that's exactly what was used to strain it.

The stall sits on the corner of Gage Street and Cochrane Street, beneath the Mid-Levels Escalator, with two separate queues running in different directions — one for dine-in, forming outside the drinks station on Gage Street, the other for takeaway, stretching along Cochrane Street. The original green stall front, dating from when this was a proper dai pai dong — an open-air street stall, increasingly rare in the city now — is still preserved at street level, even though most of the actual operation has long since moved indoors.

The founder, Lam Muk-ho, arrived in Hong Kong as a teenager from a Chaozhou family and opened his first small tea stall at twenty-four near the old Sheung Wan pier, serving coffee and basic snacks to dockworkers who needed something quick before heading back to the job. The tea strainers available at the time were poor quality, so Lam had his wife tailor-make better ones from the fabric of cotton-padded jackets. Repeated straining — exactly three times, every batch — turned the cloth dark brown, and customers, not unreasonably, assumed they were looking at old silk stockings. The name stuck. Actually, it's now recorded in official Hong Kong government heritage documents as the genuine origin of silk-stocking milk tea, alongside the tea-and-coffee blend called yuanyang, also credited to this stall.

If you ask me, that's the part worth holding onto: a drink now treated as a defining symbol of the city began as one man's practical fix for bad equipment, serving labourers who needed to get back to work. The brusque service and the plastic stools aren't a flaw in the experience — they're the same DNA the tea came from.

Graham Street Market runs immediately uphill from here — one of the last traditional wet markets left in Central, and worth a walk after your tea, before the area changes any further.

tours.title: Luk Yu Tea House | 陸羽茶室
tours.shortDescription: A 1933 tea house that has survived nearly a century by changing almost nothing — black ceiling fans, marble booths, brass spittoons, and dim sum still served by trolley.
tours.longDescription:
Now occupying three floors at the corner of Stanley Street, where it relocated in 1976, Luk Yu carries an unspoken hierarchy: the ground floor belongs, by custom rather than any sign, to regular local patrons and the old business class, while the upper floors are more relaxed. The interior is largely unchanged in decades — black ceiling fans turning overhead, mirrored and marbled private booths, stained glass, and brass spittoons standing in the corners, relics of a habit long abandoned but never removed. Calligraphy and paintings by recognised masters, including Chang Dai-chien, hang among the wooden screens.

The name comes from Lu Yu, the Tang dynasty poet who wrote The Classic of Tea; Luk Yu is simply the Cantonese pronunciation. Dim sum is served the traditional way, by trolley, and some waiters in white jackets have been doing the job for more than forty years. What's worth understanding is what Luk Yu has refused to do: in a city that demolishes its history with remarkable speed, it has survived by changing almost nothing — the recipes, the floor hierarchy, the spittoons nobody uses but nobody removes. Permanence has turned out to be the rarer trick.

stops.title: Luk Yu Tea House | 陸羽茶室
stops.transcriptText:
It's nineteen thirty-three, and a former bank clerk and a restaurant manager have just opened a tea house inside an old bank building on Wing Kut Street.

The tea house now occupies three floors at the corner of Stanley Street, having relocated here in nineteen seventy-six, and the floors carry their own unspoken hierarchy: the ground floor belongs, by long custom rather than any sign, to regular local patrons and the city's old business class, while the upper floors are slightly more relaxed, used by families and visitors. The interior hasn't been substantially altered in decades — black ceiling fans turning slowly overhead, mirrored and marbled private booths, stained-glass windows, and brass spittoons still standing in the corners, relics of a habit long since abandoned but never removed. Calligraphy and paintings by recognised masters, including Chang Dai-chien, hang on the walls among the wooden screens.

The name comes from Lu Yu, a Tang dynasty poet who wrote The Classic of Tea — the foundational text on Chinese tea culture — and Luk Yu is simply the Cantonese pronunciation of his name. The dim sum is served the traditional way, by trolley, and some of the waiters in their white jackets have been doing this job for more than forty years. I find that detail rather telling: the staff have outlasted entire careers elsewhere in the city.

What's worth understanding about Luk Yu is what it has refused to do. Hong Kong is a city that demolishes its own history with remarkable speed — rents rise, buildings come down, restaurants close within a few years of opening. Luk Yu has survived nearly a century by changing almost nothing: the recipes, the floor hierarchy, the spittoons nobody uses but nobody removes. In two thousand and two, a serious and widely reported incident took place on the premises that briefly made international headlines — and even that became absorbed into the tea house's accumulating lore rather than disrupting it. In a city built on reinvention, permanence has turned out to be the rarer and more interesting trick.

Stanley Street and the surrounding lanes — Wing Wah, Wing Kut, Graham — were once dense with exactly this kind of tea house, dozens of them, in the decades when Central was still residential as much as commercial. Almost all of them are gone. Walking the block after your tea is a quiet way to feel how much has emptied out around the one that stayed.

tours.title: Man Mo Temple | 文武廟
tours.shortDescription: A smoke-filled hall on Hollywood Road dedicated to the gods of literature and war — and, for decades, a de facto courthouse where the Chinese community settled its own disputes.
tours.longDescription:
The entrance on Hollywood Road is a dark wooden doorway easy to walk past, opening into a dim hall lit mostly by the glow of incense — thick spiral coils hang from the ceiling, each able to burn for up to two weeks. The complex is three buildings in one — Man Mo Temple, Lit Shing Kung and Kung Sor — built between 1847 and 1862 by wealthy Chinese merchants. The founding date isn't recorded; the earliest evidence is an inscription on a brass bell dated to 1847, as close to a birth certificate as the temple has.

The two principal deities give the temple its name: Man, the god of literature, in red with a calligraphy brush, and Mo, the god of war, in green with a sword — both believed to have been real people, the latter becoming patron of an unusually wide range of trades. Two gold-plated sedan chairs in the main hall, from 1862 and 1885, carried the gods' statues through the streets during festivals. The most interesting part isn't the worship at all: the Kung Sor block functioned for decades as a de facto courthouse and meeting hall, where Chinese community leaders settled disputes at a time when the colonial government offered them no real voice. The temple was never just spiritual — it was civic infrastructure, built because nobody else would provide it.

stops.title: Man Mo Temple | 文武廟
stops.transcriptText:
Look up before you go any further. The thick spiral coils of incense hanging from the ceiling can burn for up to two weeks each, and the smoke they produce is most of what you'll remember about this place.

The temple entrance sits on Hollywood Road, a dark wooden doorway easy to walk past if you're not looking for it, opening into a dim, smoke-filled hall lit mostly by the glow of the incense itself. The complex is actually three buildings in one — Man Mo Temple, Lit Shing Kung, and Kung Sor — separated by two narrow alleys and built between eighteen forty-seven and eighteen sixty-two by wealthy Chinese merchants. The exact founding date isn't recorded anywhere, oddly enough; the earliest evidence is an inscription on a brass bell, dated to the Qing dynasty year corresponding to eighteen forty-seven, which is as close to a birth certificate as the temple has.

The two principal deities give the temple its name: Man, the god of literature, dressed in red and holding a calligraphy brush, and Mo, the god of war, in green, holding a sword. Both were believed to have been real people in life — Man Cheong a third-century scholar-official, Mo Tai a second-century warrior also known as Kwan Tai, who in time became the patron of an unusually wide range of trades: restaurants, pawnbrokers, and, by tradition, the secret Triads as well. Have a look at the two gold-plated sedan chairs inside the main hall — one from eighteen sixty-two, the other from eighteen eighty-five — used to carry the gods' statues through the streets during festivals.

I always think the most interesting part of this place isn't the worship at all. The Kung Sor block functioned for decades as a de facto courthouse and meeting hall, where Chinese community leaders settled disputes among themselves at a time when the colonial government offered them no real voice in how the city was run. The temple was never just spiritual. It was civic infrastructure, built because nobody else was going to provide it.

Hollywood Road continues west from here as Hong Kong's antiques district, lined with shops selling Chinese porcelain, jade, and curios — and the Mid-Levels Escalator, the longest covered outdoor escalator system in the world, is a short walk east if your legs need the help getting back up the hill.

tours.title: Monster Building | 怪獸大廈
tours.shortDescription: Five interconnected towers, 2,243 flats, and one vertiginous courtyard — ordinary cheap housing for fifty years before a viral photograph made it a dystopian icon.
tours.longDescription:
On King's Road in Quarry Bay, the complex occupies a whole city block in an E-shaped formation: five interconnected towers — Fook Cheong, Yick Cheong, Yick Fat, Montane Mansion and Oceanic Mansion — each eighteen storeys, enclosing two courtyards reachable on foot. The courtyard is where the famous photograph is taken: standing on the concrete floor, looking straight up, the geometry compresses into something vertiginous and symmetrical. The western courtyard is plain white, the eastern one varied colours. 2,243 flats, thirty square metres each on average.

The buildings were completed in 1972, originally planned as Parker Estate, a low-income development on the former Taikoo Sugar Refinery site. The first developer went bankrupt in the 1965 banking crisis before a wall was built; a third company finished it. The flats were among the cheapest in Hong Kong, bought by mainland refugees who had arrived since the late 1940s with little else. The "Monster Building" nickname came much later — it was ordinary housing for decades before the courtyard photograph went viral and films like Transformers and Ghost in the Shell used it as shorthand for dystopian density. It wasn't designed to be arresting; it was designed to be cheap, fast and large enough to shelter as many families as possible — and it did that job for fifty years before anyone pointed a camera upward. Ten thousand people still live here.

stops.title: Monster Building | 怪獸大廈
stops.transcriptText:
You are standing in front of someone's home. Ten thousand people live here. That is worth keeping in mind before you raise the camera.
The complex sits on King's Road in Quarry Bay, occupying an entire city block in an E-shaped formation: five interconnected towers — Fook Cheong Building, Yick Cheong Building, Yick Fat Building, Montane Mansion, and Oceanic Mansion — each eighteen storeys high, enclosing two interior courtyards that are accessible on foot from the street. The courtyard is where the famous photograph is taken: standing on the concrete floor, looking straight up at the encircling towers, the geometry compresses into something vertiginous and symmetrical that photographs extremely well. The western courtyard is painted plain white; the eastern one in varied colours. Two thousand two hundred and forty-three flats. Thirty square metres each, on average.
"The buildings were completed in nineteen seventy-two, originally planned as Parker Estate — a private low-income housing development commissioned by a Hong Kong businessman on the former site of the Taikoo Sugar Refinery. The original developer went bankrupt during the banking crisis of nineteen sixty-five, before a single wall had been built, and the project sat stranded for years before a third company took it over and finished it. The units were some of the cheapest on offer in Hong Kong at the time, and they were bought by the wave of mainland Chinese refugees who had been arriving in the city since the late nineteen forties with very little else. The ""Monster Building"" nickname came much later — the buildings were entirely ordinary housing for several decades before the courtyard photograph went viral in the early two thousands, and films like Transformers and Ghost in the Shell used the complex as a visual shorthand for dystopian density."
Worth sitting with, a bit, is the gap between what the building looks like from the courtyard floor and what it actually is. It was not designed to be arresting. It was designed to be cheap, fast, and large enough to shelter as many families as possible, and it did that job for fifty years before anyone pointed a camera upward.
Quarry Bay Park is a five-minute walk east along King's Road — a modest waterfront park on the harbour's edge that gives you a completely different view of the same neighbourhood, and where the residents of the Monster Building actually go to sit outside.

tours.title: Ngong Ping 360 | 昂坪360
tours.shortDescription: A 5.7-kilometre cable car over Tung Chung Bay to the Tian Tan Buddha — a more cautious machine now, for having had a difficult adolescence.
tours.longDescription:
The gondola crosses 5.7 kilometres between Tung Chung and Ngong Ping in about twenty-five minutes, replacing what was once an hour's drive up the mountain road. The route runs over Tung Chung Bay, past the southern edge of Hong Kong International Airport, and up across the forested slopes of Lantau North Country Park, changing direction twice at angle stations where the cabin briefly detaches from the main cable. The "360" refers to the full circle of view from inside the cabin as it climbs nearly 440 metres to the plateau.

The system opened in September 2006, developed and owned by the MTR Corporation to give fast access to the Tian Tan Buddha and Po Lin Monastery. Its early history is worth knowing honestly: within a year, an empty cabin came loose during after-hours testing and fell fifty metres, closing the system while engineers investigated. Years later, a mechanical fault left around 800 passengers stranded mid-air for over two hours during Lunar New Year. Since then it has run without major incident, added glass-bottomed Crystal cabins, and become one of the island's most popular routes — a more cautious machine for having had a difficult adolescence.

stops.title: Ngong Ping 360 | 昂坪360
stops.transcriptText:
You're suspended above Tung Chung Bay, and somewhere below the waterline, a series of steel towers out in the water are the only things holding the cable up.

You're aboard a gondola somewhere along the five-point-seven-kilometre crossing between Tung Chung and Ngong Ping — a twenty-five-minute journey that replaced what used to be an hour-long drive up the mountain road. Have a look down and out: the route takes you over Tung Chung Bay, past the southern edge of Hong Kong International Airport, and up across the forested slopes of Lantau North Country Park, changing direction twice at angle stations where the cabin briefly detaches from the main cable before continuing on. The "three sixty" in the name refers to exactly what you're looking at — the full circle of view available from inside the cabin as it climbs almost four hundred and forty metres to the plateau ahead.

The system opened to the public in September two thousand and six, developed and owned by the MTR Corporation, designed to give visitors fast access to the Tian Tan Buddha and Po Lin Monastery at Ngong Ping without the long road journey. I always think the early history is worth knowing honestly rather than glossing over: within a year of opening, an empty cabin came loose during after-hours testing and fell fifty metres onto a slope near Tung Chung, closing the whole system indefinitely while engineers investigated. It reopened under tightened safety procedures. Several years later, during Lunar New Year, a mechanical fault left around eight hundred passengers stranded mid-air for over two hours in freezing temperatures, prompting another round of scrutiny and reform.

Since then, it has run without major incident, added glass-bottomed Crystal cabins for the nervous and the brave alike, and become one of the most popular routes on the island. The system above you now is a more cautious machine for having had a difficult adolescence.

At the Ngong Ping end, the Tian Tan Buddha sits at the top of two hundred and sixty-eight steps — the second-largest bronze seated Buddha statue in the world, and worth the climb before you head into the village below.

tours.title: The Peak Tram | 山頂纜車
tours.shortDescription: Asia's first funicular, opened in 1888 — once sold in three rigid classes, the lowest officially shared with livestock, now rebuilt five times into something faster and larger.
tours.longDescription:
The lower terminus on Garden Road is a curved glass-and-steel building housing the ticket hall, queuing area and a small historical gallery with a replica of the original 1888 carriage. The platform sits at the foot of a track that climbs almost 400 metres over just under 1.5 kilometres, at gradients reaching nearly 26 degrees — steep enough that the seats inside are built on a noticeable incline to keep passengers level.

The tram was the idea of Alexander Findlay Smith, a Scottish entrepreneur who had bought the Peak Hotel and wanted an easier way to get guests up the hill; the climb by sedan chair took the best part of an hour, the tram cut it to ten minutes. It opened on 28 May 1888, the first funicular railway in Asia, carrying 800 passengers on its first day. Until 1926, tickets were sold in three rigid classes: first for British colonial officials and Peak residents, second for British military and police, third for everyone else — including, as the original regulations specified, animals. The front two seats stayed reserved for the governor until 1949, and the Peak itself was legally closed to non-Chinese residents until 1930. The tram has since been rebuilt five times, most recently a sixth-generation overhaul completed in 2022, each one faster and larger — and each quietly carrying a little less of what the tram started out as.

stops.title: The Peak Tram | 山頂纜車
stops.transcriptText:
For the first thirty-eight years of its life, the tram you're about to ride had three separate classes — and the lowest one was officially shared with livestock.

The lower terminus sits on Garden Road, a curved glass-and-steel building housing the ticket hall, the queuing area, and the small Peak Tram Historical Gallery near the entrance, where a replica of the original eighteen eighty-eight carriage is on display. The platform itself sits at the foot of a track that climbs almost four hundred metres over just under one and a half kilometres, at gradients reaching nearly twenty-six degrees at the steepest point — steep enough that the seats inside the tram are built on a noticeable incline to keep passengers level as it climbs.

The tram was the idea of Alexander Findlay Smith, a Scottish entrepreneur who had recently bought the Peak Hotel and wanted an easier way to get guests up the hill — the climb by sedan chair took the best part of an hour; the tram cut that to ten minutes. It opened on the twenty-eighth of May, eighteen eighty-eight, the first funicular railway in Asia, and carried eight hundred passengers on its first day alone. From then until nineteen twenty-six, tickets were sold in three rigid classes: first for British colonial officials and Peak residents, second for British military and police, and third for everyone else, including, as the original regulations specified, animals. Even after the class system was formally abolished, the front two seats remained permanently reserved for the governor — marked with a bronze plaque — right through to nineteen forty-nine. The Peak itself stayed legally closed to non-Chinese residents until nineteen thirty.

Well, here's what's quite striking standing here: this tram has been rebuilt five times since then — new cars in nineteen fifty-six, a complete Swiss rebuild in nineteen eighty-nine, and a sixth-generation overhaul completed in twenty twenty-two, taking capacity from one hundred and twenty passengers to two hundred and ten. Each rebuild made the ride faster, larger, and more comfortable. Each one also quietly carried a little less of what the tram started out as.

Two retired tramcars are preserved nearby — one from nineteen fifty-six on a disused spur near Kennedy Road, the other on display at the upper terminus — worth finding if you want to see how far the carriage itself has actually come.

tours.title: PMQ | 元創方
tours.shortDescription: A courtyard with three completely different lives — Sun Yat-sen's school, postwar police quarters, near-demolition, and now a creative hub — almost none of it visible unless you look.
tours.longDescription:
Standing in the central courtyard, two seven-storey residential blocks rise on either side, linked overhead by a glass structure called Qube, with shops, studios and galleries in what used to be flats. The ground beneath was, in 1889, the relocated site of Central School — the first government school in Hong Kong to offer Western-style education to the public, later Queen's College. Sun Yat-sen, later known as the father of modern China, studied here. The school was badly damaged during the occupation and demolished after the war; the site sat empty until 1951, when it was rebuilt as the Police Married Quarters, the first housing of its kind for married junior Chinese officers and their families. Two future Chief Executives grew up in flats here.

The quarters were vacated in 2000, and for fourteen years sat empty — long enough that director Peter Chan used the abandoned corridors as a filming location for the horror anthology Three. The government planned to demolish the site; a petition gathered over 4,000 objections, and in 2009 the buildings were preserved instead, reopening as PMQ in 2014. School, quarters, ruin, film set, near-demolition, creative hub — almost none of that layered history is visible from the courtyard unless you go looking. The Underground Interpretation Area displays the excavated granite foundations of the original school.

stops.title: PMQ | 元創方
stops.transcriptText:
This building has had three completely different lives, and the people who run it now want you to notice all three.

You're standing in the central courtyard, two seven-storey residential blocks rising on either side of you, linked overhead by a glass-covered structure called Qube, with shops, studios, and small galleries occupying what used to be flats. The ground beneath you was, in eighteen eighty-nine, the relocated site of Central School — the first government school in Hong Kong to offer Western-style education to the public — renamed Victoria College, then Queen's College in eighteen ninety-four. Sun Yat-sen, who would later be known as the father of modern China, studied here as a young man. The school was badly damaged during the Japanese occupation and demolished after the war, and the site sat empty until nineteen fifty-one, when it was rebuilt as the Police Married Quarters — the first housing of its kind for married junior Chinese police officers and their families, a hundred and forty single rooms and twenty-eight double rooms, built to boost recruitment after a wave of immigration following the Chinese Civil War. Two future Chief Executives of Hong Kong grew up in flats here.

The quarters were vacated in two thousand, and for fourteen years the buildings sat empty — long enough that in two thousand and two, director Peter Chan used the abandoned corridors as the filming location for a segment of the horror anthology Three. The government planned to demolish the site entirely. A public petition gathered over four thousand objections, and in two thousand and nine the buildings were preserved instead, reopening as PMQ in twenty fourteen.

Well, quite a lot has happened in this courtyard for a place most visitors now treat as a weekend shopping stop. School, quarters, ruin, film set, near-demolition, creative hub — and almost none of that layered history is visible from where you're standing unless you go looking for it.

The Underground Interpretation Area, accessible from the courtyard, displays the excavated granite foundations of the original school — a short, free detour straight down through all three lifetimes at once.

tours.title: Po Lin Monastery | 寶蓮禪寺
tours.shortDescription: For its first eighteen years it was called, quite literally, the Big Hut — a 1906 mountain monastery now overshadowed by the giant Buddha it predates by decades.
tours.longDescription:
The grounds open out on the Ngong Ping plateau, the buildings facing the Tian Tan Buddha across the courtyard: red columns, golden dragons along the roofline, incense thick enough to lose sight of the person beside you on a busy day. Three monks from Jiangsu province founded the site in 1906, drawn by the cool mountain air and the isolation, and for nearly two decades it went by its plain original name before the first abbot renamed it Po Lin — Precious Lotus — in 1924.

Inside, the Grand Hall of Ten Thousand Buddhas holds thousands of small gilded statues, each individually posed against murals of the Buddha's life. The Scripture Library houses something more obscure but more remarkable — a complete Qianlong Tripitaka, over 7,000 volumes of Buddhist scripture printed from wood blocks in the 1730s, the last official wood-block printing project ever undertaken in China. The detail worth carrying is the order of events: the monastery is nearly ninety years older than the Buddha beside it, and most visitors now arrive for the statue and treat the monastery as the thing attached to it — when historically the relationship runs entirely the other way. The Wisdom Path, a fifteen-minute walk away, sets the Heart Sutra across thirty-eight wooden columns arranged as an infinity symbol.
