stops.title: Po Lin Monastery | 寶蓮禪寺
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about this place — for its first eighteen years, it was called, quite literally, the Big Hut.

The monastery grounds open out ahead of you on the Ngong Ping plateau, the buildings arranged on a north-east to south-west axis facing the Tian Tan Buddha across the courtyard: red columns, golden dragons along the roofline, the scent of incense thick enough that you can lose sight of the people standing next to you on a busy day. Three monks from Jiangsu province in mainland China founded the site in nineteen oh six, drawn by the cool mountain air and the isolation, and for nearly two decades the settlement went by its plain, unromantic original name before the first abbot renamed it Po Lin — Precious Lotus — in nineteen twenty-four.

Inside, the Grand Hall of Ten Thousand Buddhas holds exactly what it promises: thousands of small gilded Buddha statues, each individually posed, set against murals depicting scenes from the Buddha's life. The Scripture Library houses something rather more obscure but, if you ask me, more remarkable — a complete set of the Qianlong Tripitaka, over seven thousand volumes of Buddhist scripture printed from wood blocks during the Qing dynasty in the seventeen thirties, the last official wood-block printing project ever undertaken in China.

Actually, the detail worth carrying with you is the order of events. The monastery is nearly ninety years older than the Buddha sitting beside it, and most visitors now arrive for the statue and treat the monastery as the thing attached to it — when historically the relationship runs entirely the other way. Three monks came here first, for the quiet, decades before anyone considered building something this photographable. The older, quieter institution seems entirely unbothered by being overshadowed by what it now hosts.

The Wisdom Path is a fifteen-minute walk from the main grounds — thirty-eight wooden columns inscribed with the Heart Sutra in the calligraphy of Jao Tsung-I, arranged in the shape of an infinity symbol on a hillside, and considerably quieter than anywhere else on the plateau.

tours.title: Sai Wan Swimming Shed | 西環鐘聲泳棚
tours.shortDescription: The last swimming shed left in Hong Kong — a green timber hut on stilts above the Sulphur Channel, belonging to dawn swimmers and sunset photographers who never overlap.
tours.longDescription:
Steps lead down off Victoria Road, steep and a little hidden, dropping through scrubby hillside to a green wooden shed on stilts above the water, with a narrow pier extending into the Sulphur Channel toward Green Island. This is the Chung Sing Swimming Shed — known simply as the Sai Wan Swimming Shed — the only one of its kind left in the territory. The structure is plain: changing rooms, lockers, a small shrine, and the long walkway out to where swimmers enter the water, open from five in the morning until one in the afternoon for a deliberately modest fee.

Sheds like this were once common along the shoreline — bamboo and timber structures offering cheap harbour access when public pools barely existed. The first opened in 1911 at North Point; by the 1950s there were eight or ten. Land reclamation and declining water quality finished off nearly all of them through the 1970s. This one came back in 1988 on Wan Hung-fai's application and has simply continued. The daily rhythm is telling: elderly regulars arrive at dawn for laps in the open water, gone by midday; the pier then sits quiet until the light turns gold, when photographers and couples arrive for the sunset shot. The two groups barely overlap, and the shed seems to belong fully to neither.

stops.title: Sai Wan Swimming Shed | 西環鐘聲泳棚
stops.transcriptText:
It's nineteen eighty-eight, and a man named Wan Hung-fai has just persuaded the government to let him reopen something almost everywhere else in Hong Kong had already let go.

The steps lead down off Victoria Road, steep and a little hidden, dropping you through scrubby hillside to a green wooden shed perched on stilts above the water, with a narrow pier extending out into the Sulphur Channel toward Green Island. This is the Chung Sing Swimming Shed — known simply as the Sai Wan Swimming Shed — and it is the only one of its kind left standing in the territory. The structure is plain: changing rooms, lockers, a small shrine, and the long wooden walkway out to where the swimmers enter the water, open from five in the morning until one in the afternoon, for a membership fee that has stayed deliberately modest.

Swimming sheds like this one were once common along Hong Kong's shoreline — bamboo and timber structures offering cheap access to the harbour at a time when public pools barely existed. The first opened in nineteen eleven at North Point, and by the nineteen fifties there were eight or ten scattered around the coast. Land reclamation and declining water quality finished off nearly all of them through the nineteen seventies. This one came back in nineteen eighty-eight, on Wan Hung-fai's application, and has simply continued since.

I find the daily rhythm here fairly telling. Elderly regulars arrive at dawn for their laps in the open water, year-round, rain or shine, and are largely gone by midday. The pier then sits quiet through the afternoon until the light turns gold, at which point an entirely different crowd arrives — photographers, couples, people chasing the sunset shot that circulates endlessly online. The two groups barely overlap, and the shed seems to belong fully to neither.

The viewpoint from the end of the pier looks straight out toward Green Island and the container terminal beyond — go for the swimmers' hour if you want to see what the place was actually built for.

tours.title: Star Ferry | 天星小輪
tours.shortDescription: Seven minutes on the water with the city on both sides — the mandatory pause that, for over a century, kept two incompatible halves of Hong Kong running alongside each other.
tours.longDescription:
From the upper deck, crossing Victoria Harbour between Tsim Sha Tsui and Central, the view works in both directions. The Kowloon waterfront recedes — the clock tower, the Cultural Centre's curved roof, the Avenue of Stars — while the Hong Kong Island skyline builds from left to right, the mid-levels rising steeply behind the glass towers of Central and the needle of the Bank of China catching the light. Watch the gap between buildings as the ferry moves: the peaks behind the city appear and disappear between the towers as the angle shifts, sharp enough on a clear morning to seem close enough to touch.

This crossing has run continuously, in some form, since 1880 — through the Japanese occupation, the 1966 riots, and reclamation projects that moved both piers. The harbour itself is the most interesting part of what you're seeing: the water between the two halves of the city was, for most of its history, the reason Hong Kong worked the way it did — Kowloon for manufacturing and warehousing, the Island for finance and government, the ferry the necessary seam between them. The seven minutes on the water was never a scenic interlude; it was the mandatory pause that kept two incompatible economies running alongside each other. On any measure, time well spent.

stops.title: Star Ferry | 天星小輪
stops.transcriptText:
The city is on both sides of you now, and for the next seven minutes, there is nothing between you and it but water.
You're on the upper deck, crossing Victoria Harbour from Tsim Sha Tsui to Central — or the reverse, it doesn't matter; the view works in both directions. To your left, if you're heading toward Hong Kong Island, the Kowloon waterfront recedes: the clock tower, the Cultural Centre's curved roof, the Avenue of Stars curving east. Ahead, the Hong Kong Island skyline builds from left to right — the ICC tower in West Kowloon far to your right, and directly ahead, the mid-levels rising steeply behind the glass towers of Central and the needle of the Bank of China catching the light. Have a look at the gap between the buildings as the ferry moves — the peaks behind the city appear and disappear between the towers as the angle shifts, and on a clear morning the mountain behind Central is sharp enough to seem close enough to touch.
This crossing has been running continuously, in some form, since eighteen eighty — through the Japanese occupation, through the nineteen sixty-six riots, through reclamation projects that moved both piers from their original positions. I find the harbour itself the most interesting part of what you're seeing: the water between the two halves of the city was, for most of its history, the reason Hong Kong worked the way it did — Kowloon for manufacturing and warehousing, the Island for finance and government, the ferry the necessary seam between them. The seven minutes on the water was not a scenic interlude; it was the mandatory pause that kept two incompatible economies running alongside each other.
The crossing takes about seven minutes in total. That is, on any measure, time well spent.

tours.title: Star Ferry Pier | 尖沙咀天星碼頭
tours.shortDescription: The Tsim Sha Tsui pier where a five-cent fare rise sparked four nights of riots in 1966 — and why the harbour crossing stays cheap to this day.
tours.longDescription:
The Tsim Sha Tsui pier sits at the western end of the Salisbury Road waterfront, a low green-roofed terminal with the harbour ahead and the Central skyline rising beyond it across the water. The current structure was built in 1957; the ferries loading below are the green-and-white double-deckers that have run this crossing since the 1950s, each named with the word Star — Twinkling Star, Night Star, Golden Star — diesel-powered and double-headed so they never need to turn round. The crossing to Central takes about seven minutes, the upper deck a few dollars more, and the view of Victoria Harbour from the water is the best available at this price anywhere in the world.

The service was founded in 1880 by a Parsee merchant, Dorabjee Naorojee Mithaiwala, running a steam ferry between Tsim Sha Tsui and Pedder Street; the company was renamed Star Ferry in 1898. Until the Cross-Harbour Tunnel opened in 1972, this was the only affordable way for working Kowloon to reach Hong Kong Island — which is why, in spring 1966, a proposed five-cent fare increase, a quarter of the fare, triggered four nights of riots that left one person dead and sent 1,800 to prison. The ferry is affordable today because the people who depended on it in 1966 made the consequences of pricing them out quite clear.

stops.title: Star Ferry Pier | 尖沙咀天星碼頭
stops.transcriptText:
In nineteen sixty-six, a five-cent fare increase on this ferry triggered four nights of riots across Kowloon, left one person dead, and sent eighteen hundred people to prison.
The Tsim Sha Tsui pier sits at the western end of the Salisbury Road waterfront, a low green-roofed terminal building with the harbour directly ahead and the Central skyline rising beyond it across the water. The current structure was built in nineteen fifty-seven; the ferries loading at the lower level are the green-and-white double-deckers that have been running this crossing since the nineteen fifties, each one named with the word Star — Twinkling Star, Night Star, Golden Star — diesel-powered, double-headed so they never need to turn round. The crossing to Central takes about seven minutes, the upper deck costs a few dollars more than the lower, and the view of Victoria Harbour from the water is the best available at this price anywhere in the world.
The service was founded in eighteen eighty by a Parsee merchant called Dorabjee Naorojee Mithaiwala, who ran a steam ferry between Tsim Sha Tsui and Pedder Street in Central. The company was acquired in eighteen ninety-eight and renamed the Star Ferry Company. For nearly a century, until the Cross-Harbour Tunnel opened in nineteen seventy-two, this was the only affordable way for the working population of Kowloon to reach Hong Kong Island — which is why, in the spring of nineteen sixty-six, a proposed five-cent increase in the fare — twenty-five per cent — produced a response that surprised the colonial government considerably.
I always think the price of the ticket is the detail that makes the history land. The ferry is affordable today because the people who depended on it in nineteen sixty-six made the consequences of pricing them out quite clear. The romantic harbour crossing and the practical economics are the same crossing — the same boat, the same seven minutes, the same water.
Take it. The upper deck, if there's space, gives you the full harbour panorama, and the view back toward Tsim Sha Tsui as you pull away — the peninsula, the clock tower, the Cultural Centre — is rather good for something that costs less than a cup of tea.

tours.title: Tai Kwun | 大館
tours.shortDescription: A compound off-limits to the public for 160 years — police station, magistracy and prison in one enclosure, now opened, with two floating black towers above the old jail yard.
tours.longDescription:
Through the main gate on Hollywood Road, the Parade Ground opens at the heart of the complex, flanked by Victorian granite and red brick on three sides: the former police station ahead, the Central Magistracy to the right, the prison buildings to the left. Above the rooflines hover two black structures — Herzog & de Meuron's JC Contemporary and JC Cube, clad in recycled cast aluminium chosen because it is inexpensive in Hong Kong and reads clearly against the brick. The contrast is deliberate: what seems to float above the old prison yard was designed, in the architects' words, by accepting history as it exists rather than interpreting it.

The compound was established in 1841 by Captain William Caine, who served at once as Chief Magistrate, Head of Police and Head of Gaol — a remarkable concentration of authority in one man on one site. The buildings, constructed between 1864 and 1925, operated continuously for 160 years as Hong Kong's integrated law-enforcement hub. Among those held in Victoria Prison were Sun Yat-sen, briefly in 1896, and Ho Chi Minh in the early 1930s. Decommissioned in 2006, the compound was restored by the Hong Kong Jockey Club over twelve years at HK$3.7 billion, reopening in 2018. The cell blocks around the prison yard are now exhibition spaces, the cells themselves with their original dimensions and hardware intact.

stops.title: Tai Kwun | 大館
stops.transcriptText:
This compound was off limits to the public for a hundred and sixty years. You are in it now — which is, in its own way, the point.
Walk through the main gate on Hollywood Road and into the Parade Ground — the large open space at the heart of the complex, flanked by Victorian granite and red brick on three sides: the former police station block ahead of you, the Central Magistracy to your right, and the prison buildings to your left. Above and beyond the prison yard, the two black structures hovering over the rooflines are new — Herzog & de Meuron's JC Contemporary and JC Cube, clad in recycled cast aluminium chosen specifically because it is inexpensive in Hong Kong and reads clearly against the Victorian brick. The effect is deliberate contrast: what looks almost like it is floating above the old prison yard was designed, in Herzog & de Meuron's own words, by accepting history as it exists rather than interpreting it.
The compound was established in eighteen forty-one by Captain William Caine, who served simultaneously as Chief Magistrate, Head of Police, and Head of Gaol — a remarkable concentration of authority in one man, on one site. The buildings you're standing among were constructed between eighteen sixty-four and nineteen twenty-five and operated continuously for a hundred and sixty years as Hong Kong's integrated law enforcement hub: police station, court, and prison in a single enclosed compound. Among those held in Victoria Prison over that period were Sun Yat-sen, briefly, in eighteen ninety-six, and Ho Chi Minh, detained in Hong Kong at some point in the early nineteen thirties.
The compound was decommissioned in two thousand and six, and the Hong Kong Jockey Club spent HK$3.7 billion and twelve years restoring it before it opened in May two thousand and eighteen. I find that history — enforcement concentrated here for one hundred and sixty years, then opened — the more interesting thing about the place than the architecture, though the architecture is rather good.
The prison yard is behind the main block to your left — the yard where prisoners took exercise — and the cell blocks around it are now exhibition spaces. The cells themselves, with their original dimensions and hardware intact, are worth finding.

tours.title: Temple Street Night Market | 廟街夜市
tours.shortDescription: The "Poor Man's Night Club" — the last surviving example in Hong Kong of an outdoor world of fortune tellers, opera singers and street food around a seafarers' temple.
tours.longDescription:
The market runs north to south through Yau Ma Tei and Jordan, from Kansu Street down to Jordan Road, with the Tin Hau Temple roughly in the middle splitting it in two. The southern section is the busiest — clothing, gadgets and watches on folding tables under fluorescent light — while the northern section, beyond the temple, is quieter and older in feel: fortune tellers clustered under small lamps, Cantonese opera singers performing on a temporary stage after dark for a crowd that gathers and disperses.

The Tin Hau Temple is the reason any of this exists. Built in the 1860s, when Yau Ma Tei was still on the harbour front, it was a gathering place for the fishing communities who prayed to Tin Hau, goddess of the sea, before leaving port. By the 1920s an informal bazaar had grown up around it — hawkers selling food, daily goods and snake soup under banyan trees — and entertainers followed. The old nickname was the "Poor Man's Night Club": somewhere working Kowloon could eat, watch opera, hear their fortune read and stay out late without paying for a roof. That form of improvised, outdoor, collective entertainment has largely vanished from modern cities; this is, by the Tourism Board's reckoning, the last surviving example of its kind in the territory.

stops.title: Temple Street Night Market | 廟街夜市
stops.transcriptText:
This is Temple Street Night Market. You have probably seen it in a film — and it looks exactly the way it does on screen, which is a bit unusual for a place this old.
The market runs north to south through Yau Ma Tei and Jordan, stretching from Kansu Street in the north down to Jordan Road, with the Tin Hau Temple sitting roughly in the middle and splitting the market into two sections. The southern section, nearer Jordan, is the busiest with clothing, accessories, gadgets, and watches laid out on folding tables under fluorescent lights. The northern section, beyond the temple, is quieter and older in feel — fortune tellers clustered under small lamps near the temple forecourt, Cantonese opera singers performing on a temporary stage after dark for a small crowd that gathers and disperses as the evening moves.
The Tin Hau Temple is the reason any of this exists. Built in the eighteen sixties, when Yau Ma Tei was still on the harbour front, it was a gathering place for the fishing communities who worked the water — people who prayed to Tin Hau, goddess of the sea, before leaving port. By the nineteen twenties an informal bazaar had grown up around it: hawkers selling food, daily goods, Chinese medicine oil, and snake soup under the shade of giant banyan trees. Entertainers followed — jugglers, opera singers, fortune tellers — and over the following decades the market absorbed wave after wave of immigrants from the mainland, each bringing skills and nothing much else.
"The old nickname for this place was the ""Poor Man's Night Club,"" and if you ask me that phrase still captures it correctly. It was somewhere the working population of Kowloon could eat, watch opera, hear their fortune read, and stay out late without paying for a roof over the proceedings. That form of urban entertainment — improvised, outdoors, cheap, collective — has largely disappeared from modern cities. This is, according to the Hong Kong Tourism Board, the last surviving example of its kind in the territory."
The bird fortune-telling stalls near the temple are worth finding if they're still operating: a caged bird selects a card from a pack, and the card carries your fortune. Whether you believe it or not, the method is several centuries old and takes about thirty seconds.

tours.title: The Henderson | 美利道2號
tours.shortDescription: Zaha Hadid Architects' bud-shaped tower of 4,000 curved glass panels — on the most expensive plot of land ever recorded, designed by an architect who never saw it bloom.
tours.longDescription:
On Murray Road, with I. M. Pei's Bank of China Tower behind and Norman Foster's HSBC building downhill, The Henderson completes a small patch of Central holding three landmark towers from three architectural eras. It is the newest: thirty-six storeys, 190 metres, completed in 2024, its facade made from more than 4,000 panels of curved glass manufactured in Germany, with roughly a thousand different curvatures among them. The shape is modelled on a Bauhinia bud just beginning to open — the same flower that appears on Hong Kong's flag.

That detail is pointed, because this exact ground has history with the flower: the Bauhinia x blakeana was first propagated in the city's botanic gardens directly above this site. A car park stood here until 2017, when Henderson Land bought the plot for roughly three billion US dollars — by some calculations the most expensive piece of land ever recorded anywhere. The building was designed by Zaha Hadid Architects, the firm's first commercial project in Hong Kong, though Hadid died in 2016, three years before construction began. A building shaped like a flower opening, designed by someone who never got to watch it bloom, standing on the ground where the flower itself was first grown.

stops.title: The Henderson | 美利道2號
stops.transcriptText:
Look at the curve of the glass. None of it is flat.

You're on Murray Road, with I. M. Pei's Bank of China Tower behind you and Norman Foster's HSBC building a short walk downhill — which means you're standing in a small patch of Central that now holds three landmark buildings from three completely different architectural eras, each one a different answer to the same question of what a tower here should look like. The Henderson is the newest of the three: thirty-six storeys, a hundred and ninety metres tall, completed in twenty twenty-four, its facade made from more than four thousand panels of curved glass, manufactured in Germany, with roughly a thousand different curvatures among them. The shape is deliberate — the whole building is modelled on a Bauhinia bud just beginning to open, the same flower that appears on Hong Kong's own flag.

That detail is rather pointed, actually, because this exact patch of ground has history with the flower. The Bauhinia x blakeana — the Hong Kong orchid tree — was first propagated in the city's botanic gardens directly above this site, long before anyone built anything taller than a car park here. And a car park is precisely what stood here until twenty seventeen, when Henderson Land Development bought the plot for roughly three billion US dollars, a price that made it, by some calculations, the most expensive piece of land ever recorded anywhere in the world.

The building was designed by Zaha Hadid Architects — her firm's first commercial project in Hong Kong — though Hadid herself died in twenty sixteen, three years before construction even began. She never saw it finished. If you ask me, that's the detail worth sitting with: a building shaped like a flower opening, designed by someone who never got to watch it bloom, standing on the ground where the flower itself was first grown, between a triangle from nineteen ninety and a steel frame from nineteen eighty-five.

Chater Garden is directly across the road — worth a few minutes' walk, not least because it's the quietest place nearby to actually look up at all three buildings at once without craning your neck on a busy pavement.

tours.title: Tian Tan Buddha | 天壇大佛
tours.shortDescription: The Big Buddha — 34 metres of bronze atop 268 steps, conceived as an act of devotion and entangled, before a single piece was cast, in handover-era politics.
tours.longDescription:
At the base of the steps, the bronze figure sits on a three-tiered platform, 268 stone steps between you and the statue — a climb the monastery describes as a journey symbolising the path to enlightenment. The statue stands 34 metres tall including its base, weighs over 250 metric tons, and was assembled from 202 separate bronze pieces over a steel internal frame, on a lotus throne surrounded by six smaller figures known as the Offering of the Six Devas, each presenting a gift representing one of the six perfections.

The idea began in 1979, when the abbot of Po Lin Monastery visited large Buddha statues in Japan and Taiwan and decided Hong Kong should have one of comparable scale. The name and base come from a separate trip to the Temple of Heaven — Tian Tan — in Beijing, which the platform echoes. The funding didn't go to plan: initial backing came from Taiwan and Japan, but during the handover negotiations the head of the Xinhua News Agency intervened to shift funding toward mainland China, with construction overseen by a factory connected to the People's Liberation Army. A statue conceived as an act of devotion became, before a single piece of bronze was cast, entangled in the politics of the moment it was built in. Inside, three halls hold a relic said to be a fragment of the Buddha's cremated remains and a bell that rings 108 times a day.

stops.title: Tian Tan Buddha | 天壇大佛
stops.transcriptText:
If you laid every weld used to build this statue end to end, they would stretch five kilometres.

You're at the base of the steps, looking up at the bronze figure seated on its three-tiered platform, two hundred and sixty-eight stone steps between you and the base of the statue itself — a climb the monastery describes as a journey symbolising the path to enlightenment, which is either a lovely piece of framing or a useful way to make the climb feel purposeful, possibly both. The statue stands thirty-four metres tall including its base, weighs over two hundred and fifty metric tons, and was assembled from two hundred and two separate bronze pieces over a steel internal frame, sitting on a lotus throne surrounded by six smaller bronze figures known as the Offering of the Six Devas, each presenting a different gift representing one of the six perfections needed for enlightenment.

The idea began in nineteen seventy-nine, when the abbot of Po Lin Monastery visited large Buddha statues in Japan and Taiwan and decided Hong Kong should have one of comparable scale. The name and the design of the base come from a separate trip to the Temple of Heaven — Tian Tan — in Beijing, which the statue's platform deliberately echoes. Worth knowing: the funding didn't go entirely to plan. Initial backing came from Taiwan and Japan, but during the negotiations over Hong Kong's handover, the head of the Xinhua News Agency intervened to shift the project's funding toward mainland China instead, with construction subsequently overseen by a factory connected to the People's Liberation Army. A statue conceived as an act of devotion became, before a single piece of bronze was cast, entangled in the politics of the moment it was built in.

Inside the statue, three exhibition halls hold a relic said to be a fragment of the historical Buddha's cremated remains, and a large bell that rings every seven minutes, one hundred and eight times a day — said to release one hundred and eight worldly troubles with each toll.

Po Lin Monastery sits a short walk from the base of the steps — older than the statue by decades, and rather quieter, with a vegetarian kitchen worth the detour if you're hungry after the climb.

tours.title: Victoria Harbour Night View | 維港夜景
tours.shortDescription: The best argument Hong Kong has for itself after dark — a 180-degree skyline where the tallest tower of all is always standing somewhere behind you.
tours.longDescription:
On the Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, midway between the Hong Kong Cultural Centre and the Clock Tower, the full width of Victoria Harbour opens ahead and the Hong Kong Island skyline runs almost 180 degrees from the Western District to Wan Chai. Three buildings are worth picking out. Slightly left of centre, the tallest cluster is Two International Finance Centre — 415 metres, eighty-eight floors, completed in 2003, recognisable by its smooth tapered form. To its right, the angular tower with the sharp triangular profile is I. M. Pei's Bank of China Tower, completed in 1990, its topping-out ceremony deliberately held on 8 August 1988 — eight-eight-eighty-eight, the most auspicious date of the century.

Further right, toward Wan Chai, a slim triangular tower with a coloured glow along its edges is Central Plaza — 374 metres, the tallest building in Asia for four years after it opened in 1992. And behind you, though you can't see it, stands the tallest of all: the International Commerce Centre across the harbour in Kowloon, at 484 metres — the one building in this skyline that doesn't appear in your own photograph of it. That's rather the point of the view: you can never quite see the whole skyline from any single spot, because the tallest building in it is always standing somewhere behind you. Stay until eight for the nightly Symphony of Lights.

stops.title: Victoria Harbour Night View | 維港夜景
stops.transcriptText:
By day this is just water with traffic on it. After dark, it becomes the best argument Hong Kong has for itself.

You're on the Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, roughly midway between the Hong Kong Cultural Centre and the Clock Tower, with the full width of Victoria Harbour open in front of you and the Hong Kong Island skyline running almost a hundred and eighty degrees from the Western District on your right to Wan Chai on your left. Three buildings are worth picking out by name. Slightly left of centre, the tallest cluster on the island is Two International Finance Centre — four hundred and fifteen metres, eighty-eight floors, completed in two thousand and three, and recognisable by its smooth, tapered glass form rising above everything around it. To its right, the angular, faceted tower with the sharp triangular profile is the Bank of China Tower, designed by I. M. Pei and completed in nineteen ninety, its topping-out ceremony deliberately held on the eighth of August, nineteen eighty-eight — eight eight eighty-eight — considered the most auspicious date of the century.

Further right again, toward Wan Chai, look for a slim triangular tower with a distinctive coloured glow along its edges after dark — that's Central Plaza, three hundred and seventy-four metres, the tallest building in Asia for four years after it opened in nineteen ninety-two, until Hong Kong's own Two IFC eventually overtook it. And behind you, though you can't see it from here, the tallest of the lot — the International Commerce Centre across the harbour in Kowloon, at four hundred and eighty-four metres — is the one building in this skyline that doesn't appear in your own photograph of it.

I always think that's rather the point of this view: you can never quite see the whole skyline from any single spot, because the tallest building in it is always standing somewhere behind you.

Stay until eight o'clock if you can. A Symphony of Lights runs nightly from the promenade you're standing on, the towers in front of you lit in sequence for about ten minutes — worth catching once, though the buildings themselves are the real reason to be here.

tours.title: Victoria Peak | 太平山頂
tours.shortDescription: The view everyone climbs for — and a summit you still can't reach, fenced off above a mountain that has kept some ground reserved for over a century.
tours.longDescription:
Sky Terrace 428 is the open-air deck on top of the Peak Tower, 428 metres above sea level and advertised as the highest point in Hong Kong you can actually stand on. The genuine summit of Victoria Peak is 124 metres further up, at 552 metres, fenced off and occupied entirely by telecommunications masts — off-limits under telecommunications law rather than heritage protection. From the southern edge the whole of Victoria Harbour opens below: Central's towers crowded along the waterfront, the harbour threaded with ferries, and Kowloon spreading flat toward the hills of the New Territories. The eastern corner gives the clearest line down, where skyscrapers, harbour and coastline line up in one frame; after dark the same view becomes the lit skyline, with the Symphony of Lights nightly at eight.

The Peak has been selective about who gets to be where for a long time. British residents built summer houses up here from the 1860s, reached by sedan chair, and from 1904 the Peak District Reservation Ordinance restricted residence to non-Chinese, framed around plague-era public-health concerns though historians still argue whether disease or exclusion was the real motive. Robert Hotung became the first Chinese resident permitted in the 1920s. Nobody is excluded from visiting now — around seven million people a year ride up — but the mountain still hasn't entirely let go of the habit of keeping some ground reserved. It has simply changed what the reservation is for.

stops.title: Victoria Peak | 太平山頂
stops.transcriptText:
Look up, past the terrace railing, to the ridge still rising behind you. You have not reached the top of this mountain, and you are not allowed to.

You're standing on Sky Terrace 428 — the open-air deck on top of the Peak Tower, four hundred and twenty-eight metres above sea level, advertised as the highest point in Hong Kong you can actually stand on. The genuine summit of Victoria Peak is a hundred and twenty-four metres further up, at five hundred and fifty-two metres, fenced off and occupied entirely by telecommunications masts, off-limits to the public under telecommunications law rather than any heritage protection. You'll notice it from here as the wooded rise behind the tower, unremarkable to look at, legally closed regardless.

The view in front of you is the reason everyone makes the climb. From the southern edge of the terrace, the whole of Victoria Harbour opens out below: Central's towers crowded along the waterfront, the harbour itself threaded with ferries, and Kowloon spreading out flat on the far side toward the hills of the New Territories. Have a look toward the eastern corner of the deck for the clearest line down to the water — that's where the cluster of skyscrapers, the harbour, and Kowloon's coastline all line up in one frame, and it's the spot most of the photographs you've seen of this view were actually taken from. After dark, the same view turns into the city's lit skyline, building by building, with the Symphony of Lights running nightly at eight.

The Peak has been selective about who gets to be where for a long time. British residents began building summer houses up here from the eighteen sixties, reaching them by sedan chair, giving their bungalows whimsical names — the Eyrie, the Austin Arms. From nineteen oh four, the Peak District Reservation Ordinance formally restricted residence to non-Chinese, framed at the time around public health concerns following a severe outbreak of plague, though historians still argue today whether disease or social exclusion was the real motive. The law stood for twenty-six years. Robert Hotung became the first Chinese resident permitted on the Peak in the nineteen twenties, allowed through because he was, by the standards of the period, classified as mixed race.

A bit of context worth holding onto: nobody is excluded from visiting now — around seven million people a year ride up for exactly this view — but the mountain still hasn't entirely let go of the habit of keeping some ground reserved. It has simply changed what the reservation is for.

Lugard Road circles the summit at a lower elevation, a level paved promenade built between nineteen thirteen and nineteen twenty-one specifically for leisurely walking — quieter than the terrace, and the best way to see the houses that were once entirely off-limits to almost everyone now visiting them by tram.

tours.title: Western Market | 西港城
tours.shortDescription: The oldest market building still standing in Hong Kong — a 1906 Edwardian hall that has spent its whole life being repurposed by a city that keeps moving the water away from it.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Des Voeux Road and Morrison Street in Sheung Wan, Western Market is a red-brick and granite structure in the Queen Anne Revival style, completed in 1906 — rounded corner towers, arched windows, banded brickwork across the upper storeys. It is symmetrical and almost formal, more civic institution than marketplace, which makes sense: it was built as the northern extension of an existing food market, on the site of the old Harbour Office. The original southern hall of 1858 was demolished in 1981 for the MTR Island Line, leaving this the sole survivor — the oldest market structure still standing in Hong Kong.

When it opened, the building stood on a working harbourfront crowded with dockers, sailors and ferry passengers. That water is long gone — decades of reclamation pushed the shoreline back, and the harbour view is now a flyover and the Shun Tak Centre. The market operated as a working food hall until 1988, was declared a monument in 1990, and reopened as a heritage shopping complex: fabric merchants upstairs, craft stalls and a restaurant elsewhere, and a top-floor ballroom where original Edwardian ironwork sits beneath a modern chandelier. As of late 2025 it is partway through its third major renovation in under forty years — having spent its whole existence being repurposed by a city that keeps moving the water further away from it.

stops.title: Western Market | 西港城
stops.transcriptText:
Look at the corner tower. The granite staircase inside it spirals up through four floors of a building that used to face the sea.

The building occupies the corner of Des Voeux Road and Morrison Street in Sheung Wan — a red brick and granite structure in the Queen Anne Revival style, completed in nineteen oh six, with rounded corner towers, arched windows, and banded brickwork running across the upper storeys. You'll notice the building is symmetrical, almost formal, more like a civic institution than a marketplace — which makes sense, because it was built as the northern extension of an existing food market on the site of the old Harbour Office, demolished to make way for it. The original southern market hall on Queen's Road, built in eighteen fifty-eight, was demolished in nineteen eighty-one for the MTR Island Line, leaving this building as the sole survivor — the oldest market structure still standing in Hong Kong.

When it opened, the building stood on a working harbourfront, crowded with dockers, sailors, and ferry passengers moving between the water and the city. That water is long gone. Decades of land reclamation pushed the shoreline back, and the building's harbour view is now a flyover and the Shun Tak Centre. The market operated as a working food hall until nineteen eighty-eight, was declared a monument in nineteen ninety, and reopened the following year as a heritage shopping complex: fabric merchants on the upper floor, craft stalls and a Chinese restaurant elsewhere, and a ballroom on the top floor where the original Edwardian ironwork sits a bit incongruously beneath a large modern chandelier.

The building is, as of late twenty twenty-five, partway through its third major renovation in under forty years — expected to reopen with a new look in around two years. It has spent its whole existence being repurposed by a city that keeps moving the water further away from it.

The Sheung Wan tram stop is directly outside on Des Voeux Road, and the route west toward Kennedy Town runs along streets that were themselves reclaimed from the same retreating harbour — worth riding if you want to see how far the water has actually moved.

tours.title: Xiqu Centre | 戲曲中心
tours.shortDescription: A thousand-seat theatre suspended 27 metres in the air — the world's first purpose-built home for Chinese opera, and the last work of an architect who never heard a note inside it.
tours.longDescription:
Standing in the open-air atrium beneath the main structure — a column-free public space — you're under a building whose theatre isn't on the ground at all. The site was too small to put a thousand-seat hall at street level without blocking everyone walking through the district, so the architects suspended the entire Grand Theatre twenty-seven metres in the air, leaving the ground floor free. Above, the facade wraps the structure in curved aluminium fins arranged to look like a lantern glowing behind a beaded stage curtain, which appears to part at each of the four corners.

The Xiqu Centre opened in January 2019, the first of seventeen planned venues in the West Kowloon Cultural District, built to give Cantonese and Chinese opera — Xiqu, a tradition reaching back to the Ming dynasty — a purpose-built home for the first time anywhere in the world. Inside, alongside the suspended Grand Theatre, a 200-seat Tea House Theatre lets audiences drink tea while watching, much closer to how Chinese opera was originally experienced. The building was designed by Bing Thom, a Hong Kong-born architect celebrated for how his concert halls sounded inside. He died suddenly in 2016, partway through this project — his last major work, meant to give new life to a five-hundred-year-old art form. He never heard a single performance inside the theatre he had lifted into the air.

stops.title: Xiqu Centre | 戲曲中心
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about this building — the theatre inside it isn't on the ground. It's twenty-seven metres above your head.

You're standing in the open-air atrium beneath the main structure, a column-free public space that exists because the site was simply too small to put a thousand-seat theatre at street level without blocking everyone trying to walk through the district. So the architects suspended the entire Grand Theatre in the air instead, leaving the ground floor free for exactly the space you're standing in now. Above you, the building's facade wraps the whole structure in curved aluminium fins, arranged to look like a lantern glowing behind a beaded stage curtain — and at each of the four corners, that curtain appears to part, drawing you in toward the entrance.

The Xiqu Centre opened in January twenty nineteen, the first of seventeen venues planned for the wider West Kowloon Cultural District, built specifically to give Cantonese and Chinese opera — known as Xiqu, a tradition reaching back to the Ming dynasty — a purpose-built home for the first time anywhere in the world. Inside, alongside the suspended Grand Theatre, there's a smaller, two-hundred-seat Tea House Theatre, where audiences sit and drink tea while watching performances, much closer to how Chinese opera was originally experienced centuries ago, long before anyone built a thousand-seat hall for it.

I find the building's own history worth holding onto, fairly quietly, alongside everything else. It was designed by Bing Thom, a Hong Kong-born architect who spent his career on concert halls celebrated for how they sounded inside. He died suddenly in twenty sixteen, partway through this project — his last major work, a building meant to give new life to a five-hundred-year-old art form. He never heard a single performance inside the theatre he'd lifted into the air.

The sky gardens on the upper floors look out toward Victoria Harbour and the skyline beyond — worth finding before a performance, particularly if the Tea House Theatre has anything on that evening.

tours.title: Yuen Po Street Bird Garden | 園圃街雀鳥花園
tours.shortDescription: An almost-quiet corner of Mong Kok entered through a pai lou gateway — where elderly men still gather at dawn to "walk the birds," a ritual older than the city around it.
tours.longDescription:
Entered through a traditional pai lou gateway on Boundary Street, the garden is a 3,000-square-metre walled space of courtyards, shaded walkways and moon gateways, with stalls selling songbirds, parrots, birdcages and feed along the perimeter. The sounds are entirely different from the markets a few blocks south: birdsong, the low murmur of elderly men in conversation, the squeak of a bamboo cage being adjusted. The garden was built in 1997 to replace the original Bird Street on Hong Lok Street, demolished in the early 1990s for what is now the Langham Place mall, and cost HK$29 million to construct.

Bird-keeping in Cantonese culture traces back to the Qing dynasty, when carrying a songbird on your morning walk was a sign of refinement and leisure — the bird provided company, the walk a chance to meet other keepers and compare birds. That practice, "walking the birds," continues here every morning around six, when elderly men hang their cages on the garden's hooks and sit together while the birds sing. At one end is the workshop of Chan Lok-choi, Hong Kong's last traditional birdcage maker — in his seventies, still working in bamboo using techniques passed down through generations, producing cages now more likely kept as decorative objects than used for birds.

stops.title: Yuen Po Street Bird Garden | 園圃街雀鳥花園
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about this corner of Mong Kok — it's almost quiet.
The garden is entered through a traditional pai lou gateway on Boundary Street — a formal Chinese archway that marks the transition from the street into a three-thousand-square-metre walled garden of courtyards, shaded walkways, moon gateways dividing the space into smaller enclosures, and stalls selling songbirds, parrots, birdcages, and feed along the perimeter. The sounds are entirely different from the markets a few blocks south: birdsong, the low murmur of conversation between elderly men, the occasional squeak of a bamboo cage being adjusted. The garden was built in nineteen ninety-seven as a replacement for the original Bird Street on Hong Lok Street — demolished in the early nineties to make way for what is now the Langham Place shopping mall — and cost HK$29 million to construct.
"Bird-keeping in Cantonese culture traces back to the Qing dynasty, when carrying a songbird in a cage on your morning walk was a sign of refinement and leisure — the bird provided company, and the walk provided an opportunity to meet other keepers and compare birds. That practice, known as ""walking the birds,"" continues here every morning around six, when elderly men arrive with their cages, hang them from the garden's designated hooks, and sit with each other while the birds sing. It is one of the more particular rituals of Hong Kong life, and rather easier to encounter here than almost anywhere else in the city."
At one end of the garden you'll find the workshop of Chan Lok-choi, Hong Kong's last traditional birdcage maker — seventy-six years old, still working in bamboo using techniques passed down through generations, producing cages that are now more likely to be kept as decorative objects than used for birds. If you fancy a few minutes watching him work, the craft itself is worth the pause.
The Flower Market on Flower Market Road runs directly alongside the garden's eastern wall — an easy continuation, and in the early morning especially, the combination of birdsong and cut flowers is about as far from the rest of Mong Kok as it's possible to get without leaving the district.

tours.title: Central–Mid-Levels Escalators
tours.shortDescription: The world's longest outdoor covered escalator — eight hundred metres of moving walkway climbing the slope of Hong Kong Island, reversing direction with the commute, that accidentally created the SoHo nightlife district.
tours.longDescription:
The Central–Mid-Levels Escalator is a single reversible track — sixteen escalators and three inclined walkways strung together by footbridges — running eight hundred metres and a hundred and thirty-five metres of elevation up the hillside, free of charge, carrying around ninety-nine thousand trips a day. It runs downhill until ten in the morning to serve the commute, then uphill until midnight. There simply wasn't room in the narrow streets for two parallel tracks, so the engineers built one that switches direction.

It opened in 1993 as a fix for Mid-Levels traffic congestion, a job it never really did — the roads above stayed jammed. What it did instead was bring footfall to the quiet lanes of Staunton and Shelley Streets and turn them into SoHo, Hong Kong's bar-and-restaurant district. A piece of infrastructure that failed at its stated purpose and accidentally created one of the city's most distinctive neighbourhoods.

stops.title: Central–Mid-Levels Escalators
stops.caption: Step onto the Central–Mid-Levels Escalator heading uphill from Queen's Road Central.
stops.transcriptText:
Every morning until ten o'clock, this escalator runs downhill. After ten, it runs uphill until midnight. There is only one track.

You're on the escalator system somewhere between Queen's Road Central below and Conduit Road above — eight hundred metres of covered moving walkway climbing a hundred and thirty-five metres up the slope of Hong Kong Island, taking roughly twenty minutes end to end. The system is made of sixteen separate escalators and three inclined walkways, not one continuous belt, connected by footbridges and short flat sections where the street crosses underneath. The whole thing runs free of charge, carries around ninety-nine thousand trips a day, and was built at a cost of HK$245 million — six times the original estimate.

The single-track design is the engineering constraint that shaped how the whole system works. There simply wasn't room in the narrow streets to build two parallel tracks, so the engineers built one reversible track instead, switching direction to serve the morning commute downhill and the rest of the day uphill. If you're on it now, you're going up, which is almost certainly why you're here.

The system opened in October nineteen ninety-three, originally planned as a traffic congestion solution for the Mid-Levels. It didn't really work as that — the roads above are still congested, and the traffic studies that justified the cost never quite materialised. What it did instead was rather more interesting: the footfall it brought to the quiet residential lanes of Staunton Street and Shelley Street turned them into SoHo, Hong Kong's bar and restaurant district, which now lines both sides of the escalator for several hundred metres. The system failed at its original purpose and accidentally created one of the city's most distinctive neighbourhoods.

Watch for the Hollywood Road crossing, roughly halfway up — the footbridge gives you a view east and west along one of Hong Kong's oldest streets, and the Man Mo Temple is thirty seconds' walk to the left if you step off.

tours.title: Hung Shing Temple (Ap Lei Chau)
tours.shortDescription: One of Hong Kong's oldest temples, built in 1773 by the Ap Lei Chau fishing community for the Tang-dynasty god of weather and the sea — still facing the water, though land reclamation has pushed the harbour back.
tours.longDescription:
Hung Shing Temple sits on the eastern shore of Ap Lei Chau — a low, dark building with a green-tiled roof, ceramic figures along the ridge, and rare fung shui timber poles in the forecourt. Built in 1773 by the local fishing community, it is one of Hong Kong's oldest temples and one of very few to keep its original fung shui orientation, facing the sea so the deity inside could watch over the water. The bell in the main hall was cast the year the temple was built, the oldest surviving object and the clearest evidence of the founding date.

Hung Shing was a Tang-dynasty official famed for astronomy, geography, and weather forecasting — a god who could warn of a storm was a practical asset to fishermen, and his temples spread across the Pearl River Delta with the fleets. Until the 1950s the sea came right to the door; reclamation in 1950 and 1985 pushed the harbour back, so the buildings now between the temple and the water once were the sea itself. The temple still faces that direction. The water has simply moved.

stops.title: Hung Shing Temple (Ap Lei Chau)
stops.caption: Stand in the doorway of Hung Shing Temple on Hung Shing Street, Ap Lei Chau.
stops.transcriptText:
Stand in the doorway and listen. On a still day, you can hear the harbour from inside this temple — which is exactly what the people who built it intended.

The temple sits on Hung Shing Street on the eastern shore of Ap Lei Chau, a low, dark building with a green-tiled roof, ceramic figures along the ridge, and rare fung shui timber poles standing in the forecourt. Built in seventeen seventy-three by the local fishing community, it is one of Hong Kong's oldest temples and one of very few that still retains its original fung shui orientation — designed to face the sea, so that the deity inside would have a clear view of the water the fishermen sailed on. Have a look at the bell hanging inside the main hall: it was cast in the same year the temple was built, making it the oldest surviving object here and the clearest evidence of the founding date.

Hung Shing was, in his mortal life, a Tang Dynasty official celebrated for his mastery of astronomy, geography, and most importantly weather forecasting. That last skill was what the fishing community valued — a god who could read the sky and warn you before a storm was not a metaphysical comfort but a practical asset. He was deified after his death, and temples dedicated to him spread across the Pearl River Delta with the fishing fleets.

Worth knowing: the temple was designed to face open water, and until the nineteen fifties, the sea came right to its door. Land reclamation in nineteen fifty and again in nineteen eighty-five pushed the harbour back, and the buildings now standing between the temple and the water — the Marina Habitat, the Municipal Building — were once the sea itself. The temple still faces that direction. The water has simply moved.

The Hung Shing Festival on the thirteenth day of the second lunar month fills this waterfront with Cantonese opera performances staged for the god — one of the more particular local events on the Hong Kong calendar, and worth timing a visit around if you can.

tours.title: Jamia Mosque
tours.shortDescription: Hong Kong Island's oldest mosque and only minaret, set back off Shelley Street — built in 1915 on a site that Muslim sailors, soldiers and merchants have prayed on since 1850.
tours.longDescription:
Jamia Mosque sits off Shelley Street behind a green archway and a flight of stairs, its green-and-white prayer hall topped by a single minaret carrying a crescent and star — the only minaret on any mosque on Hong Kong Island. The present building went up in 1915, funded entirely by a Bombay merchant, Haji Mohamed Essack Elias, to replace an earlier structure. A mosque has stood here since 1850, when the colonial government leased the land to the Muslim community — among the earliest formal recognitions of any non-Christian faith in the colony.

The first congregation mixed South Asian merchants, Muslim soldiers in the British military, and lascars — sailors on British ships docked between voyages — and the mosque was long known as the Lascar Temple. The pulpit inside was donated in 1915 by a local Chinese Muslim, Ma Chik Ting, one detail that complicates any simple picture of the congregation. Declared a monument in 2022, it now draws most worshippers on Fridays, many Pakistani office workers riding the Mid-Levels Escalator up from Central — more or less the route the lascars walked, a hundred and seventy years on.

stops.title: Jamia Mosque
stops.caption: Stand at the green archway and stairs to Jamia Mosque, off Shelley Street above Central.
stops.transcriptText:
It's eighteen fifty, and a group of sailors has just walked uphill from the harbour, through a city that has existed for less than a decade, to find somewhere to pray.

The mosque sits off Shelley Street, set back behind a green archway and a flight of stairs, with the green-and-white facade of the prayer hall visible from the gate, a single minaret above it carrying a crescent and star at its tip — the only minaret on any mosque on Hong Kong Island. The building you're looking at was constructed in nineteen fifteen, funded entirely by a single Bombay merchant, Haji Mohamed Essack Elias, to replace an earlier structure on the same ground. The site has held a mosque since eighteen fifty, when the British colonial government leased this land to the Muslim community — among the earliest formal recognitions of any non-Christian faith in the colony.

The congregation the first mosque served was a mix of South Asian merchants, Muslim soldiers in the British military, and lascars — sailors who worked on British ships and docked in Hong Kong between voyages. Lascar is a word that came into English through Persian and Portuguese, and these men made this walk, or something close to it, up through the old streets of Central to get to this building. The mosque was known for a long time as the Lascar Temple. Worth noticing: the pulpit inside — the mimber — was donated in nineteen fifteen by a local Chinese Muslim, Mr Ma Chik Ting, one small detail that complicates any simple picture of what the congregation looked like.

The building was declared a monument in twenty twenty-two, and today sees most of its congregation on Fridays — primarily Pakistani office workers who take the Mid-Levels Escalator up from Central, more or less the same route the lascars walked, a hundred and seventy years on.

The mosque is open to respectful visitors outside prayer times — remove shoes at the entrance, and the caretaker on site is generally happy to answer questions. The interior, with its pointed multifoil arches and Kufic calligraphy, is rather fine.

tours.title: Lau Kee Aberdeen Boat Noodle
tours.shortDescription: Hong Kong's first licensed floating food kitchen, moored at Aberdeen Pier 6 since 1981 — boat noodles in a salted-flounder broth from the city's indigenous Tanka community.
tours.longDescription:
Lau Kee is moored at Aberdeen Pier Six, on the promenade beside the typhoon shelter where sampans and fishing vessels still move through the harbour. Lam Lau has sold boat noodles here since 1981 under the name Lau Kee — the first licensed floating food kitchen in Hong Kong. The bowl is flat rice noodles or vermicelli in a broth of dried salted flounder, slow-cooked daily, topped with roasted duck, poached chicken, fish balls, and char siu in whatever combination you choose; the roast duck thigh noodle is the signature.

Lam is Tanka — one of Hong Kong's indigenous communities, for much of their history forbidden from living on land and building close-knit lives entirely on the water. The food came straight from that life: the morning catch, the broth from the daily haul, the meats roasted on board. The Tanka have largely moved ashore now, and much of the harbour life that defined Aberdeen for a century has gone with them. This boat, moored in one place rather than roving as it once did, is one of the last. From the promenade, kaito sampans cross to Ap Lei Chau in about five minutes.

stops.title: Lau Kee Aberdeen Boat Noodle
stops.caption: Find the Lau Kee boat moored at Aberdeen Pier 6, beside the typhoon shelter.
stops.transcriptText:
There's a curious thing about how the duck in your bowl was cooked — until recently, it was roasted on board this boat, inside a repurposed water drum. The owner, when asked about the fire risk, gestured at the surrounding water and laughed.

The boat is moored at Aberdeen Pier Six, on the promenade beside the typhoon shelter, where sampans and fishing vessels still move through the harbour in front of you. Lam Lau has been selling boat noodles here since nineteen eighty-one, under the name Lau Kee — the first licensed floating food kitchen in Hong Kong. The bowl is flat rice noodles or vermicelli in a broth made from dried salted flounder, slow-cooked daily, topped with whatever combination of roasted duck, poached chicken, fish balls, and char siu you choose. Prices run from around thirty to sixty-five dollars depending on toppings and whether you eat on board or take it away.

Lam is Tanka — one of Hong Kong's original indigenous communities, who for much of their history were forbidden from living on land and built close-knit lives entirely on the water. The food came directly from that life: the fish caught each morning, the broth made from the daily haul, the roasted meats prepared on board with whatever could fit in the kitchen. I always think the bowl tastes differently when you know that, though I concede it may be the flounder broth doing most of the work.

The Tanka community has largely moved ashore now, and quite a lot of the harbour life that defined Aberdeen for a century has gone with them. This boat, moored in one place rather than roving as it used to, is one of the last.

Order the roast duck thigh noodle. It's the signature, and the reason people come.

From the promenade here, small kaito sampan ferries cross to Ap Lei Chau — the journey takes about five minutes and costs next to nothing. The Hung Shing Temple on the Ap Lei Chau waterfront is worth the short trip: a working Taoist temple dedicated to the god of the sea, used by the fishing community for generations, and quiet enough that you can actually hear the water from inside it.

tours.title: Mister Softee
tours.shortDescription: One of just fourteen licensed Mister Softee ice-cream vans left in Hong Kong — red, white and blue, The Blue Danube on a loop from the roof, a soft-serve cone for twelve dollars.
tours.longDescription:
Known locally as the Regal Ice Cream from the Chinese name on its side, the Mister Softee van is unmistakable: red, white and blue livery and The Blue Danube playing on a loop from the roof speakers. The government stopped issuing hawking licences in 1978, just eight years after the first truck arrived, and has granted none since — so the fleet has been capped at fourteen ever since and can only shrink. The most reliable spots are outside the Star Ferry Pier in Tsim Sha Tsui, Golden Bauhinia Square in Wan Chai, and weekends near IKEA at Sha Tin; on weekdays they park near schools and stations.

Ho King-yuen brought the first truck over on Chinese New Year's Eve, 1970, inspired by Mister Softee vans he'd seen in London. The opening price was five cents a cone, and within days the trucks were selling sixteen hundred cones an hour. The name changed to Mobile Softee in 2010 when the US brand rights were pulled back, but the jingle, the van, and the flavour stayed. Ho died in January 2025, aged ninety-eight; the vans keep going on the same fourteen licences, playing The Blue Danube, indefinitely.

stops.title: Mister Softee
stops.caption: Find the red-white-and-blue Mister Softee ice-cream van.
stops.transcriptText:
This is the Mister Softee van — known locally as the Regal Ice Cream, which is the name on the side in Chinese. It costs about twelve dollars.

You're standing in front of one of fourteen vans currently operating in Hong Kong, identifiable by the red, white, and blue livery and The Blue Danube playing on a loop from the roof speakers. The government stopped issuing new hawking licences in nineteen seventy-eight — just eight years after the first truck arrived — and no new licences have been granted since, so the fleet has been capped at fourteen ever since and can only shrink, never grow. If you're hunting one down: the most reliable spots are outside the Star Ferry Pier in Tsim Sha Tsui, Golden Bauhinia Square in Wan Chai, and on weekends near IKEA at Sha Tin MTR. On weekdays they tend to park near schools and train stations.

Ho King-yuen brought the first truck to Hong Kong on Chinese New Year's Eve, nineteen seventy — inspired, quite specifically, by seeing Mister Softee vans during a trip to London and deciding Hong Kong should have the same. The opening price was five cents a cone. Within days the trucks were selling sixteen hundred cones an hour.

Well, the name changed to Mobile Softee in twenty ten after the US rights to the Mister Softee brand were pulled back, but the jingle stayed, the van stayed, the flavour stayed. Ho died in January twenty twenty-five, aged ninety-eight. He had long since transferred the business and moved to Australia, but the vans kept going without him, as they presumably will continue to do, playing The Blue Danube on a loop, on the same fourteen licences, indefinitely.

Order the cone. It's twelve dollars.

tours.title: Upper Lascar Row Antique Street Market
tours.shortDescription: Cat Street — a narrow Sheung Wan alley of jade, old coins, propaganda badges and bronze Buddhas, named for the lascar sailors who settled here and once notorious as a thieves' market.
tours.longDescription:
Upper Lascar Row runs roughly two hundred metres below Hollywood Road in Sheung Wan, connected to it by Ladder Street and the stone steps opposite Man Mo Temple — a narrow outdoor alley of stalls selling jade jewellery, old coins, porcelain figurines, Cultural Revolution propaganda badges, vintage photographs, mahjong sets, bronze Buddhas, and things with no obvious category. Unlike the curated galleries on Hollywood Road above, nothing here is priced to reflect certainty: the gap between a five-dollar curiosity and a five-thousand-dollar find is yours to judge.

It is officially named for the lascar sailors — Indian maritime workers on British ships — who settled here from the 1840s; the Cantonese name, Mo Lo Sheung Gaai, uses a term for Indians that grew controversial, and a 2010 petition to rename the street was rejected to preserve the historical name. The market spent decades as a genuine thieves' market, where stolen goods were sold openly among sailors, refugees and dealers — one of the more genuinely multicultural streets in colonial Hong Kong. That's largely gone; what remains is the outdoor-market instinct of looking through things that have passed through many hands, not knowing what you'll find.

stops.title: Upper Lascar Row Antique Street Market
stops.caption: Walk the stalls of Upper Lascar Row (Cat Street), below Hollywood Road in Sheung Wan.
stops.transcriptText:
Pick something up from the nearest stall. The chances are reasonable that it is either genuinely old or a convincing replica of something genuinely old, and you may not immediately know which.

The street runs roughly two hundred metres below Hollywood Road, connected to it by Ladder Street and the stone steps opposite Man Mo Temple — a narrow outdoor alley of stalls and shopfronts selling jade jewellery, old coins, porcelain figurines, Cultural Revolution propaganda badges, vintage photographs, mahjong sets, bronze Buddhas, and quantities of things with no obvious category. The range is the point. Unlike the galleries on Hollywood Road above, nothing here is curated, nothing is priced to reflect certainty, and the difference between a five-dollar curiosity and a five-thousand-dollar find is something you are expected to judge for yourself.

The street is officially called Upper Lascar Row — named for the lascar sailors, Indian maritime workers on British ships, who settled in this part of Sheung Wan from the eighteen forties. The Cantonese name, Mo Lo Sheung Gaai, uses a term for Indians that became controversial over time: in twenty ten, a formal petition to rename the street was rejected by the government, on the grounds that the historical name should be preserved.

Well, the market that exists here now is considerably tidier than what it replaced — this street spent several decades as a genuine thieves' market, where stolen goods were sold openly, and where the mix of sailors, refugees, and dealers made it one of the more genuinely multicultural streets in colonial Hong Kong. That's largely gone. What remains is the outdoor market instinct — the pleasure of looking through things that have passed through many hands, not knowing what you'll find — which is, if you ask me, entirely worth preserving.

Ladder Street, directly at the western end, leads back up to Hollywood Road and down to Queen's Road below — worth the climb in both directions if you want to see how the three levels of this neighbourhood sit on top of each other.

tours.title: Yue Po Chai Antique Co
tours.shortDescription: One of Hollywood Road's oldest antique shops, by Man Mo Temple — ten thousand square feet running from Neolithic ceramics to Qing porcelain, the residue of a trade driven by displacement.
tours.longDescription:
Yue Po Chai sits at the Man Mo Temple end of Hollywood Road, one of the oldest antique shops on the street, with a facade you know before you've read the sign. The showroom runs to about ten thousand square feet and carries pieces from Neolithic burial ceramics to Qing-dynasty porcelain, stone carvings, jade, furniture, and scroll paintings — no single period, and no apology for it.

Hollywood Road was built in 1844, one of the first roads in the new colony, and for most of its history the antiques trade here was driven by displacement. A first wave came after 1949, when refugees fleeing Communist China sold family heirlooms; a second came during the Cultural Revolution, when artefacts were smuggled across the border ahead of the Red Guards who were burning what they caught. Most of what passed through was bought by Western dealers and museums and left Hong Kong entirely. The pieces still here — the ones that didn't sell fast enough, or found local buyers — are the residue of that trade: an accidental archive built not by curators but by consecutive waves of people who had to leave.

stops.title: Yue Po Chai Antique Co
stops.caption: Stand outside Yue Po Chai Antique Co at the Man Mo Temple end of Hollywood Road.
stops.transcriptText:
During the Cultural Revolution, Red Guards across China were destroying antiques as symbols of the old order. Some of what they didn't reach ended up here.

Yue Po Chai sits at the Man Mo Temple end of Hollywood Road — one of the oldest antique shops on the street, with a facade that lets you know it before you've read the sign. The showroom inside runs to around ten thousand square feet and carries pieces ranging from Neolithic burial ceramics to Qing Dynasty porcelain, stone carvings, jade, furniture, and scroll paintings. The stock has no single period and makes no apologies for it.

Hollywood Road was built in eighteen forty-four, one of the first roads in the new British colony, and for most of its history the antiques trade here was driven by displacement. The first wave came after nineteen forty-nine, when refugees fleeing Communist China brought family heirlooms to sell. The second came during the Cultural Revolution of the nineteen sixties and seventies, when artefacts were smuggled across the border by people hoping to outrun the Red Guards — who were, at the same time, burning what they caught. Dealers on Hollywood Road described the period as extraordinary. Objects that had been in Chinese families for centuries appeared, priced to move.

I find it worth knowing where things come from, standing in a shop like this one. Most of what passed through Hollywood Road was eventually bought by Western dealers, European museums, or private collectors overseas, and left Hong Kong entirely. The pieces still here — the ones that didn't sell fast enough, or that found local buyers, or that accumulated in the back rooms of shops like this one — are the residue of that trade. An accidental archive, built not by curators but by consecutive waves of people who had to leave.

The steps down to Cat Street — Upper Lascar Row — are directly opposite Man Mo Temple. The street below runs parallel and sells at considerably more affordable prices than Hollywood Road, which is rather the point of going down them.

tours.title: St Pancras & King's Cross
tours.shortDescription: George Gilbert Scott's Gothic fairy-tale hotel and Barlow's record-breaking train shed — the whole Victorian century in one building, saved from demolition by a poet.
tours.longDescription: St Pancras pairs two Victorian masterpieces: the Midland Grand Hotel, George Gilbert Scott's exuberant Gothic frontage of spires and pinnacles, and behind it William Barlow's single-span iron-and-glass train shed, once the widest enclosed space on earth. By the 1960s it had fallen so far out of fashion that it was slated for demolition, like the Euston Arch before it. The poet John Betjeman led the fight that saved it — and a bronze statue of him now gazes up at the roof he rescued.

stops.title: St Pancras & King's Cross
stops.caption: Stand on the Euston Road, on the south side, and look up at the front of St Pancras station — the red-brick palace with the spires and the clock tower, the one that looks far too grand to be somewhere you catch a train.
stops.transcriptText:
Stand on the Euston Road, on the south side, and look up at the front of St Pancras station — the red-brick palace with the spires and the clock tower, the one that looks far too grand to be somewhere you catch a train.

That's the point. It was meant to.

When it went up in the 1860s, the railway companies were the richest, most aggressive businesses in Britain, and the Midland Railway wanted a London terminus that said so out loud. So they hired George Gilbert Scott, the most fashionable Gothic architect of the day, and told him to build a hotel that would shame every rival into silence. The result is the Midland Grand — all those pinnacles and pointed arches and that improbable roofline, Gothic detail borrowed from Venetian palaces and Flemish cloth halls and stacked up in front of a railway shed.

Look past the hotel for a moment, at the great curved roof of the shed behind it. When William Barlow threw that single span of iron and glass over the platforms, it was the widest enclosed space anywhere in the world. The fairy-tale front and the engineering miracle behind it — that's the whole Victorian century standing in one place.

Now here's the part that should stop you. By the 1960s all of this had fallen so far out of fashion that there were serious plans to pull it down. Euston station, just up the road, had its famous arch demolished in 1962, and St Pancras looked like it was next in the queue.

What saved it was, more or less, one man — the poet John Betjeman, who loved Victorian buildings when almost nobody else did, and who led the fight to keep this one standing. There's a statue of him inside now, up on the platform concourse, cap in hand, gazing up at the roof he helped rescue.

[beat]

So when you go in — and you should — head up to the platform level and find him. A slightly rumpled man in bronze, looking up. It's easy to walk straight past him on the way to a train, but he's the reason there's anything here worth walking past.

tours.title: Coal Drops Yard
tours.shortDescription: Victorian coal warehouses behind King's Cross — once among London's dirtiest spots — reborn as a Heatherwick-designed quarter that kept the brick, the bones, and the names.
tours.longDescription: Granary Square and the Coal Drops were the grimy engine room of Victorian London, where trains tipped coal, grain and ice straight onto canal barges. Derelict for most of the twentieth century — and home to a lawless 1990s nightclub scene — the two long brick sheds were reborn when Thomas Heatherwick stretched their swooping roofs towards each other until they kiss. The result keeps the industrial bones and the old names intact rather than pretending at history.

stops.title: Coal Drops Yard
stops.caption: You're standing in the middle of what used to be one of the dirtiest places in London.
stops.transcriptText:
You're standing in the middle of what used to be one of the dirtiest places in London.

It doesn't look it now. Granary Square, where you're standing, is all clean stone and open water, and if you've timed it right there are a thousand little fountains rising and falling out of the ground in front of you, lit up after dark, children running through them in summer. But pull the gloss away and the names tell you what this was. Coal Drops. Granary. Goods Yard. This whole quarter behind King's Cross station was where the railways unloaded the things that kept Victorian London alive — coal, grain, ice, fish — straight off the trains and onto the canal barges waiting beside it.

Turn and look at the long brick buildings to one side, the ones with the great swooping roofs that seem to lean towards each other across a narrow gap. Those are the Coal Drops — built in the 1850s so that trains could run along the top and tip their coal down through the floors into wagons and barges below. Industrial machinery, essentially, the size of a street. For most of the twentieth century, once the coal stopped, they sat derelict, and for a while in the 1980s and 90s the only thing happening in here was a legendary, slightly lawless nightclub scene.

Now look at what's been done to them. The architect Thomas Heatherwick took those two Victorian roofs and stretched them towards each other until they kiss in the middle, making a single covered space out of two old sheds, and filled the lot with shops and restaurants.

[beat]

That's the move worth noticing here. Nobody knocked the coal yards down and started again. They kept the brick, kept the bones, kept the names, and let the new thing grow out of the old one. So when you walk through, you're not in a shopping centre that's pretending to have history — you're in the actual machinery of Victorian London, still standing, still useful, just asked to do something gentler with its old age. Walk the canal towpath behind it if you've time. The barges are gone, but the water's exactly where it was.

tours.title: Regent's Park
tours.shortDescription: John Nash's grand stage set: palatial white terraces that are really rows of ordinary houses in disguise, ringing a park composed like a landscape painting.
tours.longDescription: Designed by John Nash for the Prince Regent in the early 1800s, Regent's Park is a piece of theatre. The palatial stuccoed terraces around it aren't great houses at all but rows of ordinary homes dressed up to read, from across the lawns, as one enormous palace. Nash spent his money on the silhouette — the view from the middle distance — and composed the park's lake, trees and sightlines as carefully as a painter arranges a canvas.

stops.title: Regent's Park
stops.caption: Find a spot near the edge of the park where you can see one of the long white terraces — a whole row of grand houses behind a single unbroken facade, columns and pediments running the length of the street, painted the colour of wedding cake.
stops.transcriptText:
Find a spot near the edge of the park where you can see one of the long white terraces — a whole row of grand houses behind a single unbroken facade, columns and pediments running the length of the street, painted the colour of wedding cake.
