Walk up close to one, and look carefully. It's a fake. Or rather, it's theatre.

These terraces were the work of John Nash, the favourite architect of the Prince Regent — the future George the Fourth — in the early 1800s. The Prince wanted a grand new park on the edge of town, ringed with palaces fit for the aristocracy, with a route running straight down to his own residence. Nash gave him exactly that, on paper. What actually got built is cleverer and slightly more dishonest. Behind those palatial fronts aren't single great houses at all — they're rows of ordinary terraced homes, dressed up to look, from across the park, like one enormous palace.

That's the trick worth seeing. Nash wasn't really designing buildings here so much as designing a view. He understood that almost nobody would ever stand where you're standing and inspect the brickwork — they'd see it from a carriage, from across the lawns, at a distance, where the columns and the symmetry read as sheer magnificence. So that's where he put all the money: on the surface, the silhouette, the bit you see from far away.

[beat]

Now turn and look at the park itself, because it's the other half of the same idea. It feels wild and rolling and natural, the lake curving off out of sight, trees placed as if they simply grew there. None of it is accidental. Every tree, every bend in the water, every gap that lets a terrace appear suddenly through the leaves was composed, the way a painter composes a landscape.

So this whole place is a stage set two hundred years old — the grandest backdrop in London, built to be admired from exactly the middle distance you're probably standing in. Step back. Let the terrace blur slightly into one great white sweep. That's the view Nash actually built. Up close was never the point.

tours.title: Primrose Hill
tours.shortDescription: The closest big view of central London you can reach on foot — and a skyline legally protected so nothing may ever block the dome of St Paul's.
tours.longDescription: From the top of Primrose Hill the whole of central London lies below — St Paul's, the City's glass towers, the Shard across the river. The view is more than scenic: a protected sightline drawn through the air legally forbids any building that would block the dome of St Paul's from this spot. It has been a poets' hill for two centuries, too — William Blake claimed a vision of the spiritual sun here, and his words are carved into the stone near the summit.

stops.title: Primrose Hill
stops.caption: You've climbed the hill, so catch your breath, turn around, and look south across the city.
stops.transcriptText:
You've climbed the hill, so catch your breath, turn around, and look south across the city.

That view is the reason you came up here, and it's also the reason this hill looks the way it does — open, grassy, undeveloped, when it sits on some of the most valuable land in London. Somebody decided, long ago, that what you're looking at mattered more than what could be built here.

Take the skyline in for a second. St Paul's dome, off to your right, low and pale. The cluster of glass towers in the City beyond it. The Shard further round to the left, standing on its own south of the river. The whole of central London laid out below you like a model, the parks stitching the green between the grey.

Here's the thing that holds it all in place: this is a protected view. There's an actual law — a sightline drawn through the air — that says nothing may be built in the gap between this hill and St Paul's Cathedral that would block the dome. Developers have to prove their tower won't intrude on it. So the view in front of you isn't just nice. It's legally defended, an agreement that some things are worth keeping clear even when there's money to be made by filling them in.

[beat]

There's a softer history here too. This hill has been a poets' hill for two centuries — Blake claimed he had a vision of the spiritual sun up here, and writers and walkers have come for the same reason you have: it's the closest big view to the centre that you can reach on foot, with no ticket and no queue. On the railings near the top you'll find William Blake's words carved into the stone, about the spiritual fourfold vision he said he saw from this spot.

So stay a while. This is one of the few places in London where the whole city agrees to hold still and let you look at it — and where, by law, it has to keep doing so. Come back at dusk if you can. The towers light up one by one, and the dome holds its ground in the middle, exactly where the law says it must.

tours.title: London Zoo
tours.shortDescription: The world's oldest scientific zoo, where the word 'zoo' was coined — and home to Lubetkin's empty, beautiful 1934 Penguin Pool, a building the penguins themselves voted against.
tours.longDescription: Opened in 1828 as a research collection for the Zoological Society, London Zoo is the oldest scientific zoo in the world — and the place where the word 'zoo' was coined. Its most famous structure is Berthold Lubetkin's 1934 Penguin Pool, two wafer-thin spiralling concrete ramps that became a manifesto for modern architecture. The penguins were eventually moved out — the hard concrete was unkind to their feet — leaving a gorgeous listed monument standing empty, a perfect emblem of two centuries of changing minds about animals.

stops.title: London Zoo
stops.caption: Before you go in, a quiet fact: this is the oldest scientific zoo in the world.
stops.transcriptText:
Before you go in, a quiet fact: this is the oldest scientific zoo in the world.

Not the oldest place where animals were kept for show — kings had been doing that for centuries, and London itself had a royal menagerie in the Tower for six hundred years. This was something new. When it opened in 1828, the London Zoo wasn't built for the public at all. It was a research collection for the Zoological Society, a place for scientists to study living animals rather than dead specimens, and the word "zoo" — short for zoological gardens — was actually coined right here.

But the reason to slow down at this particular zoo isn't only the animals. It's the architecture, and one building above all. If you can find it — or look it up before you go — seek out the old Penguin Pool.

It was built in 1934 by Berthold Lubetkin, a Russian émigré architect, and it is one of the most famous small buildings in Britain. Two long curving concrete ramps, thin as ribbons, spiralling around each other over a shallow blue pool, with nothing holding them up in the middle. At the time, nobody believed concrete could be made to do that. It became a manifesto — proof that this strange new modern architecture could be elegant, even playful, not just stark.

[beat]

Here's the bittersweet part. The penguins were eventually moved out, because for all its beauty the pool turned out to be a slightly miserable home for them — the hard concrete was hard on their feet, and the design served the idea more than the birds. So it stands empty now, a listed monument with nothing living in it, beautiful and beside the point.

And that's the thing worth carrying around the rest of the zoo. This whole place is two centuries of humans changing their minds about animals — from specimens to spectacle to the careful, enclosure-redesigning conservation thinking you'll see today. The empty penguin pool is the perfect emblem of it: a gorgeous building, built with total confidence, that the animals themselves quietly voted against. Keep an eye, as you walk round, for how much newer everything feels. That's not an accident. That's the apology.

tours.title: Camden Market
tours.shortDescription: A Georgian canal interchange that lost its job, sat empty, and filled up with stalls, music and youth culture — best understood from the lock that gave it its name.
tours.longDescription: Everyone comes to Camden for the market, but the canal is the reason it's all here. Camden Lock sits on the Regent's Canal, cut in the 1810s to carry coal and cargo across north London, with warehouses built to store the goods. When the canal trade died, the empty buildings filled with craftspeople, market stalls and a music scene that became an engine of British youth culture. Look for the old horse hospital and the cobbled ramps — relics of when the whole operation ran on horses.

stops.title: Camden Market
stops.caption: Everyone comes to Camden for the market — the stalls, the food, the leather and the band t-shirts, the crowds three deep on a Saturday. But stand for a moment by the water, at the lock itself, because the water is the reason all the rest of it is here.
stops.transcriptText:
Everyone comes to Camden for the market — the stalls, the food, the leather and the band t-shirts, the crowds three deep on a Saturday. But stand for a moment by the water, at the lock itself, because the water is the reason all the rest of it is here.

This is Camden Lock, and the clue's in the name. That gentle drop in the canal in front of you, where the water steps down between two sets of wooden gates — that's the lock that gave this whole place its identity. The canal is the Regent's Canal, cut in the 1810s and 20s to carry cargo across north London between the docks and the Midlands. Camden was a working interchange: goods came off the canal boats here, onto carts and later onto the railway, and the warehouses you can see around you were built to store all of it.

Look at those buildings now, because they tell the story of what happened next. By the second half of the twentieth century the canal trade was finished, the warehouses were empty, and into that emptiness — cheap rent, big spaces, nobody minding — came craftspeople, then market stalls, then a music scene that turned Camden into one of the engines of British youth culture for decades. Punk, then everything after it, played the pubs and venues a few minutes' walk from where you're standing.

[beat]

There's one lovely detail to hunt for. Tucked into the market there's a place called the Horse Hospital — or stables, depending which corner you find — a reminder that this was a transport hub long before lorries, when the whole operation ran on horses hauling boats and carts, and the animals needed somewhere to be stabled and treated. Look for the ramps and the cobbles built for hooves, not feet.

So here's Camden in one frame: a piece of Georgian shipping infrastructure that lost its job, sat empty long enough for something stranger and louder to move in, and turned into a marketplace for a kind of London that doesn't fit anywhere tidier. Buy something you didn't plan to. Then come back to the lock and watch a narrowboat work through it — slow, patient, exactly as it did two hundred years ago.

tours.title: Highgate Cemetery
tours.shortDescription: London's most fashionable Victorian garden cemetery — theatrical tombs, the pharaonic Egyptian Avenue, and Karl Marx — half-reclaimed by ivy and roots.
tours.longDescription: Opened in 1839 as a grand answer to London's catastrophically overcrowded churchyards, Highgate became the most fashionable of the city's garden cemeteries — a place to walk, to be seen, and to admire monuments. Its tombs are pure Victorian theatre: broken columns, draped urns, sleeping angels, and the pharaonic Egyptian Avenue, now half-swallowed by ivy. Karl Marx rests on the eastern side, still drawing visitors from around the world, but the quieter graves carry the same message — that grief deserved grandeur.

stops.title: Highgate Cemetery
stops.caption: Go gently here. This is a cemetery, still in use, and people come to visit graves that matter to them. But it's also one of the most extraordinary places in London, and it's worth understanding why it looks the way it does.
stops.transcriptText:
Go gently here. This is a cemetery, still in use, and people come to visit graves that matter to them. But it's also one of the most extraordinary places in London, and it's worth understanding why it looks the way it does.

Highgate opened in 1839, and to understand it you have to know what came before it. By the 1830s London's old parish churchyards were catastrophically full — bodies stacked beyond any decency, a genuine public health crisis. The answer was a ring of huge new private cemeteries on the city's edge, landscaped like parks, and Highgate became the most fashionable of them all. People didn't just bury their dead here. They came to walk, to be seen, to admire the monuments. Death, to the Victorians, was something you dressed up and displayed.

You can read that everywhere you look. The tombs are theatrical — broken columns, draped urns, sleeping stone angels, a whole grammar of mourning carved in marble. The most famous stretch is the Egyptian Avenue, a row of tombs built like the entrance to a pharaoh's chamber, leading to a sunken circle of vaults ringed by a great cedar tree. It's overgrown now, half-reclaimed by ivy and roots, and that wildness is part of why people love it — a Victorian fantasy of death slowly being taken back by the living world.

[beat]

There are famous people resting here — the one most visitors look for is Karl Marx, whose great bearded bust sits on the eastern side, still drawing visitors from across the world more than a century after he died a few streets away in poverty. But try not to let it become a celebrity hunt. The quieter graves matter just as much.

What this place really preserves is an attitude — a century that believed grief deserved grandeur, that the dead should be remembered out loud, in stone, under trees. Walk slowly. Read a few of the ordinary names, not just the famous one. The whole point of a garden cemetery was that remembering could be beautiful rather than only sad, and standing here among the ivy and the leaning angels, you can feel them having got that right.

tours.title: Abbey Road
tours.shortDescription: The most photographed zebra crossing on earth, outside the working studio where the Beatles recorded — a piece of road so loved it's been given protected status.
tours.longDescription: On 8 August 1969 four Beatles walked across this zebra crossing while a photographer took six frames from a stepladder; one became the cover of Abbey Road. Behind it stands Abbey Road Studios, one of the oldest purpose-built recording studios in the world, opened in 1931 and still working today. The crossing has a live 24-hour webcam pointed at it and has been given listed status — a very British thing to do to some paint. The studio wall, endlessly painted over with fans' messages, is part of the ritual.

stops.title: Abbey Road
stops.caption: You're looking at a zebra crossing. Black and white stripes, a Belisha beacon blinking on each side, cars waiting, slightly impatient, while people stop in the middle to take photographs. It is, almost certainly, the most photographed zebra crossing on earth.
stops.transcriptText:
You're looking at a zebra crossing. Black and white stripes, a Belisha beacon blinking on each side, cars waiting, slightly impatient, while people stop in the middle to take photographs. It is, almost certainly, the most photographed zebra crossing on earth.

You already know why. On the eighth of August, 1969, four members of the Beatles walked across this exact crossing while a photographer stood on a stepladder in the road and took six frames in ten minutes. One of them became the cover of the album they named after the street: Abbey Road. No title on the sleeve, no band name — just four men walking. It didn't need words.

Now turn and look at the building behind it, the white Georgian townhouse set back from the road. That's Abbey Road Studios, and it's the real reason any of this happened. It's one of the oldest purpose-built recording studios in the world, opened in 1931, and the Beatles recorded most of their music inside it. The crossing is famous because the studio was there, and the studio is still working — bands and film composers record here today, which is why you can't go in.

[beat]

Here's the small modern absurdity worth knowing. There is a webcam, pointed at this crossing, live, twenty-four hours a day. At any moment, people anywhere in the world can be watching this exact spot — watching you, if you step out onto it now. The crossing itself is so beloved that it's been given protected status, a piece of ordinary road infrastructure listed for its cultural importance, which is a very strange and very British thing to do to some paint.

So go ahead — wait for a proper gap, be kind to the drivers, and walk across. You'll be repeating something a few million people have done in imitation of a ten-minute photo shoot from over fifty years ago. It's daft, and it's joyful, and that's rather the point. The wall by the studio gate is covered in fans' messages, painted over and rewritten endlessly. Add nothing, just read a few. People came from everywhere to stand exactly where you are.

tours.title: Lord's Cricket Ground
tours.shortDescription: Cricket's cathedral: the Long Room walk, a playing field that famously isn't flat, and a joke trophy smaller than your hand that never leaves the building.
tours.longDescription: Lord's is the closest cricket has to a cathedral, home of the Marylebone Cricket Club that wrote and guarded the laws of the game worldwide for two centuries. Players walk out to bat through the wood-panelled Long Room, past members and portraits — by reputation the most nerve-shredding short walk in sport — onto a pitch that famously slopes two and a half metres from one side to the other. The Ashes urn, a tiny terracotta joke from 1882, lives in the museum here and almost never leaves; the teams play for a replica.

stops.title: Lord's Cricket Ground
stops.caption: If you know cricket, you don't need me to tell you what this place is. If you don't, here's the only thing you need: this is the closest the sport has to a cathedral, and people say "Lord's" the way they might say "the Vatican."
stops.transcriptText:
If you know cricket, you don't need me to tell you what this place is. If you don't, here's the only thing you need: this is the closest the sport has to a cathedral, and people say "Lord's" the way they might say "the Vatican."

It's called the home of cricket, and the name on the gate is just a man's — Thomas Lord, who set up the ground in the early 1800s. But the institution behind it, the Marylebone Cricket Club, spent two centuries as the body that wrote and guarded the laws of the game for the entire world from this one address in north London.

There are a few things to look for. Inside the pavilion — the grand old building with the long windows — is the Long Room, a wood-panelled hall the players walk through on their way out to bat. Imagine it: you're about to play in front of thirty thousand people, and the path takes you through a quiet room full of members in ties and portraits of the dead greats watching you go. It is, by reputation, the most nerve-shredding short walk in sport.

Then there's the slope. The whole playing field tilts — it drops by around two and a half metres from one side to the other, enough that the ball behaves differently depending on which end you bowl from. The most famous ground in cricket is, gloriously, not flat.

[beat]

And then the strangest treasure of all. Cricket's biggest contest between England and Australia is called the Ashes — and the trophy they supposedly play for is a tiny terracotta urn, just a few inches tall, said to contain the burnt remains of a cricket bail, created as a joke in a newspaper after England lost in 1882. That urn lives here, in the little museum, and here's the twist: it's so fragile and so precious that it almost never leaves. The teams play for it, but the actual urn stays at Lord's. They win a replica.

So that's this place — laws, a sloping pitch, a terrifying walk through a quiet room, and a national obsession that comes down to a joke urn smaller than your hand. Even if you never watch a match, that's a very English kind of greatness.

tours.title: Kenwood House
tours.shortDescription: A free Robert Adam mansion on Hampstead Heath holding a Rembrandt self-portrait and a Vermeer — given to the nation on the condition no one is ever charged to see them.
tours.longDescription: At the top of Hampstead Heath, Kenwood House is Robert Adam's cool, pale remodelling of the 1760s, crowned by one of the most beautiful rooms in England — a long library with a curved, softly painted ceiling. In the 1920s Edward Guinness left the house and its art to the nation as the Iveagh Bequest, on condition it stay open and free forever. Among the collection hangs a late Rembrandt self-portrait and Vermeer's Guitar Player — a world-class masterpiece you can walk up to on a weekday afternoon for nothing.

stops.title: Kenwood House
stops.caption: You've walked up through Hampstead Heath, and here at the top, white against the green, is Kenwood House. Before you go in, know the best thing about it: it's free, and inside hangs one of the most valuable paintings in Britain, which you can walk up to without paying a penny.
stops.transcriptText:
You've walked up through Hampstead Heath, and here at the top, white against the green, is Kenwood House. Before you go in, know the best thing about it: it's free, and inside hangs one of the most valuable paintings in Britain, which you can walk up to without paying a penny.

But take the house in first. This is the work of Robert Adam, the most fashionable architect of the late 1700s, who remodelled an older house into this cool, pale, perfectly balanced thing in the 1760s. Adam's whole genius was the interior — light, delicate, classical, every ceiling and doorframe part of one coordinated scheme. The room to find is the library, a long space with a curved ceiling painted in soft pinks and blues, gilded just enough, considered down to the last inch. It's one of the most beautiful rooms in England, and it's a room, not a roped-off shrine. You can stand in it.

[beat]

Now the painting. In the 1920s the house and its art were given to the nation by Edward Guinness, of the brewing family — the Iveagh Bequest — on the condition that it stay open and free forever. And among what he left is a small, quiet picture by Rembrandt: a self-portrait of the artist as an old man, and, hanging nearby, a Vermeer — the Guitar Player — one of only a tiny handful of Vermeers anywhere in the world, in a country house on the edge of a public heath, guarded by almost nobody, costing nothing to see.

Sit with that for a second. There are people who fly across the world and queue for hours to see a single Vermeer behind glass in a crowded gallery. Here's one you can walk up to on a Tuesday afternoon, on the way back from a walk, for free, because a man a century ago decided that was how it should be.

So that's Kenwood — a perfect Adam interior and a world-class collection, given away on the condition that nobody would ever be charged to enjoy it. When you've looked your fill, go back out and sit on the lawn facing the city. The view down the slope to the ponds is the same one the painting's owners had. They just had to share it eventually.

tours.title: Kensington Palace
tours.shortDescription: The least palatial royal palace — three centuries of the family's domestic 'aunt heap' — that became a national shrine after Diana lived and was mourned here.
tours.longDescription: From the gardens Kensington looks less like a palace than a very grand brick house, and that modesty is the point: for three hundred years it has been where the royal family housed its wider relatives, divided into apartments — George V called it 'the aunt heap.' Diana, Princess of Wales lived here, and after her death in 1997 the railings vanished under a sea of flowers in one of Britain's greatest outpourings of public grief. The Sunken Garden, replanted as a memorial with a statue of Diana, carries that story forward.

stops.title: Kensington Palace
stops.caption: From the gardens, Kensington Palace doesn't look much like a palace. No great dome, no vast facade — just a long, handsome brick house, the kind a very rich family might live in, set among lawns and avenues of trees. That modesty is the first clue to what this place actually is.
stops.transcriptText:
From the gardens, Kensington Palace doesn't look much like a palace. No great dome, no vast facade — just a long, handsome brick house, the kind a very rich family might live in, set among lawns and avenues of trees. That modesty is the first clue to what this place actually is.

For three hundred years, Kensington has been where the royal family puts the relatives. The grand state business happens at Buckingham Palace, a couple of miles east. Kensington is the home — divided up into apartments, occupied by princes and princesses and minor royals, generation after generation. King George the Fifth is supposed to have called it "the aunt heap," and the nickname stuck, because that's exactly what it was: a comfortable brick warren full of the wider family.

But one resident made it a place of pilgrimage. Diana, Princess of Wales, lived here for years, and after her death in 1997 the gates and railings in front of you vanished entirely under a sea of flowers — bouquets stacked metres deep, left by hundreds of thousands of strangers. If you stand at the main gates and picture them buried in cellophane and handwritten cards, you're picturing one of the most extraordinary outpourings of public grief Britain has ever seen.

[beat]

Walk round to find the Sunken Garden, because it's the loveliest corner and it carries that story forward. It's a formal garden, planted in tiers around a still pool, blazing with colour in summer — and it was redesigned and replanted as a memorial, with a statue of Diana now standing in it. A quiet, deliberately beautiful place made out of remembering one person.

So here's Kensington, in a sentence: the least palatial of the royal palaces, the family's domestic back-stage for three centuries, that became, almost by accident, a national shrine. Most of it is still lived in, behind those windows, by people you'd recognise from the news. Have a wander through the parts that are open, then sit by the round pond in the gardens. The whole point of this place was always that it felt like somewhere people actually lived. It still does.

tours.title: Portobello Road
tours.shortDescription: Two streets in one: the world's most famous antiques market on Saturdays, a working neighbourhood market the rest of the week — and the cradle of Notting Hill Carnival.
tours.longDescription: Portobello Road is really two streets sharing a name. On Saturdays it's the world's most famous antiques market, running nearly a mile; on weekdays it reverts to an ordinary fruit-and-veg street market for the people who live there. Notting Hill, the neighbourhood around it, became a main home for the Windrush generation arriving from the Caribbean after the war — and from that community grew the Notting Hill Carnival, now one of the largest street festivals on the planet. Beneath the pastel houses and photo-ops is a tougher story about who built the place.

stops.title: Portobello Road
stops.caption: The first thing to know about Portobello Road is that it's really two streets wearing the same name, and which one you get depends entirely on the day you came.
stops.transcriptText:
The first thing to know about Portobello Road is that it's really two streets wearing the same name, and which one you get depends entirely on the day you came.

Come on a Saturday, and this is the most famous antiques market in the world — stalls and shopfronts running for the better part of a mile, packed shoulder to shoulder with silver, old jewellery, prints, cameras, coins, the lovely and the dubious side by side, dealers who've been here for decades next to people who set up that morning. Come on a Tuesday, and the antiques largely vanish, and you find a working neighbourhood street market instead — fruit and veg, fish, household bits, the ordinary shopping of the people who actually live here. Same paving stones, two completely different streets.

That doubleness is the real story of Notting Hill, the area you're walking through, because this place has changed identity more than once. After the Second World War, this was a poor, run-down quarter, and it became one of the main places where families arriving from the Caribbean settled — part of the Windrush generation who came to help rebuild Britain and met, too often, hostility and terrible housing.

[beat]

Out of that community came something the whole city now claims as its own: the Notting Hill Carnival. Every August, these streets fill with sound systems and steel bands and costume and a couple of million people, in what's grown into one of the biggest street festivals on the planet — a celebration rooted directly in Caribbean culture and in this neighbourhood's history of being told it didn't belong.

So as you walk the market, hold both pictures at once. The pastel houses are achingly desirable now, some of the priciest in London, and the street's been polished by films and tourism into something charming. But underneath the antiques and the photo-op facades is a tougher, richer story about who built this place and what they made here. Buy something small if the spirit moves you — haggle gently, it's expected. And if you can possibly come back for Carnival, do. That's when the street tells you the truth about itself.

tours.title: Leighton House
tours.shortDescription: Behind a stern red-brick villa, the most establishment painter in Victorian Britain built a private pleasure-palace — its domed Arab Hall tiled floor to ceiling and murmuring with a fountain.
tours.longDescription: From the street Leighton House is a sober Victorian villa, and the gap between that plain exterior and the extraordinary interior is the whole experience. Frederic Leighton — president of the Royal Academy and the ultimate art-world insider — spent thirty years turning his home into a single growing work of art. Its centrepiece is the Arab Hall, a domed chamber with a black marble fountain pool and walls covered in genuine 16th- and 17th-century tiles collected across Syria and Turkey: one of the most sensuous interiors in London, built by the most proper man in British art.

stops.title: Leighton House
stops.caption: From the street, Leighton House looks like a fairly stern red-brick Victorian villa — handsome, but not the kind of thing you'd cross London for. That's deliberate. The whole point of this house is what it hides, and the gap between the plain outside and the extraordinary inside is the entire experience.
stops.transcriptText:
From the street, Leighton House looks like a fairly stern red-brick Victorian villa — handsome, but not the kind of thing you'd cross London for. That's deliberate. The whole point of this house is what it hides, and the gap between the plain outside and the extraordinary inside is the entire experience.

This was the home of Frederic Leighton, the most successful, most establishment painter in Victorian Britain — president of the Royal Academy, eventually made a lord, the absolute summit of the respectable art world. And he built himself, behind that sober brick, a private pleasure-palace devoted entirely to beauty. A bachelor with no family to house, he spent thirty years turning his home into a single, growing work of art.

The room you've come to see is the Arab Hall. Step into it and the temperature of the place changes completely. It's a domed chamber modelled on a palace hall in the Islamic world, with a fountain murmuring in a black marble pool at its centre, and every wall covered floor to ceiling in antique tiles — genuine sixteenth and seventeenth-century tiles that Leighton collected on his travels through Syria, Turkey and beyond, brilliant blues and greens, gilded ceiling glinting above. It is one of the most sensuous interiors in London, built by the most proper man in British art.

[beat]

That contradiction is the thing to hold on to. The Victorians are caricatured as buttoned-up and repressed, and Leighton was their ultimate insider — and yet here, in his own home, he built a space of pure escape, an Arabian fantasy soaked in colour and water and silence, the opposite of everything that public respectability was supposed to be. The house was part of a whole movement of artists who believed your surroundings should be works of art in themselves: the cult of the House Beautiful.

So take your time in here. Notice how the dim, jewel-box rooms make you lower your voice. This was never built to impress committees or sell paintings — it was built for one man to live inside his own idea of beauty. Standing in the Arab Hall, with the fountain going and the tiles glowing, you're standing in the most private wish of a very public man.

tours.title: Battersea Power Station
tours.shortDescription: Four white chimneys on a brick cathedral of electricity by Giles Gilbert Scott — derelict for thirty years, immortalised by a runaway Pink Floyd pig, now reborn.
tours.longDescription: Battersea Power Station is one of the largest brick buildings in Europe, designed by Giles Gilbert Scott — the same hand behind the red telephone box — to make a power station London could bear to look at. It generated electricity into the 1980s, then stood roofless and rotting for over thirty years, one of the world's most famous derelict buildings. Pink Floyd floated an inflatable pig between its chimneys in 1976; it broke loose and grounded planes at Heathrow. Now restored into shops, flats and offices with a chimney-top glass lift, the four chimneys still stand exactly where they always did.

stops.title: Battersea Power Station
stops.caption: Four white chimneys, one at each corner, rising off a vast brick box the colour of dried blood. You've almost certainly seen this building before you ever came to London — on a record sleeve, in a film, in the background of the city's imagination. This is Battersea Power Station, and for most of its life it didn't make a single beautiful thing. It made electricity.
stops.transcriptText:
Four white chimneys, one at each corner, rising off a vast brick box the colour of dried blood. You've almost certainly seen this building before you ever came to London — on a record sleeve, in a film, in the background of the city's imagination. This is Battersea Power Station, and for most of its life it didn't make a single beautiful thing. It made electricity.

Look at the brick. There's an absurd amount of it — this is one of the largest brick buildings in Europe — and it's worked like architecture, not just stacked like a factory. That's the hand of Giles Gilbert Scott, who was brought in to make a power station that London could bear to look at. And here's a detail worth carrying: Scott is the same man who designed the red telephone box you've been photographing all over the city, and his cousin's firm later shaped Bankside Power Station downriver — the building that's now Tate Modern. Once you know that, you start seeing the family resemblance: the same instinct that an industrial brick monster could be made monumental, even noble.

It generated power into the 1980s, then fell silent — and then came the strange decades. For more than thirty years it stood empty, roofless for much of that time, slowly rotting, one of the most famous derelict buildings in the world. Plans came and went; nothing stuck.

[beat]

The pop-culture moment that fixed it forever: in 1976, the band Pink Floyd floated an enormous inflatable pig between those chimneys for an album cover. The pig broke loose, drifted off into the flight path of Heathrow airport, and grounded planes. The building has been impossible to take entirely seriously ever since, which is part of its charm.

Now look at what's happened. After all those derelict years it's finally been brought back — the shell restored, the interior turned into shops and flats and offices, one chimney rebuilt as a glass lift you can ride to the top. Some people love it, some mourn the romantic ruin it used to be. Either way, the four chimneys still stand exactly where they always did. Walk up to the brick and put your hand on it. Whatever's inside now, that wall was built to outlast all of us.

tours.title: Kew Gardens Palm House
tours.shortDescription: A rainforest under glass and curved iron, built like an upturned ship's hull — one of architecture's most important buildings, and the botanical engine room of an empire.
tours.longDescription: The Palm House at Kew is a swelling bubble of glass and curved iron, and one of the most important buildings in architectural history. When it rose in the 1840s nobody had built anything like it: the ironworker Richard Turner solved the problem with shipyard techniques, curving wrought-iron ribs the way a ship's hull is built — essentially an upturned, glazed hull filled with rainforest. It was a machine for keeping the tropics alive in a cold country, the heart of Kew's role as the botanical engine room of the British Empire — a complicated history worth sitting with among the palms.

stops.title: Kew Gardens Palm House
stops.caption: In front of you is a building made almost entirely of glass and curved iron, swelling up in the middle like a great green bubble caught mid-rise. This is the Palm House at Kew, and it's one of the most important buildings in the history of architecture — which is a strange thing to say about a greenhouse.
stops.transcriptText:
In front of you is a building made almost entirely of glass and curved iron, swelling up in the middle like a great green bubble caught mid-rise. This is the Palm House at Kew, and it's one of the most important buildings in the history of architecture — which is a strange thing to say about a greenhouse.

Here's why. When it went up in the 1840s, nobody had built anything like it. Architects worked in stone; you could not make a building this size out of glass, because glass and iron behaved in ways the old rules couldn't describe. So the people who solved it weren't really architects at all. The structural genius behind it, Richard Turner, was an ironworker — and the form they landed on came straight from the shipyards.

That's the detail to carry inside with you. Look at the great curved ribs holding the glass up. They're built the way the hull of a ship is built — the same wrought-iron techniques, the same understanding of how to make iron curve and hold under stress. They essentially took the upturned hull of a boat, inverted it, glazed it, and filled it with the rainforest. When you stand inside, under that soaring ribbed ceiling, you're standing inside a ship turned upside down and pointed at the sky.

[beat]

And then the purpose. This wasn't built for beauty, though it's beautiful. Kew was the botanical engine room of the British Empire — plants from every conquered and colonised corner of the world brought here to be studied, propagated, and sent on to wherever they could be made profitable. The Palm House was a machine for keeping the tropics alive in a cold country, so that the world's plants could be gathered, controlled, and understood in one place. That history is complicated, and worth sitting with as you walk among the palms.

So go in. Feel the heat and the wet hit you at the door — they keep it tropical year-round. Climb the spiral stair to the high walkway if it's open, up among the tops of the palms, and look at those iron ribs from close. A Victorian shipwright's solution to an impossible problem, still standing, still doing its job, nearly two centuries on.

tours.title: After the Fire: Wren's City

stops.title: The Great Fire — Introduction
stops.caption: It's the second of September, 1666, and London is about to burn.
stops.transcriptText:
It's the second of September, 1666, and London is about to burn.

It starts just before two in the morning, in a baker's shop on a narrow lane near the river — a single oven, banked too hot, in a city built almost entirely of timber and pitch, packed so tightly that the upper floors lean towards each other across the streets. There's been a long dry summer and a steady east wind. By the time anyone takes it seriously, it's already past stopping.

For four days the Great Fire of London eats the city alive. When it finally burns out, around eighty per cent of the old City is gone — some thirteen thousand houses, the medieval cathedral, eighty-seven parish churches, the London of the Middle Ages erased in a long weekend.

And here's the turn this whole walk is about. Out of that catastrophe came the greatest opportunity any architect has ever been handed. The man who took it was Christopher Wren, and over the next fifty years he rebuilt the skyline of London in stone — fifty-one churches and one enormous cathedral, a whole city's worth of domes and steeples, out of a single mind.

This walk traces the fire from beginning to end: from the lane where the spark caught, to the column that commemorates it, through two of Wren's churches, and finishing at the masterpiece he spent his life building. And unlike a lot of London, this one really is a walk — it's all packed into the Square Mile, barely a mile end to end, the city rebuilt so tightly on its own ashes that you can cross three and a half centuries on foot in twenty minutes.

Let's go back to where it started. Find Pudding Lane.

stops.title: Pudding Lane
stops.caption: You're standing on Pudding Lane, looking for a plaque, because there's almost nothing else to see here — and that, in a way, is the point.
stops.transcriptText:
You're standing on Pudding Lane, looking for a plaque, because there's almost nothing else to see here — and that, in a way, is the point.

This narrow, unremarkable City street is where the Great Fire began. In the early hours of that September morning in 1666, fire broke out in the bakehouse of Thomas Farriner, baker to the King, somewhere near the bottom of this lane. A small fire, in a small shop, of the kind that must have started a hundred times before. What was different this time was the wind, and the drought, and the timber, and the fact that the Lord Mayor, hauled out of bed to look at it, famously waved it away — a woman could put it out, he scoffed — and went back to sleep.

By the time the city understood what it had, the fire had jumped from the bakery to a riverside inn, into warehouses packed with oil and tallow and brandy along the Thames, and turned into something that made its own weather.

As for the name — don't picture desserts. Pudding Lane was named for the "puddings," the offal and guts that butchers carted down here on their way to the river. It was a working lane in a working city, and it has the dubious honour of being the most famous street in London for something that lasted a few hours before everything around it turned to ash.

Stand here a second and try to feel the scale of what started on this exact spot: one oven, one ordinary night, and four days later a medieval capital gone. Then look up the lane, because the city built a giant exclamation mark to mark it, and it's right around the corner. Walk to the Monument.

stops.title: The Monument
stops.caption: Tip your head back, because this thing is two hundred and two feet of pure Baroque showing-off, and there's a number hidden inside it.
stops.transcriptText:
Tip your head back, because this thing is two hundred and two feet of pure Baroque showing-off, and there's a number hidden inside it.

This is the Monument — just "the Monument," it doesn't need more — built by Christopher Wren and his brilliant friend Robert Hooke and finished in 1677 to commemorate the Great Fire and the rebuilding of the city. It's a single column of Portland stone, topped with a gilded urn of golden flames, and at two hundred and two feet it's the tallest free-standing stone column in the world.

Now the number. It stands two hundred and two feet from the spot in Pudding Lane where the fire began. If you could lay the column down on its side, the flaming urn at the top would come to rest exactly on the bakery. The height isn't arbitrary — it's a measurement, a line drawn in stone between the monument and the wound.

And it was never only a monument. Hooke was one of the great scientists of the age, and he designed the hollow core and the three hundred and eleven spiral steps to double as a giant scientific instrument — a fixed telescope pointing at the sky, a place to run experiments on air pressure and gravity, with a laboratory tucked in the base. A memorial that was also a piece of laboratory equipment.

There's a darker footnote. For over a century, an inscription on the base blamed the fire on a Catholic plot — a falsehood, born of the religious fear of the time, finally chiselled away in the 1830s. Worth remembering that monuments record the feelings of the people who build them as much as the events themselves. Climb it if you've the legs — the view is the reward. Then we head into Wren's churches. Make for St Stephen Walbrook, over towards Bank.

stops.title: St Stephen Walbrook
stops.caption: From the outside, St Stephen Walbrook is easy to walk straight past — a modest Wren church wedged in behind the Mansion House.
stops.transcriptText:
From the outside, St Stephen Walbrook is easy to walk straight past — a modest Wren church wedged in behind the Mansion House. Go in. It's one of the most beautiful rooms in London, and it's a secret hiding in plain sight.

Look up. Floating above you is a dome — a great coffered dome, lifted on eight slender arches, flooding the whole space with even, calm light. It seems impossible that a building this plain outside could hold something this serene within. And here's why it matters: Wren built this dome in the 1670s, while he was still wrestling with the design for St Paul's, and many people believe he used St Stephen Walbrook as a try-out. A dome before the dome. The great one you'll finish the walk at was, in part, rehearsed right here, at small scale, in a side street.

So this little church is a working drawing you can stand inside — Wren thinking out loud in stone, testing whether he could float a dome on slim supports and fill a room with light, before he bet a cathedral on the answer.

Two more layers worth holding. Under the dome sits a great rounded altar carved by the modern sculptor Henry Moore — smooth pale stone that startled people when it arrived, and now looks as though it was always meant to be there. And it was in this parish, in the 1950s, that a vicar named Chad Varah founded the Samaritans, the helpline for people in despair — quiet, serious, life-saving work that began behind these walls. A small church doing large things. When you've taken in the light, head out towards Cheapside. We're going to listen for some famous bells at St Mary-le-Bow.

stops.title: St Mary-le-Bow
stops.caption: Listen.
stops.transcriptText:
Listen. If the bells happen to be ringing while you're here, stop and pay attention, because these are the most famous bells in England — and they decide who counts as a Londoner.

This is St Mary-le-Bow, on Cheapside, one of Wren's churches, finished around 1680 with a steeple so elegant that other architects spent the next century copying it. But it isn't the steeple people know — it's the sound that comes out of it. By old tradition, a true Cockney, a real native Londoner, is someone born within the sound of Bow Bells. Not within a boundary, not within a postcode — within earshot of this one particular peal. The bells draw an invisible map of who belongs, and they've done it for centuries.

The bells carry a story, too. In the old legend it was Bow Bells that called the young Dick Whittington back to London just as he was trudging away, broke and beaten — ringing out, the tale says, "turn again, Whittington" — back to a city where he'd go on to be Lord Mayor three times over. A discouraged young man, turned around by a sound.

There's a wartime coda. The bells you might hear aren't quite the originals — those were shattered when the church was bombed in 1941, then recast and rehung in the 1960s. But during the war, a recording of these bells was broadcast by the BBC into occupied Europe: the steady sound of London still standing, sent across the Channel to people in the dark. The name, by the way, comes from the bow-shaped stone arches in the crypt beneath you — older than Wren, older than the Fire. Go down and find them if it's open. Then we walk west for the finale. Up Cheapside to St Paul's.

stops.title: St Paul's Cathedral
stops.caption: And here it is — the building the whole walk has been climbing towards.
stops.transcriptText:
And here it is — the building the whole walk has been climbing towards. Stand back far enough to take in the dome, because that dome is the point of everything we've seen today.

When the Great Fire destroyed the old medieval St Paul's, it handed Christopher Wren the commission of a lifetime: build a new cathedral, from nothing, for the rebuilt city. He spent the next thirty-five years on it — fighting committees, redrawing plans, watching it rise stone by stone — and finished in 1710, an old man who lived to see his masterpiece complete, which almost no cathedral-builder in history ever managed.

The dome is one of the largest in the world, and it's a magic trick. What you see from outside is far too tall and too perfect to work as a ceiling inside — so Wren built three domes, one within another: an inner dome for the view from below, a hidden brick cone in the middle holding everything up, and the great lead outer dome you see on the skyline. Three domes pretending to be one. Every dome you saw rehearsed back at St Stephen Walbrook was leading here.

Wren is buried downstairs, in the crypt, beneath a plain slab — no grand tomb. Instead, carved above him, a line in Latin that translates as: reader, if you seek his monument, look around you. He meant the cathedral. But after today you can read it wider. Look around the whole City — the steeples, the Monument, the churches, the skyline itself — and nearly all of it traces back to the four days of fire we started with, and the one man who answered them. That's the end of the walk. You're standing in his monument. So is everyone else, for half a mile in every direction.

tours.longDescription: In September 1666 the Great Fire destroyed some four-fifths of the old City of London. Out of the ruins came the greatest opportunity ever handed to an architect: over fifty years Christopher Wren rebuilt the skyline with fifty-one churches and one vast cathedral. This compact Square-Mile walk traces the fire from its start in Pudding Lane to the column that marks it, through two of Wren's churches, and ends at his masterpiece, St Paul's.
tours.shortDescription: A half-mile walk through the City: from the lane where the Great Fire of 1666 began to St Paul's, tracing how Christopher Wren rebuilt London's skyline in stone.

tours.title: Albertopolis

stops.title: Albertopolis — Introduction
stops.caption: Most great cities grow by accident.
stops.transcriptText:
Most great cities grow by accident. This corner of London was planned — on purpose, by one man, as a kind of argument.

You're in South Kensington, standing on Exhibition Road, with the Victoria and Albert Museum on the corner ahead of you. Over the next ten minutes or so we're going to walk a tight little cluster: three enormous museums in a row, then a concert hall and a memorial just to the north. It's the most compact walk in this set — you could throw a stone across most of it.

Here's the idea that built all of it. In 1851, Britain threw the Great Exhibition — a vast show of art, industry and invention, housed in a glass palace in Hyde Park, just up the road. Six million people came. It made a fortune. And the man behind it, Prince Albert, Queen Victoria's husband, had a plan for the money: buy the land down here, and build a permanent district devoted to art, science and learning. For everyone, not just the wealthy. The papers nicknamed it Albertopolis.

Then, in 1861, Albert died. He was forty-two. He never saw most of this finished.

So that's the thread to hold as we walk. Nearly everything here was built after the man whose idea it was had gone — completed in his memory, by a grieving country and a grieving wife. It's a whole neighbourhood built as remembrance, dressed up as progress. And it ends, fittingly, at a memorial to him.

Start with the building on the corner. Walk to the main entrance of the V&A, on Cromwell Road, and we'll begin there.

stops.title: Victoria and Albert Museum
stops.caption: The Victoria and Albert Museum — the V&A.
stops.transcriptText:
The Victoria and Albert Museum — the V&A. You're at the Cromwell Road entrance, under the great carved doorway, with the tall fluted tower rising above you.

This is the world's leading museum of art and design — and that last word is the key to it. Not a museum of masterpieces behind rope, but a museum of making: furniture, fashion, ceramics, ironwork, jewellery, wallpaper, whole rooms lifted out of old buildings and rebuilt inside. Around two and a quarter million objects, spanning five thousand years. You can find a Raphael cartoon and a Vivienne Westwood dress under the same roof, and the V&A would tell you they belong together.

Now, the gift. The V&A was the first museum in the world to have a proper restaurant — a place to sit, eat, and stay a while. That sounds small. It wasn't. The whole idea here was that ordinary people, after a day's work, might come in, look at beautiful things, learn how they were made, and go home wanting to make better ones themselves. Feed them, and they'll stay longer. The refreshment rooms are still there, still serving — three rooms by three different designers, and worth a look even if you're not hungry.

That mission is the V&A's real exhibit. Most museums of this age were built to show off — empire's spoils, a nation's wealth. This one was built to teach: to raise the standard of British design by letting anyone study the best of it, free. It's a school disguised as a palace.

The foundation stone for the front you're looking at was laid by Queen Victoria herself, in 1899 — her last great public appearance. An old woman, laying the cornerstone of the museum that carries her dead husband's name alongside her own.

When you've had your look, come back out and turn right. The next building along Cromwell Road is impossible to miss — it looks like a cathedral. That's where we're headed.

stops.title: Natural History Museum
stops.caption: This is the Natural History Museum, and the first thing to do is just stand back and look at it, because it doesn't look like a museum.
stops.transcriptText:
This is the Natural History Museum, and the first thing to do is just stand back and look at it, because it doesn't look like a museum. It looks like a cathedral.

That was the point. The architect, Alfred Waterhouse, designed it in the 1870s as a temple — but a temple to nature instead of God. Two great towers, a vast rounded doorway, the whole front faced in pale terracotta tile. And here's the gift, once you know to look for it: the building is covered in animals. Carved and moulded into the stone, everywhere. Look at the west side, to your left, and the creatures are living ones — monkeys, birds, things still walking the earth. Look at the east side, to your right, and they're extinct — pterodactyls, ancient beasts. Living on one wing, dead on the other. The building sorts the natural world the way the collection inside does.

And what a collection. Around eighty million specimens — plants, animals, fossils, rocks, meteorites — making this one of the most important records of life on earth anywhere.

Step inside, even just into the entrance, and you're in Hintze Hall, the great central space under the arched roof. For decades a diplodocus skeleton stood here, nicknamed Dippy, and a couple of generations of British children grew up greeting him on the way in. A few years ago he was retired, and now a real blue whale skeleton hangs from the ceiling above you, diving through the air — the largest animal that has ever lived, where the dinosaur used to be.

Here's the quiet thing about this building. It was finished in the same decades Britain was arguing hard about evolution — about whether nature needed God at all. And the answer the museum gave wasn't a sermon. It was this: build nature a cathedral, and give it the grandeur we used to reserve for the divine.

Behind this building, on Exhibition Road, is the third of the three museums. Head round to the Science Museum.

stops.title: Science Museum
stops.caption: The Science Museum.
stops.transcriptText:
The Science Museum. After a museum of art and a museum of nature, this is the third panel — a museum of what people have made and figured out. The making of the modern world, it calls one of its great halls, and that's about right.

Two things in here are worth planning your visit around, because they're not copies. They're the actual objects.

The first is Stephenson's Rocket. Not a model, not a replica: the real locomotive that, in 1829, more or less invented the railway age — small, battered, sitting quietly in a case. Almost everything about how the modern world moves traces back to that one machine.

The second is up in the space gallery: the Apollo ten command module. The genuine capsule, scorched from re-entry, that carried three astronauts around the Moon in 1969 — the dress rehearsal, two months before the first landing. It went to the Moon and back, and now it sits in South Kensington, and you can stand a few feet from it.

That's the gift of this whole place: the real things, not the story of them.

And it completes Albert's idea. Walk it back in your head — art and design at the V&A, the natural world next door, human invention here. Three museums, side by side, making one argument: that beauty, nature, and knowledge belong together, and belong to everybody. That was the whole point of the money from 1851.

Now we leave the row of museums and walk north. Head up Exhibition Road, towards the park. In a few minutes a huge round building comes into view on your left — terracotta and glass, like a great drum. That's the Royal Albert Hall, and that's our next stop.

stops.title: Royal Albert Hall
stops.caption: The Royal Albert Hall.
stops.transcriptText:
The Royal Albert Hall. Stand back far enough to take in the shape — a vast oval drum, red brick and terracotta, with a frieze running right around the top like a ribbon. It opened in 1871, and it's been London's great room ever since: concerts, boxing, tennis, Churchill, the Beatles, and every summer the Proms — weeks of nightly concerts that end in the famous, flag-waving last night.

But the gift here is about a flaw. For its first century, this beautiful room had a terrible secret — the echo. The domed ceiling bounced sound back so badly that people used to joke the Albert Hall was the only place a British composer could be sure of hearing his work twice. They tried everything. Finally, in 1969, they hung a constellation of large fibreglass discs from the ceiling — saucer-shaped, floating up there in the dome. The mushrooms, everyone calls them. They scatter the sound before it can echo, and they fixed in an afternoon what had defeated the engineers for a hundred years. Look up next time you're inside; they're still hanging there.

Now the thread. This is the first building in the whole district to actually carry Albert's name — Royal Albert Hall. Victoria laid the stone and came to the opening, but she was so overcome with grief, still, years after his death, that she couldn't bring herself to speak. Someone else declared it open on her behalf.

Which brings us to the last stop, and it's just across the road. Turn and face north, away from the hall, and look across Kensington Gore into the park. You'll see something tall and gold glinting under a pointed canopy. Walk towards it. That's the Albert Memorial — and it's where this whole walk has been heading.

stops.title: Albert Memorial
stops.caption: Here it is.
stops.transcriptText:
Here it is. Stand at the foot of the steps and look up.

This is the Albert Memorial, finished in 1872, and it is unashamedly a shrine. A tall, pointed, gilded canopy — like a spire lifted off a cathedral and set down over a single seated figure. And that figure, gold from head to foot, glinting when the sun catches him, is Albert. The man whose idea you've spent the last ten minutes walking through.

Look at what he's holding. In his hands, resting on his knee, is a book — and it's not a Bible, or a sceptre, or anything you'd expect a prince to be shown with. It's the catalogue of the Great Exhibition of 1851. The very thing that started all of this. He's sitting here, for ever, holding the programme of his own great show, facing the hall that bears his name, with the three museums his money built just behind it. The whole district arranged around him like an offering.

The man who designed this was George Gilbert Scott — and if the name rings a bell, it should. He also built the great red Gothic front of St Pancras station across town. And his grandson, Giles Gilbert Scott, went on to design Battersea Power Station, the Tate Modern building, and the red telephone box. One family, three generations, scattered right across this city.

Around the base, at the four corners, sit groups representing the four continents — a very Victorian, very imperial idea of the world gathering at Albert's feet. Worth seeing, and worth seeing clearly for what it is.

But mostly, just stand here a moment. Everything you've walked past today — the art, the bones, the rockets, the music — was the living version of an idea. This is the man who had it, who didn't live to see it built, sitting in gold at the centre of the city that finished it for him. A whole neighbourhood, raised as a kind of thank-you note. Not many people get one of those.

That's the end of the walk. Take your time here before you go.

tours.longDescription: In 1851 the Great Exhibition made a fortune, and Prince Albert had a plan for it: buy land in South Kensington and build a permanent district of art, science and learning for everyone. The papers nicknamed it Albertopolis. Albert died in 1861 and never saw most of it finished — a whole neighbourhood raised in his memory. This short walk runs the row of three museums — the V&A, Natural History and Science Museums — then up to the Royal Albert Hall and the gilded Albert Memorial where it all points back to him.
tours.shortDescription: The most compact walk in the set: the South Kensington cultural quarter Prince Albert built with the 1851 Great Exhibition's profits — three great museums, a concert hall, and a memorial.
